_meta.description: Person stubs generated from the Orthodox Menaion. One Person per primary saintId and per `also[]` sub-commemoration. Used as a backfill layer by commentary-loader.ts — seed-library and ingested-catalog Persons take precedence on ID collision. Regenerate with `tsx scripts/normalize/normalize-menaion-people.ts`.

people.name: Abba Diocles of the Thebaid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abda and Abdjesus and the forty companions of Beth-Kashkar in Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abdas, Bishop in Persia
people.summary: A fifth-century Persian bishop whose destruction of a fire-temple drew down the wrath of Sapor II and his own martyrdom.

people.name: Abdas, Bishop in Persia, with Deacon Benjamin
people.summary: A fifth-century Persian bishop and his deacon slain together under Yazdegerd I for destroying a fire-temple.

people.name: Abdechalas the Priest and Ananias the Priest of Persia
people.summary: Two priests of fourth-century Persia who shared in the martyrdom of Symeon Bar Sabbae.

people.name: Abdon and Sennen of Rome, Persian princes
people.summary: Two third-century Persian nobles brought captive to Rome who under Decius cared for the imprisoned Christians and were beheaded for it.

people.name: Abercius and Helen, children of the Apostle Alphaeus
people.summary: A first-century brother and sister, the son exposed to bees and the daughter stoned for their confession of Christ.

people.name: Abercius, Bishop of Hierapolis
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Phrygia whose journeys for the faith reached as far as Rome and Edessa; his epitaph, still extant, is one of the earliest Christian inscriptions to confess the Mystery.

people.name: Abgar V, King of Edessa
people.summary: The first-century king of Edessa in Osroene who by tradition sent a letter to the Lord asking for healing and received the cloth of the Mandylion bearing His face.

people.name: Abibus, Bishop of Nekresi
people.summary: A sixth-century Georgian bishop, one of the Thirteen Holy Fathers, slain by the Persians for breaking the fires of the Mazdean temples.

people.name: Abibus of Edessa, the Deacon
people.summary: A fourth-century deacon of Edessa burned at the stake under Licinius, whose body was found uncharred by the fire.

people.name: Abibus (Sabinus) of Hermopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abilius, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: First-century bishop of Alexandria, second pastor of the see after the apostle Mark.

people.name: Abo of Tiflis the Perfumer
people.summary: An eighth-century Arab perfumer of Baghdad who converted in Georgia and was beheaded at Tbilisi for refusing to return to Islam.

people.name: Aborsam and Senoe of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abraham, Bishop of Charres in Mesopotamia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abraham of Mount Latros
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Abramius, Bishop of Arbela in Assyria
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop slain under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

people.name: Abramius, Bishop of Cratea in Bithynia
people.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Cratea who labored long in the cause of Chalcedonian orthodoxy in his see.

people.name: Abramius the Recluse and his niece Mary of Mesopotamia
people.summary: A fourth-century recluse of Mesopotamia who, when his niece Mary fled into the world after a temptation, sought her out and brought her back to repentance.

people.name: Abudimus of Tenedos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Acacius, Bishop of Amida
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Amida in Mesopotamia who melted down the church's sacred vessels to ransom seven thousand Persian captives.

people.name: Acacius II, Bishop of Melitene
people.summary: A fifth-century bishop of Melitene in Armenia who took part in the Council of Ephesus against Nestorius.

people.name: Acacius, Martyr Irenarchus, and the Seven Women-martyrs of Sebaste
people.summary: A bishop and a young man together with seven Christian women slain at Sebaste under Diocletian.

people.name: Acacius of Miletus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Acacius of Mount Latros
people.summary: The young monk whose obedience unto death stands in the Ladder of St. John of Sinai as an icon of the monastic life.

people.name: Acacius of Sinai
people.summary: The young monk whose obedience unto death St. John of the Ladder set forth as the pattern of monastic submission.

people.name: Acacius the Centurion of Byzantium
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Roman officer of Cappadocia beheaded at Byzantium under Diocletian.

people.name: Acacius the Confessor, Bishop of Melitene
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Melitene in Armenia who endured imprisonment and torture under Decius.

people.name: Acacius the Wonderworker of Mount Latros
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Acepsimas the Bishop, Joseph the Presbyter, and Aeithalas the Deacon of Persia
people.summary: A bishop, priest, and deacon of fourth-century Persia under Sapor II, scourged and slowly killed over months in prison for refusing to worship the sun, the trinity of their order matching the Trinity they confessed.

people.name: Acepsimas the Hermit of Cyrrhus
people.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of Syria who lived sixty years walled into a cave near Cyrrhus.

people.name: Achaemonides (Hormisdas) of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian noble who renounced his rank for poverty and exile rather than the worship of fire.

people.name: Achillas, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Early-fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, predecessor of Alexander I, under whom Arius first taught.

people.name: Achilles the Confessor of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Achillius, Bishop of Larissa
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Larissa in Thessaly and a father of the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Acindinus, Antoninus, Victor, Zenon, Zoticus, Theonas, Caesareus, Severian, and Christophoros
people.summary: Nine believers of the great persecution under Diocletian slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Acindynus, Pegasius, Aphthonius, Elpidiphorus, and Anempodistus of Persia
people.summary: Five Christians of the Persian court under Sapor II who endured every torture together for confessing the Lord, drawing thousands to the faith before they were beheaded.

people.name: Acoluthus of Hermopolis in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Adauctus and his daughter Callisthene of Ephesus
people.summary: A nobleman of fourth-century Ephesus and his daughter, who in flight disguised as a man preserved her faith and at last reposed in peace.

people.name: Adelphios, Bishop of Onouphis
people.summary: Fourth-century Egyptian bishop and contemporary of Athanasius the Great, exiled by the Arians for his Nicene faith.

people.name: Adrian and Eubulus of Caesarea
people.summary: Two Christians of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine who at Maximinus Daia's tribunal openly confessed Christ and were given over to the beasts.

people.name: Adrian and Natalia
people.summary: An imperial officer at Nicomedia under Maximian, converted by the witness of the Christians he was assigned to register, and his young wife who supported him through every torment until he was crowned.

people.name: Adrian of Corinth
people.summary: A third-century Christian slain in Persia for the name of Christ.

people.name: Adrian, son of the emperor Probus, of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Adrion, Victor, and Basilla of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aeithalas and Acepsius of Arbela
people.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Arbela in Assyria slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Aeithalas and Ammon of Adrianople
people.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Thrace scourged to death together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Aeithalas the Deacon of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian deacon who chose death rather than worship the sun.

people.name: Aemilian the Roman, with Jacob and Marianos
people.summary: Three companions of third-century Rome martyred together under Valerian.

people.name: Aesia and Susanna of Taormina
people.summary: Two first-century disciples of Pancratius of Taormina in Sicily, crowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Aetherus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Virgin-martyrs Agape, Irene, and Chionia of Aquileia
people.summary: Three sisters of Aquileia under Diocletian who in their parents' country house had hidden away copies of the Scriptures; on their refusal to surrender them, the elder two were burned and the third pierced with arrows.

people.name: Agapetus of the Kiev Caves
people.summary: Eleventh-century unmercenary physician of the Kiev Caves Lavra who at his own deathbed healed Prince Vladimir Monomachus by sending him a small herb.

people.name: Agapitus I, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Rome who at Constantinople deposed the Monophysite patriarch Anthimus before his own repose.

people.name: Agapitus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Synnada in Phrygia
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Synnada who under Licinius confessed Christ before the magistrates and was returned in peace to his see.

people.name: Agapius, Nicephorus, and Charisius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agapius and his seven companions at Caesarea
people.summary: Eight Christians of Caesarea in Palestine under Galerius Maximianus, who at separate audiences before the governor each refused to sacrifice and were given to the beasts together in the arena.

people.name: Agapius of Caesarea Maritima
people.summary: A young Christian of fourth-century Caesarea given up to the beasts under Maximinus Daia.

people.name: Agapius, Seleucus, Mamas, Indus, Domna, Glycerius, and forty companions
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agatha of Sicily
people.summary: A young noblewoman of third-century Catania whom the Roman governor Quintianus pursued for her wealth and beauty; refused, he had her breasts torn off and at last killed her in prison — the apostle Peter appeared to heal her in her cell.

people.name: Agathadorus of Tyana in Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agathangelus, Bishop of Damascus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agathangelus of Damascus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agathius (Acacius) of Byzantium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Agatho, Eutychia, Cassia, and Phillippa of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Four believers of Thessaloniki imprisoned under Diocletian whose witness brought sisters Agape, Irene, and Chionia to their crown.

people.name: Agatho, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Rome whose synodical letter against the monothelites was received by the Sixth Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Agathocleia
people.summary: A maidservant of the third century, scourged and her tongue cut out for refusing her pagan mistress; given over to the fire.

people.name: Agathon of Aleppo
people.summary: An ascetic of the monastery of St. Symeon Stylites near Aleppo in Syria.

people.name: Agathon of Egypt
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt whose sayings stand among the apothegms of the elders.

people.name: Agathonicus of Nicomedia
people.summary: A Greek of Nicomedia who under Maximian led many of his fellow citizens to Christ before being beheaded in Thrace together with several of those he had taught.

people.name: Agathopodes the Deacon and Theodulus the Reader of Thessalonica
people.summary: An aged deacon and a young reader of fourth-century Thessalonica drowned together under Diocletian.

people.name: Agrippina of Rome
people.summary: A young Roman of the third century who under Valerian endured being scourged with iron flails and gave up her soul in confession; her relics were later translated to Sicily.

people.name: Aiglon the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aitherius (Aetherius)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Akakios the Presbyter
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Akyla the Eparch, Emmanuel, Rodian, and Lollian the Elder
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Albian (Olbian), Bishop of Anaea
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Anaea in Asia Minor martyred with his disciples under Maximian.

people.name: Alcibiades
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander, Alphius, Zosimas, and twenty-seven companions
people.summary: Three brothers of fourth-century Pisidia and a great company put to death together under the pagan persecutors.

people.name: Alexander and Antonina of Crodamon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander and the Virgin-martyr Antonina of Byzantium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander, Archbishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Jerusalem and friend of Origen who reposed in chains under Decius.

people.name: Alexander, Barbaras, and Acolythus of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander, Bishop of Adrianople, and his companions
people.summary: A bishop of second-century Adrianople and the believers — Heraclius, Anna, Elizabeth, Theodota, and Glyceria — who suffered with him.

people.name: Alexander, Bishop of Comana the Charcoal-Burner
people.summary: Third-century philosopher of Comana who at Gregory the Wonderworker's bidding was raised from his charcoal kiln to the bishopric of his city.

people.name: Alexander, Bishop of Prusa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander, Bishop of Thessalonica
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Thessalonica and one of the three hundred and eighteen fathers of the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Alexander, Cronion, Julian, and Macarius of Alexandria
people.summary: Four believers of third-century Alexandria slain under Decius with thirteen more of their company.

people.name: Alexander, Dionysius, and Frontonus of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Thessaloniki of the imperial household, slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Alexander, Dionysius, Ingeniani, Panteros, Parthenios, and Saturninus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander, first Bishop of Methymna in Lesbos
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Lesbos and a father of the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Alexander, Founder of the Monastery of the Unsleeping Ones
people.summary: Fifth-century Syrian abbot whose monastery near Constantinople established the unbroken round of psalmody day and night.

people.name: Alexander I, Pope of Rome
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Rome who according to tradition added the institution-words to the canon of the Mass.

people.name: Alexander of Corinth
people.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Corinth beheaded under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

people.name: Alexander of Iconium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander of Marcianopolis in Thrace
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexander of Side
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Side in Pamphylia who was put to death by fire for his confession.

people.name: Alexander of Thessalonica
people.summary: An early-fourth-century believer of Thessalonica beheaded for the name of Christ.

people.name: Alexander, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria under whom Arius the priest was first condemned, and who took part in the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Alexander the Ever-Vigilant
people.summary: Fifth-century founder of the akoimetai monastery in Bithynia, where the round of psalmody was kept day and night.

people.name: Alexandra of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexandra the Empress
people.summary: Wife of Diocletian who at the witness of St. George openly confessed Christ and was beheaded with her attendants.

people.name: Alexis the Man of God
people.summary: A fifth-century noble of Rome who on his wedding day fled to Edessa and lived seventeen years as a beggar at a church door, then returned home unrecognized to spend another seventeen years as the family's anonymous servant under his father's stairs.

people.name: Alexis, Metropolitan of Moscow
people.summary: Fourteenth-century hierarch who healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian Church together.

people.name: Alexius, Bishop of Bithynia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alexius, Metropolitan of Moscow
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alkison, Bishop of Nicopolis
people.summary: A bishop of sixth-century Nicopolis in Epirus, defender of the Council of Chalcedon against the Monophysite party.

people.name: Alonius of Scetis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Alphaeus of the Seventy
people.summary: Father of the martyrs Abercius and Helen, named among the Seventy.

people.name: Alypius the Stylite of Adrianopolis
people.summary: A seventh-century deacon of Adrianopolis who lived fifty-three years upon a pillar in Paphlagonia, gathering both a monastery and a convent around its base.

people.name: Amandus, Amantius, Alexander, Lucius, Alexandria, Avdaldus, Donatus, and Peregrinus of Noviodunum
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ambrose, Deacon of Alexandria
people.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Didymus the Blind in the catechetical school of Alexandria.

people.name: Ambrose, Bishop of Milan
people.summary: The fourth-century hierarch who as the Roman governor of Milan was acclaimed bishop by the people before he was baptized, who taught Augustine the Scriptures, and who refused the Eucharist to Emperor Theodosius until he repented of the slaughter at Thessalonica.

people.name: Ammon (Amun) the Great, Father of the Monks of Nitria
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt, founder of the great monastic settlement of Nitria south of Alexandria.

people.name: Ammon of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Nitria, disciple of Anthony the Great, who founded the great laura on the Mountain of Nitria.

people.name: Ammon of Nitria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ammon the Deacon and the Forty Holy Virgin-martyrs of Heraclea
people.summary: A deacon and forty maidens of Heraclea in Thrace whose teacher he was; all crowned together under Licinius.

people.name: Ammon the Hermit of Egypt
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Ammonius and Alexander of Solia in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Amon (Anoub) the Standard-bearer of Raithu
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Amonathus of Pelusium and Anthus of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Amphianus and Aedesius of Patara in Lycia
people.summary: Two brothers of early-fourth-century Lycia who at Caesarea in Palestine in turn rebuked the prefect at the public sacrifices and were crowned together under Galerius.

people.name: Amphianus the Confessor, Bishop of Cilicia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Amphilochius of Iconium and Gregory of Acragas
people.summary: Amphilochius, the fourth-century cousin of Gregory the Theologian who at the council of Constantinople defended the divinity of the Holy Spirit. Also commemorated: Gregory of Sicily, hierarch of Acragas in the sixth century.

people.name: Amun of Tabennisi
people.summary: A fifth-century disciple of the cenobitic tradition founded by Pachomius in upper Egypt.

people.name: Ananias
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ananias of Damascus
people.summary: One of the Seventy and bishop of Damascus, who at the Lord's word laid hands on the blinded Saul and gave him sight and baptism.

people.name: Ananias of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Persia who at his beheading under Sapor II beheld a ladder leading from earth to heaven.

people.name: Ananias the Presbyter, Peter the Prison-Keeper, and seven soldiers of Phoenicia
people.summary: A third-century priest and the guard converted by his witness, together with seven soldiers of his prison, all martyred together.

people.name: Ananias the Wonderworker of the Euphrates
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anastasia the Deliverer-from-bonds
people.summary: A noble of fourth-century Sirmium who took her wealth to the prisons of Aquileia and Sirmium to ransom and serve the Christians there; after the slaughter she was bound, starved, and finally burned together with three sisters of her household.

people.name: Anastasia the Deliverer-from-bonds
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anastasia the Patrician of Alexandria
people.summary: A sixth-century noblewoman of Constantinople who fled the attentions of Justinian to the Egyptian desert and lived twenty-eight years disguised as a male monk.

people.name: Virgin-martyr Anastasia the Roman
people.summary: A young ascetic raised in a convent of Rome in the third century, taken under Decius and after multiple torments beheaded outside the city, the first virgin-martyr to bear that name.

people.name: Anastasius, Disciple of Maximus the Confessor
people.summary: Seventh-century disciple and amanuensis of Maximus the Confessor, exiled and reposing with him for the cause of the two wills of Christ.

people.name: Anastasius I, Bishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anastasius II, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Seventh-century patriarch of Antioch slain by Jews enraged at the imperial edict for their forced baptism.

people.name: Anastasius II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anastasius Sinaita
people.summary: Seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose Hodegos shaped Christology in the controversies of his age.

people.name: Anastasius the Fuller of Salona
people.summary: A Roman fuller of third-century Salona in Dalmatia who painted a cross on the door of his shop and was beheaded for refusing to deny Christ.

people.name: Anastasius the Persian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anatolius and Protoleon of Nicomedia
people.summary: Two Roman officers converted at the spectacle of George's endurance and beheaded with him.

people.name: Anatolius, Bishop of Laodicea
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Laodicea in Syria of immense philosophic and mathematical learning.

people.name: Anatolius, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century patriarch who at the Fourth Ecumenical Council confirmed the Tome of Leo.

people.name: and Unmercenaries Cosmas and Damian of Arabia
people.summary: Two unmercenary physicians of fourth-century Cilicia martyred together for healing the sick freely in the name of Christ.

people.name: Andreas and Anatolios of Jerusalem
people.summary: Two fifth-century monks of Palestine, disciples of Euthymius the Great.

people.name: Andreas and Probus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Andrew and Anatolius, disciples of Euthymius the Great
people.summary: Two fifth-century monastics of Palestine trained at the lavra of Euthymius.

people.name: Andrew and his spouse
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Andrew, John, Peter, and Antoninus of Syracuse
people.summary: Four believers of Sicily — a father, his son, and two companions — martyred in Africa by the Saracens in the ninth century.

people.name: Andrew of Crete, Archbishop of Crete
people.summary: The seventh-century author of the Great Canon read in Great Lent — a masterpiece of penitential poetry that walks the soul through the whole of Scripture in the voice of personal contrition.

people.name: Andrew of Crete, Archbishop
people.summary: Hymnographer of the Great Canon.

people.name: Andrew Stratelates and 2593 with him
people.summary: A Roman general in Cilicia under Maximian who, after a great victory attributed to Christ, was baptized with the soldiers of his command and, denounced as Christians, were beheaded together in the passes of the Taurus.

people.name: Andrew the First-Called
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Andronicus and Athanasia of Egypt
people.summary: A sixth-century husband and wife of Antioch who upon the death of their children left the world together for the Egyptian desert.

people.name: Andronicus of Atroa
people.summary: A ninth-century monk of Bithynia in the days of the iconoclast emperors.

people.name: Anectus of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Caesarea slain under Diocletian for breaking the city's idols.

people.name: Anianas, second Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: The cobbler whom Mark by miracle converted in Alexandria and afterwards ordained as his successor on the throne of the see.

people.name: Anicetus, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Rome whose meeting with Polycarp of Smyrna over the date of Pascha became proverbial of charity holding apart from agreement.

people.name: Aninas the Wonderworker of Mesopotamia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anna, mother of the Theotokos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anna of Larissa and her son John
people.summary: A ninth-century holy woman of Thessaly remembered for the holy household she kept with her son.

people.name: Anna of Leucadia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anna of Mount Olympus
people.summary: A ninth-century widow of Constantinople who under the name Euphemianus lived as a monk in a community on Mount Olympus in Bithynia.

people.name: Anna of Novgorod
people.summary: Swedish princess Ingegerd, wife of Yaroslav the Wise of Kiev, who at the end of her life took the monastic habit at Novgorod.

people.name: Anna of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Symeon the God-receiver and Anna the Prophetess
people.summary: The day after the Meeting honors the elder to whom it had been revealed that he would not see death until he had seen the Lord's Christ, and the widow of fourscore years and four who served God day and night in the Temple.

people.name: Anterus, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Rome of Greek birth who reposed after a short pontificate during the persecution of Maximin Thrax.

people.name: Anthimus, Bishop of Nicomedia
people.summary: The bishop of Nicomedia at the time of Diocletian's great persecution who, after the destruction of his cathedral, was found in hiding and beheaded together with many of his flock.

people.name: Anthimus the Elder, Paul, Bilonus, Theonas, Heron, and 36 companions of Thessalonica
people.summary: Forty-one Christians of fourth-century Egypt slain together at Thessalonica for the name of Christ.

people.name: Anthion the Ascetic
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthony, Bishop of Sardis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthony, burned alive
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthony of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthony Rawah of Qoraisite
people.summary: Eighth-century Arab convert from Islam crowned at Damascus for refusing to deny Christ.

people.name: Anthony the Confessor, Archbishop of Thessalonica
people.summary: Ninth-century hierarch of Thessalonica who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under the resurgent iconoclasm.

people.name: Anthony the Great, father of monks
people.summary: The third-century Egyptian who, hearing the Gospel words 'sell what you have and give to the poor,' went out to the desert and there became the father of all monastic life — his life, written by Athanasius, shaped Christian asceticism for all time.

people.name: Anthony the Hermit of Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthony the New of Bithynia
people.summary: A ninth-century soldier of Bithynia turned monk at Kios who reposed near Constantinople after long ascetic struggle.

people.name: Anthony the Roman, Wonderworker of Novgorod
people.summary: A twelfth-century Roman ascetic who was miraculously carried on a stone across the seas to the Volkhov River near Novgorod, where he founded a monastery and reposed in peace.

people.name: Anthony the Stylite of Martqopi
people.summary: Sixth-century Georgian father, one of the Thirteen who came up from Antioch into Iberia, who founded a pillar-ministry at Martqopi.

people.name: Anthony, Zevinus, Germanus, Nicephorus, and Maratho of Caesarea
people.summary: Five Christians of Caesarea in Palestine slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Anthousa of Seleucia, with the Martyrs Charesimus and Neophytus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthousa the Confessor, Abbess of Mantineus
people.summary: Eighth-century abbess of ninety nuns in Bithynia who under Constantine V endured scourging and exile for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Anthousa the New
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthusa of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anthusa with her twelve servants
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Antigonus, Loukianos, Terentius, Nicomedes, and Theophanis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Antiochus, Monk of Mar Saba
people.summary: A seventh-century monk of the great Lavra of Mar Saba whose Pandect provided the Christian East with its first systematic florilegium of patristic counsel.

people.name: Antiochus the Physician of Sebaste
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Antipas of Pergamum
people.summary: Disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Pergamum mentioned by name in the Revelation as 'My faithful witness'; he was roasted alive in a brass bull-shaped idol under Domitian.

people.name: Antipater, Bishop of Bostra in Arabia
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Bostra whose writings against Origenism survive in fragments.

people.name: Anton the Stylite of Martqopi
people.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia, who founded monasticism on the holy mountain of Martqopi.

people.name: Antonina of Nicaea
people.summary: An early-fourth-century maiden of Nicaea drowned in a marsh for her confession of Christ.

people.name: Antonina of Nicomedia
people.summary: An early martyr of Nicomedia drowned in the sea for the name of Christ.

people.name: Antoninus, Nicephorus, Germanus, and the maiden Manetha of Caesarea
people.summary: Three young men and a young woman of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine who openly rebuked the prefect for the sacrifices and were given over to the fire.

people.name: Antonius, Marcellus, Silvester, and Sophronius of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Antony II, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Anysia of Thessalonica
people.summary: A young Christian of Thessalonica under Maximian who was stopped on her way to the assembly by a soldier; refusing the imperial sacrifice, she was stabbed through with the soldier's sword in the street.

people.name: Anysios, Bishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Late-fourth-century bishop of Thessaloniki, friend of Ambrose and defender of the Antiochene orthodoxy.

people.name: Anysius, Bishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apelles, Lucius, and Clement of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles: Apelles bishop of Heraclea, Lucius bishop of Laodicea in Syria, and Clement of Sardis.

people.name: Apelles, Luke, and Clement of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Apelles bishop of Heraclea, Luke bishop at Smyrna (not the Evangelist), and Clement of the Philippian church and third bishop of Rome.

people.name: Aphrahates the Persian, Hermit of Antioch
people.summary: The fourth-century Persian wisdom-writer whose Demonstrations are the earliest extant Syriac Christian writings.

people.name: Aphrodisius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aphrodisius, Leontius, Anthony, Valerian, and Macrobius of Scythopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aphrodisius of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aphrodisius the Priest and his thirty companions of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aphrodisius, with Agapius, Eusebios, Charalampos, and Christina of Nicomedia
people.summary: A first-century company of believers of Nicomedia burned together for the name of Christ under Nero.

people.name: Apodosis of the Transfiguration
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apollinaria the Senator's Daughter
people.summary: A fifth-century noblewoman of Rome who slipped away from her family on pilgrimage and lived hidden as a male monk Dorotheos in the Egyptian desert.

people.name: Apollinaria the Virgin of Egypt
people.summary: A fifth-century noblewoman of Rome who slipped away to the Egyptian desert and lived hidden as a male monk.

people.name: Apollinaris, Bishop of Ravenna
people.summary: First-century disciple of the apostle Peter and first bishop of Ravenna.

people.name: Apollo of the Thebaid
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community at the Mountain of Hermopolis, renowned for many miracles.

people.name: Apollo the Bishop
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apollonios of the Thebaid
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community of cells at the Mountain of Hermopolis.

people.name: Apollonius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apollonius, Alexandrion, and Epimachus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apollonius (Apollo) of the Thebaid
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community on the Mountain of Hermopolis.

people.name: Apollonius of Rome
people.summary: Second-century philosopher of Rome whose apology before the senate has been preserved in part.

people.name: Apollonius of Sardis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: Andronicus of the Seventy
people.summary: Greeted by St. Paul in the letter to the Romans as 'my kinsman and fellow prisoner, of note among the apostles.' He labored with St. Junia in spreading the Gospel through Pannonia.

people.name: Aquila of the Seventy
people.summary: Together with his wife Priscilla, host of the apostle Paul at Corinth and Ephesus, instructor of Apollos in the way more perfectly, and remembered by Paul as 'fellow workers in Christ Jesus.'

people.name: Archippus of the Seventy
people.summary: Disciple of Paul named in his letter to Philemon ('our fellow soldier'), bishop of Colossae after Epaphras, who there worked through the earthquakes that twice destroyed the city's church.

people.name: Aristarchus, Pudens, and Trophimus of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul in his letters: Aristarchus the Macedonian of Thessalonica who shared Paul's Roman imprisonment, Pudens the Roman senator who hosted Paul at his house, and Trophimus the Ephesian who fell ill at Miletus.

people.name: Apostles Bartholomew and Barnabas
people.summary: Bartholomew, one of the Twelve, preached as far as India and was flayed in Armenia; Barnabas, of the Seventy, was the companion of Paul and is honored as the founder of the Church of Cyprus, where he was stoned.

people.name: Carpus of the Seventy
people.summary: Companion of St. Paul, who entrusted to him the parchments and cloak in the second letter to Timothy. He served as bishop of Beroea in Thrace and reposed in peace.

people.name: Hermas of the Seventy
people.summary: One of the apostles greeted in the closing chapter of the letter to the Romans. Tradition makes him bishop of Philippopolis in Thrace; sometimes identified with the author of The Shepherd.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: Apostle James, the Brother of the Lord
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: and Evangelist John the Theologian
people.summary: The disciple whom Jesus loved, who alone of the Twelve died in peace. The Church commemorates today the dust that rose annually from his tomb at Ephesus, healing the sick.

people.name: Apostle Jude, brother of the Lord
people.summary: Author of the Epistle of Jude and one of the Twelve, called the kinsman of the Lord. He preached through Mesopotamia and Persia and met his end by martyrdom in Armenia.

people.name: Apostle and Evangelist Luke
people.summary: The physician and companion of Paul who wrote the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles, painted the first icon of the Theotokos, and reposed bishop of Thebes in Boeotia at the age of eighty-four.

people.name: Apostle and Evangelist Mark
people.summary: The companion of Peter who wrote the second Gospel from the apostle's preaching at Rome, and the first bishop of Alexandria — dragged through the streets of the city by a rope until he gave up his soul.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: Apostle Nathaniel (Bartholomew) of the Twelve
people.summary: The disciple whom Philip brought to Christ at the beginning of the public ministry, by tradition Bartholomew of the Twelve.

people.name: and All-Praised Chief Apostles Peter and Paul
people.summary: The two coryphaei of the apostolic college, who having labored together for the Gospel were crowned in martyrdom at Rome on the same day under Nero — Peter crucified head-downward, Paul beheaded.

people.name: and All-Praised Chief Apostles Peter and Paul
people.summary: The two coryphaei of the apostolic college, who having labored together for the Gospel were crowned in martyrdom at Rome on the same day under Nero — Peter crucified head-downward, Paul beheaded.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Exaltation of the Cross
people.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast of the Cross. Also commemorated: Quadratus, who under Hadrian addressed his apology for the faith to the emperor and reposed bishop of Athens.

people.name: Simon the Zealot
people.summary: One of the Twelve, called the Zealot in distinction from Simon Peter. Tradition sends him preaching as far as Britain, where he received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Apostle Thaddaeus of the Seventy
people.summary: One of the Seventy, sometimes identified with Judas the brother of the Lord. He brought the image of the Lord to King Abgar at Edessa, baptized the king with his household, and reposed in peace at Beirut.

people.name: the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
people.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

people.name: Erastus, Olympas, Rodion, Sosipater, Quartus, and Tertius of the Seventy
people.summary: Six of the seventy named by Paul in the Letter to the Romans, who served as bishops in Corinth, Rome, Patras, Iconium, Berytus, and Macedonia, and met their crowns at the hands of pagans.

people.name: Herodion, Agabus, Rufus, Asyncritus, Phlegon, and Hermes
people.summary: Six of the Seventy, all named in Romans 16: Herodion bishop of Patras and kinsman of Paul; Agabus the prophet of the famine; Rufus of Thebes; Asyncritus of Hyrcania; Phlegon of Marathon; Hermes of Philippopolis.

people.name: Stachys, Amplias, Urban, Narcissus, Apelles, and Aristobulus
people.summary: Six of the Seventy named in Romans 16: Stachys became bishop of Byzantium under Andrew; Amplias and Urban at Diospolis and Macedonia; Narcissus and Apelles at Athens and Heraclea; Aristobulus, brother of Barnabas, in Britain.

people.name: Appearance of the Archangel Gabriel on Mount Athos
people.summary: The tenth-century revelation of the hymn 'It Is Truly Meet' to a monk in his cell at Mount Athos by the Archangel Gabriel.

people.name: Appearance of the Kazan Icon of the Theotokos
people.summary: The 1579 discovery of the icon at Kazan by a young girl after the fire that destroyed the city.

people.name: Appion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aprionus, Bishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aquila and Hilary
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aquila and his wife Priscilla of the Seventy
people.summary: Companions of the apostle Paul named in his epistles, makers of tents at Corinth and afterwards teachers at Ephesus of Apollos.

people.name: Aquilina of Byblos
people.summary: A twelve-year-old maiden of Phoenicia who under Diocletian was tortured for confessing Christ and beheaded after enduring with quiet courage what would have broken many.

people.name: Arcadius, Emperor of New Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arcadius of Cyprus and his disciples Julian and Euboulos
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Cyprus and the two monks he trained.

people.name: Arcadius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Arsinoe in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archangel Barachiel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: the Holy Archangel Gabriel
people.summary: The summer feast of the archangel who proclaimed the conception of the Forerunner to Zacharias, the conception of the Lord to the Virgin, and stands ever near the throne of the Most High.

people.name: Archangel Jegudiel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Commemoration of the Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae
people.summary: When pagans turned a river to flood the church and shrine of the Archangel at Chonae in Phrygia, the deacon Archippus prayed; the Archangel struck the rock with his staff and the waters were swallowed up.

people.name: Archangel Raphael
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archangel Selaphiel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archangel Uriel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archbishop Makarios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archelaus, Bishop of Harran
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Harran in northern Mesopotamia whose disputation with Mani is preserved in the Acts of Archelaus.

people.name: Archelaus, Kyrillos, Photios, and Virgin-martyr Irais, with one hundred and fifty-two companions of Egypt
people.summary: A great company of believers of Egypt slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Archil II, King of Georgia
people.summary: Eighth-century king of Iberia beheaded by an Arab emir for refusing Islam.

people.name: Archilias and Martyr Jeremiah of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Archippus, Philemon, and Onesimus of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named in the Letter to Philemon — its recipient, his wife's son, and the runaway slave who returned home a brother.

people.name: Archippus the Deacon of Hierapolis
people.summary: The deacon whose prayer at Chonae moved the Archangel to deliver the church from the rising flood.

people.name: Ardalion the Actor of Rome
people.summary: A Roman stage-actor under Maximian who while mocking Christians on the stage was suddenly converted, climbed off the platform, and was given over to the fire.

people.name: Ares of the Egyptian desert
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arethas and his companions of Najran
people.summary: A noble of sixth-century Arabia, slain with hundreds of fellow Christians by the Jewish king Dhu Nuwas of Himyar for refusing to renounce Christ — the martyrs whose blood prepared the way for Christian Ethiopia.

people.name: Ariadne
people.summary: Sixth-century daughter of the emperor Leo I, raised in piety in the imperial palace at Constantinople.

people.name: Ariadne of Phrygia
people.summary: A maidservant of second-century Phrygia who fled into the mountains rather than join her master's pagan rites and was hidden in a cleft of the rock.

people.name: Arianus, Philemon, and Theoctychus of Alexandria
people.summary: A governor of Alexandria converted by the patience of his prisoners under Diocletian and beheaded together with them and two of his guards.

people.name: Aristarchus, Pudens, and Trophimus of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Aristarchus of Thessalonica, Pudens the Roman senator, and Trophimus the Ephesian.

people.name: Aristides the Athenian
people.summary: A philosopher of second-century Athens whose apology for the faith addressed to Hadrian is the earliest extant.

people.name: Aristion, Bishop of Alexandria Scabiosa
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Alexandria Scabiosa in Cilicia, witness of Christ in the days of the apostolic fathers.

people.name: Aristobulus, Bishop of Britain
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aristobulus of the Seventy, first Bishop of Britain
people.summary: An apostolic-age disciple by tradition sent to the British Isles, identified with the Aristobulus whom Paul greets in Romans.

people.name: Aristocleus, Demetrian, and Athanasius of Salamis in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aristocleus the Priest, Demetrian the Deacon, and Athanasius the Reader of Salamis
people.summary: A priest, a deacon, and a reader of Salamis in Cyprus all crowned together under Diocletian.

people.name: Ariston the Wonderworker, Bishop of Arsinoe in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Aristotle and Leandros
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arkadios, Archbishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Sixth-century archbishop of Cyprus whose long episcopate spanned the reign of Justinian.

people.name: Arsacius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arsen the Great, Catholicos of Georgia
people.summary: A ninth-century catholicos of the Georgian church remembered for his learning, his hymnody, and his peace with his suffering people.

people.name: Arsenius, Archbishop of Corfu
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arsenius, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Arsenius the Ascetic of Mount Latros
people.summary: A Byzantine patrician who set aside his rank for the monastic life on the holy mountain near Miletus.

people.name: Arsenius the Great
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt and tutor of the imperial princes Arcadius and Honorius before his flight to the wilderness of Scetis.

people.name: Artemidorus and Thallos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Artemius of Antioch
people.summary: A noble general under Constantine the Great who refused to share in Julian the Apostate's restoration of idolatry, was stripped of his command, and was crushed beneath a great stone at Antioch.

people.name: Artemon, Bishop of Seleucia in Pisidia
people.summary: A first-century bishop ordained by the apostle Paul on his journey through Pisidia.

people.name: Forefeast of the Annunciation
people.summary: The eve of the Annunciation. Also commemorated: Artemon, presbyter of Laodicea in the second century, ordained by Paul on his last journey through Phrygia and afterwards bishop in his old age.

people.name: Artemon, Presbyter of Laodicea
people.summary: A priest of fourth-century Laodicea in Asia who, having served the church from his youth, received the crown of martyrdom in old age by fire.

people.name: Ascepsimus, Monk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ascholius, Bishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Thessaloniki, friend of Basil the Great, who baptized the emperor Theodosius the Great.

people.name: Asclas of the Thebaid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asclepiades and Linus, with Martyr Sabina of Smyrna
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asclepias the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asclepius and Jacob, Monks of Cyrrhus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ashot I Kurapalates of Georgia
people.summary: Ninth-century prince of Tao-Klarjeti and founder of the Bagrationi house of Georgia, slain by the Arabs for the cause of his Christian people.

people.name: Ashot Kuropalates of Tao-Klarjeti
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asiya the Wonderworker of Tanis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asteius, Bishop of Dyrrachium in Macedonia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asterius, Bishop of Petra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asterius, Metropolitan of Amasea
people.summary: Fourth-century metropolitan of Amasea on the Black Sea coast, a celebrated preacher whose homilies still survive.

people.name: Astius, Bishop of Dyrrachium in Macedonia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Asyncretus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasia and her daughters Theoctista, Theodota, and Eudoxia of Canopus
people.summary: A mother and her three daughters of Egypt strengthened in their suffering by Cyrus and John and beheaded with them.

people.name: Athanasia the Wonderworker of Aegina
people.summary: Ninth-century abbess of Aegina who at her widowhood took up the monastic life with her companions.

people.name: Athanasia the Wonderworker of Aegina with Matthew (Matthias)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasios, Bishop of Corinth
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasios the Pentaschoinitis of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius, Bishop of Chytri in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius, Bishop of Methone
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius, Bishop of Methoni in Messenia
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Methoni in the Peloponnese, a Catanian by birth and renowned for his pastoral care.

people.name: Athanasius, Bishop of Tarsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius, Nicholas, and Anthony of Vatopedi
people.summary: Three tenth-century disciples of Athanasius the Athonite and co-founders of the Vatopedi monastery on Mount Athos.

people.name: Athanasius of Mount Athos
people.summary: The tenth-century founder of the Great Lavra and organizer of cenobitic monasticism on Athos under Nicephorus Phocas, reposed when the dome of his church collapsed upon him as he inspected its construction.

people.name: Athanasius of Klysma in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athanasius of the Medikion Monastery
people.summary: A ninth-century abbot of the Medikion monastery in Bithynia who together with Nicephorus suffered exile under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: Athanasius the Confessor of Constantinople
people.summary: Ninth-century monastic confessor of Constantinople who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under Leo V the Armenian.

people.name: Athanasius the Deacon of Jerusalem
people.summary: A fifth-century deacon of Jerusalem slain by monophysite monks for defending the Council of Chalcedon.

people.name: Athanasius the Great, Archbishop of Alexandria
people.summary: The fourth-century defender of the Nicene faith against the Arians, five times exiled for confessing the Son consubstantial with the Father. Today commemorates the translation of his relics.

people.name: Athanasius the Hieromonk of Traiannou Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Athenagoras of Athens
people.summary: Second-century Athenian apologist whose Plea on behalf of the Christians was addressed to Marcus Aurelius.

people.name: Athenodorus of Mesopotamia
people.summary: An early-fourth-century monastic of Mesopotamia whose endurance through every torture under Diocletian shamed his judges.

people.name: Athenodorus the Leper
people.summary: A fourth-century disciple of Pachomius the Great in the cenobia of upper Egypt.

people.name: Athenogenes, Bishop of Heracleopolis
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop in Cappadocia who composed the evening hymn 'O Gladsome Light' as he was led to execution under Diocletian, and was beheaded with ten of his disciples.

people.name: Atre of Nitria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Attalus the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Atticus, Agapius, Eudoxius, Carterius, Istucarius, Pactobius, and Nictopolion of Sebaste
people.summary: A company of fourth-century soldiers at Sebaste in Armenia crowned together under Licinius.

people.name: Atticus and Cyntianus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Atticus and Sissinius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Atticus, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Constantinople who at length reconciled the followers of John Chrysostom to the see.

people.name: Auctus, Taurion, and Thessalonica of Amphipolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Audatus of Phrygia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Augusta, the Empress, with Porphyrius Stratelates and the two hundred soldiers
people.summary: The empress Augusta and her commander Porphyrius and his soldiers, all converted by St. Catherine in prison and beheaded with her.

people.name: Augustina of Byzantium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Augustine, Bishop of Hippo
people.summary: Late-fourth- and early-fifth-century bishop of Hippo in north Africa whose Confessions and City of God shaped Western Christian thought.

people.name: Autonomus, Bishop in Italy
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Italy who fled the persecution of Diocletian to Bithynia, founded a church there, and was slain at the altar by pagans of Soreoi after celebrating the Liturgy.

people.name: Auxentios of Laodicea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Auxentius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Auxentius of Bithynia
people.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Mount Auxentius near Chalcedon, attendant of the Fourth Ecumenical Council, after which the mountain bore his name.

people.name: Auxibius, Bishop of Soli in Cyprus
people.summary: First-century disciple of the apostle Mark who at Mark's word went to Soli in Cyprus and was bishop there fifty years.

people.name: Auxitheus (Eudoxius), Bishop of Thessaloniki, a father of the Fourth Ecumenical Council
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Azat the Eunuch of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century courtier of Persia and one of a thousand Christians slain together under Sapor II.

people.name: Azes of Isauria and one hundred and fifty soldiers with him
people.summary: An officer of third-century Isauria who at his arrest converted his guards and was beheaded with one hundred and fifty of them.

people.name: Babylas of Sicily with his disciples Timothy and Agapius
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Sicily and his two disciples martyred together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Babylas of Tarsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bacchus (Iakchos) of Triglia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bachtisius, Isaac, and Symeon of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bademus (Vadim), Archimandrite, with his seven disciples
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian abbot of a great monastery and seven of his community beheaded together under Sapor II.

people.name: Bagrat III, King of Georgia
people.summary: Eleventh-century king of a united Georgia under whom the catholicate of Mtskheta was set in order.

people.name: Baradates of Antioch
people.summary: A fifth-century anchorite of the Antiochene wilderness who lived shut up in a wooden box exposed to the elements.

people.name: Barbara
people.summary: A noble maiden of third-century Heliopolis whose pagan father, finding she had received baptism while he was away, denounced her himself and beheaded her with his own hand — and was struck dead by lightning as he came down the mountain.

people.name: Barbaros the Myrrhgusher of Pentapolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Barbarus, Bacchus, Callimachus, and Dionysius of the Morea
people.summary: A soldier and three companions of fourth-century Greece slain together under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Barbarus the Former Brigand of Epirus
people.summary: A ninth-century brigand of Greece converted in repentance who at length received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Barbarus the Myrrh-gusher
people.summary: An early-ninth-century holy man of Greece whose relics streamed myrrh after his repose.

people.name: Bardas of Constantinople
people.summary: Founder in Constantinople of a monastery of the Forerunner.

people.name: Bargabdesian the Deacon of Arbela
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Barlaam and Ioasaph of India
people.summary: The hermit-teacher and the Indian prince whose dialogue, set down by John of Damascus, brings the gospel through the framework of the life of the Buddha.

people.name: Barlaam of Bald Mountain near Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Barlaam of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: An elderly peasant of Caesarea under Diocletian whose hand was held over the altar with burning coals and incense, and who endured rather than let the coals fall.

people.name: Barlaam of Khutyn, Wonderworker of Novgorod
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Barnabas and Sophronius of Soumela, with Christopher
people.summary: Three founders of the Panagia Soumela monastery near Trebizond on the cliffs of Mount Mela.

people.name: Barsabias and his ten disciples of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century abbot of a Persian monastery and his community, slain together under Sapor II for the name of Christ.

people.name: Barsanuphius the Great and John the Prophet of Gaza
people.summary: Sixth-century elders of Gaza whose surviving letters of spiritual direction are among the treasures of the desert tradition.

people.name: Barses of Edessa, Eulogius of Edessa, and Protogenes of Carrhae
people.summary: Three fourth-century bishops of Mesopotamia who suffered exile under the Arian Valens for their Nicene confession.

people.name: Barsimaeus, Bishop of Edessa
people.summary: Early-second-century bishop of Edessa who baptized Sarbelus and Bebaia and at length received the crown with them.

people.name: Barsus the Confessor, Bishop of Edessa
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Edessa exiled by the Arian Valens, who reposed in his place of banishment far from his see.

people.name: Bartholomew
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Baruch and John
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Barypsabas of Dalmatia
people.summary: A hermit of Dalmatia in the second century slain by robbers for the vial of the Lord's blood entrusted to his keeping.

people.name: Basil, Abbot of Patalaria Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil and Gregory, Uncles of Eustratius of Tarsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil and the sixty-nine companions of Scythopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil, Bishop of Hamah
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Hamah in Syria who suffered martyrdom for the name of Christ.

people.name: Basil the Confessor, disciple of St. Procopius
people.summary: The fellow sufferer and disciple of Procopius of Decapolis, who endured the same prisons and scourging for the icons and lived to teach in freedom after the great persecution had subsided.

people.name: Basil the Confessor, Bishop of Parium
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Parium on the Hellespont who refused to sign the iconoclast decree of the council of Hieria and was beaten and exiled, surviving to die in peace.

people.name: Basil, Euphrasius, Florentius, and Alexander of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil I, Emperor of New Rome
people.summary: Ninth-century founder of the Macedonian dynasty whose reign saw the restoration of churches and the codification of Byzantine law.

people.name: Basil I the Confessor, Archbishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil II the Synaxaristis of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil, Bishop of Amasea
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Amasea on the Black Sea who tried to deliver the empress Glaphyra from the unwanted attentions of Licinius; the emperor in his rage had Basil beheaded on the shore of the Bosphorus.

people.name: Basil of Ancyra
people.summary: A fourth-century layman of Ancyra in Galatia beheaded under Julian the Apostate, distinct from the priest-martyr of March 22.

people.name: Basil of Cappadocia, Founder of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil of Khakhuli
people.summary: Eleventh-century Georgian prince, son of King Bagrat III, who took the monastic habit at Khakhuli.

people.name: Basil, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basil, Stephen, Gregory, and the three hundred and thirty-nine monks of Constantinople
people.summary: A great body of monastics slain in eighth-century Constantinople with Stephen the New for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Circumcision of the Lord
people.summary: On the eighth day from His birth the Lord was circumcised and given the name Jesus, as the angel had said. The Church remembers Him together with the fourth-century archbishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia, framer of the Liturgy still served on Sundays of Lent and on his feast.

people.name: Basil the Younger of Constantinople
people.summary: Tenth-century anchorite near Constantinople whose disciple Gregory wrote his life and the great vision of the Aerial Toll-houses.

people.name: Basilides the Soldier of Alexandria
people.summary: An early-third-century Roman soldier of Alexandria converted at the patience of Potamiaena and crowned with her.

people.name: Basiliscus
people.summary: Nephew of St. Theodore the Tyro and a soldier of the Roman army, martyred in Comana at the start of the fourth century. St. John Chrysostom in his exile prayed at his tomb the night before he died.

people.name: Basiliscus the Elder
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Basilissa and Anastasia of Rome
people.summary: First-century disciples of the apostles Peter and Paul at Rome who gathered the bodies of the martyrs for burial under Nero and at length received the crown themselves.

people.name: Bassa, Igumenia of Jerusalem
people.summary: Fifth-century patrician of Jerusalem and abbess of the convent of St. Menas in the holy city.

people.name: Bassa of Edessa with her three sons
people.summary: A mother of fourth-century Edessa in Macedonia who encouraged her three sons to confession of Christ and shared in their crowning.

people.name: Bassa of Jerusalem
people.summary: Fifth-century abbess at Jerusalem who founded the monastery of St. Menas.

people.name: Bassa of Tyre
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bassian of Constantinople
people.summary: A fifth-century Syrian ascetic of Constantinople, founder of a monastery there and a fearless adversary of Nestorius.

people.name: Bassus and the forty-two companions with him, Martyrs of Heraclea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bassus, Eusebius, Eutychius, and Basilides of Nicomedia
people.summary: Four noble companions of the imperial court at Nicomedia crowned together under Diocletian.

people.name: Batas of Nisibis
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian hieromartyr slain under Sapor II for the name of Christ.

people.name: Beginning of the Dormition Fast
people.summary: The two-week fast preparing for the feast of the Mother of God's passing into life, observed from August 1 to August 14.

people.name: Benedict of Nursia
people.summary: The sixth-century father of Western monasticism whose Rule for the cenobium of Monte Cassino joined the desert's wisdom to the moderate work of community — 'pray and work,' in everything seeking the love of Christ.

people.name: Benjamin and Berius of the Hebdomon Palace
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Benjamin of Nitria
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose body grew vast in his last sickness so that the door of his cell had to be broken open at his repose.

people.name: Benjamin the Deacon of Persia
people.summary: A fifth-century deacon of Persia released from prison on condition of silence about Christ and, refusing the terms, returned to martyrdom under Yazdegerd I.

people.name: Bessarion the Great of Egypt
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony, whose sayings stand among the apothegms.

people.name: Bessarion the Great, Wonderworker of Egypt
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony and Macarius, whose sayings stand in the apothegms.

people.name: Betimius of Scetis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bianor and Silvanus of Pisidia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blaise of Amorion on Mount Athos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blaise of Amorium and Mount Athos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blaise of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Caesarea remembered for the healing of bodily and spiritual blindness.

people.name: Blaise, Bishop of Sebaste in Armenia
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop and physician who fled Diocletian's persecution into a cave where wild animals came to him for healing; arrested and tortured with wool-combs, he was finally beheaded.

people.name: Blaise (Vlasios) of Sklavaina
people.summary: Eleventh-century ascetic of Acarnania in Greece slain by Saracen pirates together with his fellow monks.

people.name: Blandina of Lyon
people.summary: A young slave of second-century Gaul whose endurance through every torment in the arena at Lyon humbled the wisdom of her masters.

people.name: Blessed Anastasius I, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blessed Andrew the Fool-for-Christ of Constantinople
people.summary: Tenth-century Slavic-born holy fool of Constantinople who with his disciple Epiphanius beheld the Pokrov vision at Blachernae.

people.name: Blessed Cleopatra and her son John of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century noblewoman of Egypt who brought up the body of St. Varus to her ancestral land and lost her young son in death the day she translated his relics.

people.name: Blessed Elesbaan (Caleb), King of Ethiopia
people.summary: Sixth-century Ethiopian king who avenged the martyrs of Najran and at length put off his crown to live in repentance as a monk.

people.name: Blessed Gregory, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blessed Jerome of Stridon
people.summary: Fifth-century Western father and ascetic who from Bethlehem translated the Scriptures into Latin — the Vulgate.

people.name: Blessed John the Soldier
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blessed Paul of Corinth, Fool-for-Christ
people.summary: A holy fool of Corinth who concealed his ascetic prayer under the disguise of madness.

people.name: Blessed Procopius of Ustyug, Fool-for-Christ
people.summary: Thirteenth-century holy fool of northern Russia, the first canonized fool-for-Christ of the Russian Church.

people.name: Blessed Studius, Founder of the Stoudios Monastery
people.summary: Fifth-century Roman senator who at Constantinople founded the monastery that would become the great Stoudios of Eastern monastic tradition.

people.name: Blessed Thais (Taisia) of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Blessed Theophano, Empress of New Rome
people.summary: Ninth-century empress and first wife of Leo VI the Wise, who from her childhood lived in fasting and almsgiving and was canonized soon after her repose.

people.name: Blessed Theosebia the Deaconess
people.summary: Sister of Basil the Great and Gregory of Nyssa, a deaconess whose service in the church Gregory the Theologian sang of after her repose.

people.name: Blessed Xenia of St. Petersburg, Fool-for-Christ
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Boniface and Righteous Aglaida of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Boniface of Tarsus
people.summary: An early-fourth-century official of Rome sent to gather martyrs' relics from Tarsus who at the sight of their courage confessed Christ himself and was crowned with them.

people.name: Passion-Bearers Boris and Gleb
people.summary: Sons of Great Prince Vladimir, who at his death refused to take up arms against their elder brother Sviatopolk for fear of shedding kindred blood. Both were murdered by Sviatopolk's men. The first canonized saints of the Russian Church and the icon of non-resistance to evil.

people.name: Boris-Michael, Prince of Bulgaria
people.summary: Ninth-century khan of Bulgaria baptized by Byzantine missionaries who at length brought the whole of his nation to the Christian faith.

people.name: Boros
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Bretanion (Vetranion), Bishop of Tomis
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Tomis on the Black Sea who stood unmoved against the Arian Valens when the emperor came to him in his church.

people.name: Bryaene of Nisibis
people.summary: An early-fourth-century desert mother of Nisibis in Mesopotamia, kinswoman of Ephraim the Syrian.

people.name: Bucolus of Smyrna
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Busiris
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Caesarius, Dacius, Sabbas, Sabinian, Agrippa, Adrian, and Thomas of Damascus
people.summary: Seven Christians of seventh-century Damascus crowned together for the faith in the early days of the Arab conquest.

people.name: Caesarius of Nazianzus
people.summary: Brother of Gregory the Theologian and physician at the court of Julian the Apostate, who at the brink of apostasy was reclaimed by his brother's prayers.

people.name: Callinicus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Callinicus of Cilicia
people.summary: A traveling preacher of Christ in Asia Minor under Diocletian, made to walk in iron sandals studded with nails for thirty leagues and then cast into a furnace at Gangra.

people.name: Callinicus, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Calliope (Callitropos), Nun of Cyprus, sister of St. Epiphanius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Calliope of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Calliopios of Pompeiopolis
people.summary: A young Christian of Cilicia under Maximian crucified head downwards in mockery of his Lord.

people.name: Callista, Evodus, and Hermogenes of Nicomedia
people.summary: Three siblings beheaded together at Nicomedia in the early fourth century.

people.name: Callistratus and the forty-nine soldiers with him
people.summary: A Christian soldier in third-century Rome whose unmoved patience under torture converted forty-nine of his comrades; they were sewn into sacks and cast into the sea, and the sacks broke open at the shore.

people.name: Candida and Gelasia of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Candida the Elder
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Canides of Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cantidius and Cantidianus of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Capito and Hymenaeus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Capito of Cherson
people.summary: One of the seven bishops sent in the fourth century to evangelize Cherson on the Black Sea, who reposed in peace there after great labors.

people.name: Capitolina and Eroteis of Cappadocia
people.summary: A noblewoman of Cappadocia and her maidservant beheaded together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Carion of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Scetis whose sayings stand among the apothegms of the elders.

people.name: Carpus, Papylus, Agathonike, and Agathodorus
people.summary: Carpus the bishop, Papylus the deacon, and two others of Pergamum under Decius who were dragged behind chariots, scourged, and finally beheaded together for refusing to sacrifice.

people.name: Carterius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: A teacher of fourth-century Caesarea slain under Diocletian for his confession.

people.name: Cassius, Philip, and Eutychius of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Castinus, Bishop of Byzantium
people.summary: Third-century bishop of the city of Byzantium, predecessor of the patriarchs of Constantinople.

people.name: Castor of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Castulus and Achaicus of Rome
people.summary: Castulus, chamberlain of Diocletian, buried alive in a pit for the Christians he hid in the imperial palace; Achaicus, his companion.

people.name: Catherine of Alexandria
people.summary: A noble maiden of Alexandria, peerless in learning, who at eighteen confounded fifty pagan philosophers gathered by Maximin to dispute with her; she was beheaded after the wheel of her torture was shattered by angels.

people.name: Cecilia of Rome, with Valerian, Tiburtius, and Maximus
people.summary: A Roman noblewoman of the third century who converted her husband Valerian and his brother Tiburtius and the soldier Maximus, all four crowned together for the faith.

people.name: Celestine I, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Rome whose legates at the Council of Ephesus stood beside Cyril of Alexandria against Nestorius.

people.name: Chaeremon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Chaeremon of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Charalampias the Wonderworker, Bishop of Magnesia
people.summary: A bishop of second-century Magnesia in Thessaly who at one hundred and thirteen years old endured many torments under Septimius Severus and converted his judges before being beheaded.

people.name: Charalampias of Magnesia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Charitina of Amisus
