people.summary: A noble orphan of Pontus raised by a Christian senator, who under Diocletian was scourged, had her teeth and hair torn out, and was at last cast into the sea — but coming up alive, was beheaded.

people.name: Chariton, Charita, Euelpistus, Hierax, Peonus, Valerian, and Justus, companions of Justin
people.summary: Disciples of Justin who at the trial before the prefect Rusticus shared in his confession and his beheading.

people.name: Chariton the Confessor
people.summary: A confessor under Aurelian who survived his tortures, settled in the Judean wilderness, and founded the first lavra at Pharan beyond Jericho — the father of Palestinian monasticism.

people.name: Child-martyr Barulas of Antioch
people.summary: A young boy of Antioch who, asked whether the gods were many or one, named Christ and was beheaded — encouraged in his answer by St. Romanus.

people.name: Child-martyr Carellus and his seven companions of Caesarea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christina and Callista of Caesarea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christina of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christina of Tyre
people.summary: A noble maiden of Tyre under Diocletian whose three rounds of torture ended in her piercing through with arrows.

people.name: Christodoula and her three sons
people.summary: A mother of the third century who suffered together with her three children for the name of Christ.

people.name: Christodoulos, with the household of Photini
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christodulus and Christodula
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christopher and Maura
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christopher I, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Tenth-century patriarch of Antioch slain by Saracen raiders for the cause of Christ.

people.name: Christopher of Lycia, with the Martyrs Callinike and Aquilina
people.summary: A third-century convert of huge stature who in his pagan days had borne the name Reprobus and was baptized Christopher (Christ-bearer); his witness brought Callinike and Aquilina and two hundred soldiers to the same crown.

people.name: Christopher the Roman of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Christopher, Theonas, and Antoninus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Chrysanthus and Daria
people.summary: A young convert of Roman Alexandria and the Vestal virgin sent to dissuade him; instead she became his wife in faith and they lived in chastity together. Both were buried alive in a sand-pit under Numerian.

people.name: Chrysanthus and Euphemia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Chryse
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Chrysogonus of Aquileia
people.summary: Catechist of St. Anastasia at Rome and Aquileia, beheaded for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Chrysogonus of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Eparch of Thessaloniki who at length received the crown of martyrdom for the name of Christ.

people.name: Chusdazat (Usphazanes) and Azat the Eunuchs of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cindeus, Bishop of Pisidia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cindeus the Presbyter of Pamphylia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Claudia Procula, wife of Pontius Pilate
people.summary: The wife of the Roman procurator who at the Lord's trial sent word to her husband not to condemn the Just One she had seen in a dream.

people.name: Claudius, Asterius, Neon, and Theonilla of Aegae
people.summary: Four believers — three brothers and their sister — of third-century Cilicia who under Diocletian were beheaded at Aegae for the name of Christ.

people.name: Claudius, Diodorus, Victor, Victorinus, Pappias, Serapion, and Nicephorus of Corinth
people.summary: Seven believers of Corinth slain together under Decius for the name of Christ.

people.name: Claudius the Ascetic
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Claudius the Tribune, Hilaria, Jason, Maurus, Diodorus, and Marianus, companions of Chrysanthus and Daria
people.summary: A military officer of Rome, his wife, their two sons, and three more believers who at the witness of Chrysanthus and Daria converted and shared in their crown.

people.name: Clement and Thea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Clement I, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Late-first-century bishop of Rome, named by Paul in Philippians, whose letter to the Corinthians is among the earliest writings outside the New Testament; drowned with an anchor about his neck in the Crimea.

people.name: Clement of Ancyra
people.summary: Clement the boy-bishop of Ancyra, brought before nine successive judges over twenty-eight years of confession before his final beheading at the altar. Agathangelus, his disciple, was crowned with him.

people.name: Clement of Ohrid, Angelarius, Gorazd, Nahum, and Sabbas, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: Disciples of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century enlighteners of the Bulgarians and South Slavs.

people.name: Clement of Ohrid, Bishop of Bulgaria, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: Disciple of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century bishop of Ohrid, father of the Slavic literary tradition and enlightener of Bulgaria.

people.name: Clement of Ohrid, Naum of Preslav, Sabbas, Angelarius, and Gorazd
people.summary: Disciples of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century enlighteners of the Bulgarians and South Slavs.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Entrance
people.summary: The closing of the eight-day feast of the Entrance. Also commemorated: Clement, a disciple of the apostles Peter and Paul and third bishop of Rome, exiled to the Crimea and drowned with an anchor about his neck.

people.name: Clement the Hymnographer, Abbot of the Stoudios
people.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the Stoudios monastery in Constantinople, composer of many of the canons of the Octoechos.

people.name: Cleopas, Tertius, Mark, Justus, and Artemas of the Seventy
people.summary: Five of the Seventy: Cleopas the brother of Joseph the Betrothed, to whom the Lord appeared at Emmaus; Tertius scribe of Romans; Mark cousin of Barnabas; Justus called Joseph the just; and Artemas bishop of Lystra.

people.name: Codratus, Emmanuel, Theodosius, and forty companions
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Codratus (Quadratus)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Codratus, Saturninus, and Rufinus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cointus of Phrygia the Confessor and Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cointus (Quintus) the Wonderworker of Phrygia
people.summary: A third-century confessor of Phrygia known for his many miracles, who at his release after long torture reposed in peace.

people.name: Commemoration of the Finding of the Precious Cross by the Empress Helena
people.summary: The uncovering at Jerusalem in 326 by Constantine's mother Helena of the True Cross together with the nails.

people.name: Commemoration of the Flight into Egypt
people.summary: The journey of the Holy Family from Bethlehem into Egypt at the angel's warning, that the word might be fulfilled: Out of Egypt have I called my Son.

people.name: Commemoration of the Founding of Constantinople
people.summary: Constantine the Great's dedication of the city of New Rome on the Bosphorus in the year 330.

people.name: Commemoration of the Great Earthquake at Constantinople in 740
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Commemoration of the Holy Fathers of the Second Ecumenical Council
people.summary: The hundred and fifty fathers of the council of Constantinople in 381 who completed the Nicene Creed and confirmed the divinity of the Holy Spirit.

people.name: Commemoration of the Placing of the Precious Robe of the Lord at Moscow
people.summary: The seventeenth-century enshrining of a fragment of the Lord's seamless tunic in the Dormition Cathedral of the Moscow Kremlin.

people.name: Commemoration of the Twelve Minor Prophets
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Companions of Agathonicus: Martyrs Zoticus, Acindynus, and others
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Companions of Agrippina: Martyrs Paula, Bassa, and Agathonica
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Companions of Lucillian: Martyrs Claudius, Hypatius, Paul, and Dionysius
people.summary: Four young men baptized by Lucillian and crucified with him at Byzantium.

people.name: Companions of Patrick of Prusa: Acacius, Menander, and Polyenos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Companions of St. Dionysius: Rusticus the Priest and Eleutherius the Deacon
people.summary: The presbyter and the deacon who labored with Dionysius in Gaul and were beheaded with him outside Paris.

people.name: Confessor Anubius of Scetis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Conon, Abbot of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Conon and Christopher of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Conon of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Conon of Isauria and his father Nestor
people.summary: A first-century Christian of Isauria and his father, both beheaded together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Constantine I, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Constantine III, Emperor of New Rome
people.summary: Seventh-century Byzantine emperor and son of Heraclius, remembered for his piety and brief reign.

people.name: Constantine-Kakhi, Prince of Georgia
people.summary: A ninth-century prince of Kartli in Georgia martyred in Samarkand by Caliph al-Mutawakkil for refusing Islam.

people.name: Constantine of Synnada
people.summary: A ninth-century convert from Judaism who became a monk and reposed in his monastery at Synnada in Phrygia.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Constantine and his mother Helena
people.summary: The emperor who saw the sign of the Cross before the Milvian Bridge in 312 and granted peace to the Church, and his mother who in old age sought out and recovered the True Cross in Jerusalem.

people.name: Coprius of Palestine
people.summary: A sixth-century monk of Palestine raised from infancy in the wilderness, who reposed in peace at his lavra.

people.name: Cornelius the Centurion
people.summary: The Roman captain at Caesarea baptized by Peter — the first Gentile received into the Church — who reposed bishop of his city.

people.name: Coronatus, Bishop of Iconium
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Iconium in Lycaonia beheaded under the governor Perennius.

people.name: Cosmas, Abbot in Bithynia
people.summary: A tenth-century abbot of a monastery in Bithynia, remembered for his patient leadership.

people.name: Cosmas and Auxentius of Chalcedon
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop and his fellow ascetic, both confessors of the holy icons.

people.name: Unmercenaries Cosmas and Damian of Rome
people.summary: Brothers and physicians of third-century Rome who healed the sick and refused all payment, declaring that they had received freely and so must freely give. They reposed in peace after long labors.

people.name: Cosmas and Damian, Unmercenaries of Asia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cosmas the Hermit of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cosmas the Hymnographer, Bishop of Maiuma
people.summary: Eighth-century foster-brother of John of Damascus, author of many of the great festal canons of the Church and bishop of Maiuma near Gaza.

people.name: Crescens, Maximus, and Theonest, Bishops of Mainz
people.summary: Three fifth-century bishops of Mainz in Gaul who succeeded one another in the see and were crowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Crescens of Myra in Lycia
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Myra given over to the fire for his confession.

people.name: Crispus of the Seventy, Bishop of Chalcedon
people.summary: An early disciple of Paul named in First Corinthians, the ruler of the Corinthian synagogue whom the apostle himself baptized.

people.name: Cyprian and Juliana of Tarsus
people.summary: A bishop and a maiden of Tarsus under Maximian whose case Justina of Antioch would echo a generation later.

people.name: Cyprian of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cyprian and Martyr Justina
people.summary: A former pagan magician of Antioch converted when his spells against the virgin Justina were broken by her prayer; bishop and virgin alike were beheaded together at Nicomedia.

people.name: Cyria, Caleria, and Marcia of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: Three Christian women of Caesarea slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Cyriacus, Chorepiscopus of Jerusalem
people.summary: The Jewish guide of Helena who revealed to her the place of the Lord's Cross, was baptized, became bishop, and under Julian the Apostate was at last martyred.

people.name: Cyriacus, Chorepiscopus of Jerusalem, and his mother Anna
people.summary: The Jewish guide of Helena who at the discovery of the True Cross was baptized with his mother and both at length received the crown under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Cyriacus, Faustus, Abibus, and ten others of Alexandria
people.summary: A company of Alexandrian Christians beheaded together for the name of Christ around the year 250.

people.name: Cyriacus the Hermit
people.summary: A sixth-century desert father of Palestine, disciple of Euthymius and abbot at the Souka lavra, whose counsel guided many through the Origenist controversies of his day.

people.name: Cyricus and his mother Julitta
people.summary: A widow of Iconium and her three-year-old son, martyred at Tarsus under Diocletian.

people.name: Cyricus (Quiricus) of Apros in Thrace
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cyril and Eumenius of Gortyna, with Andrew of Crete, the Synaxis of the Bishops of Crete
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cyril and Methodius and their disciples Clement, Sava, Angelar, Gorazd, and Nahum
people.summary: The Slavic apostles of the ninth century and their five disciples — the seven Equals-to-the-Apostles whom the Bulgarian church gathers together this day.

people.name: Cyril, Bishop of Gortyna in Crete
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Gortyna who, having confessed under Diocletian and survived, was at length beheaded in old age.

people.name: Cyril, Chindeu, and Tasie of Axiopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Cyril and Methodius
people.summary: Brothers from Thessalonica who in the ninth century brought the Gospel and the liturgy to the Slavic peoples, devising the alphabet that bears their name and translating the Scriptures into Slavonic.

people.name: Cyril I, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cyril, Archbishop of Alexandria
people.summary: The fifth-century pillar of Orthodoxy who at the Third Ecumenical Council in Ephesus defended the title Theotokos against Nestorius, and whose treatises remain a foundation of Christology.

people.name: Cyril of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: A young Christian of third-century Caesarea baptized in secret against his pagan father's will and beheaded for his confession.

people.name: Cyril, Archbishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: The fourth-century catechist of the newly baptized whose lectures from the Anastasis preserve the rites and teaching of the Jerusalem church; exiled three times by Arian opposition, restored by the Second Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Cyrilla of Cyrene
people.summary: A widow of fourth-century Libya tortured for refusing to offer incense to idols.

people.name: Cyrus (Abba-Cyr) of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: the Unmercenaries Cyrus and John
people.summary: The fourth-century Egyptian physicians who healed without payment and were beheaded under Diocletian; their relics were brought to Manuthi near Alexandria to drive out the cult of an idol.

people.name: Cyrus (Kyros), Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Cyrus the Monk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dada, Gabdelas, and Casdoe of Persia
people.summary: Kinsmen of King Sapor II of Persia whose conversion to Christ brought them to torment and death at his hand.

people.name: Dadas, Quinctillian, and Maximus the Readers
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Damaris of Athens
people.summary: The woman of Athens converted with Dionysius the Areopagite at the apostle Paul's preaching from the Areopagus.

people.name: Damian, Bishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Damian the Healer, Jeremiah, and Matthew of the Kiev Caves
people.summary: Three eleventh-century fathers of the Kiev Caves Lavra — Damian who healed by the holy oil, Jeremiah and Matthew who saw into the hearts of their brethren.

people.name: Damon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Danax, Mesirus, and Therin
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Daniel and the Three Holy Youths
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Daniel of Scetis
people.summary: Fifth-century elder of the Egyptian desert whose stories of his journeys are gathered in the Sayings of the Fathers.

people.name: Daniel of Thasos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Daniel the Confessor of Egypt
people.summary: A tenth-century Spanish dignitary who at length came to Egypt and lived out his days as a monk under the name Stephen-in-the-schema.

people.name: Daniel the Monk of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Daniel the Stylite
people.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Simeon the Stylite who at Constantinople ascended his own pillar near the Black Sea coast, where he stood thirty-three years counseling emperors and crowds alike.

people.name: Darius, Zosimus, Paul, and Secundus of Nicaea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dasius, Gaius, and Zoticus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Three Christian soldiers of Nicomedia drowned together in the sea for the name of Christ.

people.name: Dasius of Dorostolum
people.summary: A Christian soldier of fourth-century Dorostolum (modern Silistra) chosen by lot for the role of mock-king at the feast of Saturn, who refused the impure rites and was beheaded.

people.name: David and Constantine, Princes of Argveti
people.summary: Two Georgian princes who in 740 refused to renounce Christ before Murvan the Deaf and were thrown into the Rion river.

people.name: David and Tarechan of Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: David of Echmiadzin
people.summary: A seventh-century Armenian monk renowned for his ascetic life at the great see of his nation.

people.name: David of Hermopolis
people.summary: A sixth-century desert father in upper Egypt who from being a brigand was converted to long penance and a life of prayer.

people.name: David of Thessalonica
people.summary: A sixth-century ascetic who lived three years in the branches of an almond tree outside the walls of Thessalonica, drawing the city to repentance and reposing in peace at his cell.

people.name: David, Symeon, and George of Mytilene
people.summary: Three brothers of Mytilene who in the eighth and ninth centuries gave their long episcopal and ascetic labors to the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Day of Prayer for God's Creation
people.summary: A modern observance kept on the first day of the church year for the renewal of the created order.

people.name: Deaconess Phoebe of Cenchreae
people.summary: The servant of the church at Cenchreae near Corinth whom the apostle Paul commended to the Romans at the close of his epistle.

people.name: Commemoration of the Dedication of the Church of the Resurrection at Jerusalem
people.summary: The great basilica built by Constantine over the Tomb of the Lord at Jerusalem, dedicated in 335 on this day; the next day the Cross is lifted up over the gathered Church.

people.name: Demas and Protion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Demetrianos the Wonderworker, Bishop of Tamassos in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Demetrianus, Bishop of Antioch
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Antioch carried off into Persian captivity by Sapor I where he reposed in his exile.

people.name: Demetrianus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Cytheria in Cyprus
people.summary: A tenth-century bishop of Cyprus, husband and monk before he was raised to the episcopate, remembered for his prayer for his captured flock.

people.name: Demetrius and Donatus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Demetrius, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Bishop of Alexandria in the early third century who watched over Origen as a young man and reposed in peace after a long episcopate.

people.name: Demetrius, Evanthia, and Demetrian of Skepsis
people.summary: A first-century household of Skepsis on the Hellespont — father, mother, and son — who suffered together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Demetrius the Myrrh-streamer of Thessalonica
people.summary: A young proconsul of Thessalonica under Galerius, openly catechizing the people of his city, who was speared in the bathhouse where he had been imprisoned and whose tomb has streamed myrrh ever since.

people.name: Demetrius of Thrace
people.summary: A third-century Christian of the village of Davoudio in Thrace beheaded under the governor Pomplius.

people.name: Demetrius the Skevophylax of Constantinople
people.summary: Eighth-century keeper of the sacred vessels of the Great Church at Constantinople.

people.name: Desan, Mariabus, Abdiesus, and two hundred and seventy companions of Persia
people.summary: A bishop, a priest, a deacon, and a great company of clergy and laypeople of fourth-century Persia slain under Sapor II.

people.name: Diadochos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diadochos, Bishop of Photiki
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Photike in Epirus whose Hundred Chapters on spiritual perfection stand at the head of the Philokalia.

people.name: Didymos, Nemesios, and Potamios of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dinara, Queen of Khereti
people.summary: Tenth-century queen of Khereti in Georgia who restored the Christian faith of her people after long pagan resurgence.

people.name: Diodoros and Rodopianos of Aphrodisia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diodorus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diodorus and Rhodopianus the Deacons of Aphrodisia
people.summary: Two deacons of Aphrodisia in Caria slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Diodorus, Didymus, and Diomedes of Laodicea
people.summary: Three companions martyred at Laodicea under Julian the Apostate in the middle of the fourth century.

people.name: Diodorus of Emesus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diogenes and Abercius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diomedes and Laurence, shot with arrows
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diomedes, Eulampius, Asclepiodotus, and Golinduc, companions of Hyacinth
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diomedes, Julian, Philip, Eutychianos, Hesychius, Leonides, Eutychios, Philadelphos, Melanippos, and Parthagape of Nicomedia
people.summary: Ten witnesses of Christ at Nicomedia under Diocletian, put to death each by his own torment — fire, water, sword, gridiron, and stone.

people.name: Diomedes, Orestes, and Rodon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Diomedes the Physician of Tarsus
people.summary: A third-century Cilician doctor beheaded under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Dionysius, Bishop of Corinth
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Corinth and the city's apostle in his day, whose pastoral letters were preserved by Eusebius.

people.name: Dionysius Exiguus
people.summary: Sixth-century Scythian monk at Rome whose calculations gave the Church its reckoning of the years of the Lord.

people.name: Dionysius the Areopagite, Bishop of Athens
people.summary: A member of the Athenian council converted by the apostle Paul on the hill of Mars and consecrated first bishop of Athens. Tradition makes him a missionary to Gaul, where he was beheaded at the place that bears his name.

people.name: Dionysius the Great, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Third-century patriarch of Alexandria, disciple of Origen and confessor under Decius and Valerian, who endured exile and finally reposed in his see.

people.name: Dionysius the Wonderworker of Glushetsk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dioscorus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dioscorus of Cynopolis in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dismas the Good Thief
people.summary: The thief crucified at the Lord's right hand who confessed Him in his agony and heard the promise: 'This day shalt thou be with Me in paradise.'

people.name: Dius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dius, Bithonius, and Galycus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dius of Antioch the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Djan Darada (Aetius) the Ethiopian Eunuch
people.summary: The chamberlain of Queen Candace baptized in the chariot by Philip the Deacon on the road from Jerusalem to Gaza.

people.name: Djan Darada the Ethiopian Eunuch
people.summary: The chamberlain of Queen Candace baptized in the chariot by Philip the Deacon on the road from Jerusalem to Gaza.

people.name: Dodo of David-Gareji
people.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia and labored with David at Gareji.

people.name: Dometius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dometius of Phrygia
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Phrygia slain under Julian the Apostate's restoration of paganism.

people.name: Dometius the Persian
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian convert and monk of Nisibis who under Julian the Apostate, refusing to come out of his cave, was stoned to death together with two of his disciples.

people.name: Domitian, Bishop of Melitene
people.summary: Sixth-century wonderworker-bishop of Melitene in Armenia, kinsman and adviser of the emperor Maurice.

people.name: Domitilla of Rome
people.summary: A first-century niece of the Roman emperor Domitian exiled to the island of Ponza for her Christian confession.

people.name: Domnina, Berenice, and Prosdoce of Syria
people.summary: A mother and her two daughters who in flight from the persecutors of Antioch in 303 leapt together into a river rather than be defiled.

people.name: Domnina of Anazarbus
people.summary: A maiden of Cilicia under Diocletian whose feet were burned to the bone before she gave up her soul to God.

people.name: Domninus, Bishop of Salona, with eight soldiers
people.summary: First-century bishop of Salona in Dalmatia and the soldiers of his guard, all martyred together under Domitian.

people.name: Domninus of Thessalonica
people.summary: A fourth-century believer of Thessalonica who under Maximian was beaten with sinews and his bones broken for the name of Christ.

people.name: Domninus, Timothy, Theophilus, Theotimus, Dorotheus, Eupsychius, Carterius, Silvanus, Pamphilius, Philotheos, and Nearchos of Palestine
people.summary: Eleven Christians of Palestine put to death together under Maximinus Daia at the dawn of the fourth century.

people.name: Donagus the Bishop of Libya
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Donatus and Therinus of Bothrotus in Epirus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Donatus, Bishop of Euroea in Epirus
people.summary: A late-fourth-century bishop of Epirus whose holy life flourished under the emperor Theodosius the Great.

people.name: Donatus, Macarius, and Theodore of Thmuis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Donatus of Libya
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Donatus, Romulus, Secundian, and 86 companions of Concordia
people.summary: A great company of believers near Venice slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Dorotheus, Abbot of Gaza
people.summary: Seventh-century elder of Gaza whose Discourses, drawing on the teaching of Barsanuphius and John the Prophet, became a classic of the desert tradition.

people.name: Dorotheus of Gaza
people.summary: Sixth-century disciple of Barsanuphius and John whose Discourses formed Eastern monasticism.

people.name: Dorotheus of Thebes
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dorotheus, Bishop of Tyre
people.summary: A bishop of immense learning who endured exile under Diocletian, returned to his see under Constantine, and was beaten to death under Julian the Apostate at over a hundred years of age.

people.name: Dorotheus the Hermit of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Thebaid known for his unbroken silence and ceaseless prayer.

people.name: Dositheos of Gaza
people.summary: Disciple of Dorotheus whose short and obedient life Dorotheus wrote as an icon of submission.

people.name: Dositheus of Gaza
people.summary: Seventh-century disciple of Abba Dorotheus of Gaza whose perfect obedience to his elder is set forth in the Discourses.

people.name: Dracontius, Bishop of Nicaea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Drakonas of Arauraka in Armenia
people.summary: A fourth-century Armenian Christian beheaded under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Drosis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Dulas of Cilicia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Easios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elappas
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elenos, Bishop of Tarsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eleutherius and Leonides of Constantinople, with many infants
people.summary: Two early-fourth-century Christians of Constantinople burned together with many infants for the name of Christ.

people.name: Eleutherius and Zoilus of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eleutherius of Byzantium
people.summary: Second-century bishop of the city of Byzantium slain for the name of Christ in the days of Hadrian.

people.name: Eleutherius of Constantinople
people.summary: A young chamberlain of fourth-century Constantinople in the days of Maximian, crowned for confessing Christ before the emperor.

people.name: Eleutherius, Bishop of Illyria
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Illyrian Christians under Hadrian who was scourged, roasted on a gridiron, cast to lions, and finally beheaded — together with his pious mother Anthia and the soldier converted at his judgment.

people.name: Elias I, Patriarch of Jerusalem, Confessor
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Jerusalem exiled by Anastasius for the same Chalcedonian confession.

people.name: Elias II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elias, Metropolitan of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Sixth-century metropolitan of Thessaloniki who took a leading role in the Fifth Ecumenical Council at Constantinople.

people.name: Elias of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elias of Murom, Wonderworker of the Kiev Caves
people.summary: Twelfth-century warrior of Russian epic who became a monk of the Kiev Caves at the end of his days.

people.name: Elias, Probus, and Ares of Egypt
people.summary: Three Egyptian Christians who under Maximian came up to Cilicia to comfort the imprisoned brethren and shared in their crowns.

people.name: Elias the Cave-dweller of Calabria
people.summary: A tenth-century hermit of Calabria in southern Italy who passed his days in a cave with his spiritual father Arsenius.

people.name: Elias the Hermit of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt renowned for spiritual power and many miracles.

people.name: Elias the Younger of Calabria
people.summary: Ninth- and tenth-century Sicilian hermit of Enna who at his repose was honored across both Calabria and Greece.

people.name: Elias, Zoticus, Lucian, Valerian, Macrobius, and Gordian of Tomis
people.summary: Six companions martyred together at Tomis on the Black Sea coast under Licinius.

people.name: Elijah of Heliopolis
people.summary: Eighth-century Christian of Damascus called Elias the New, slain by the Saracens for refusing Islam.

people.name: Elizabeth the Wonderworker of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ellius of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elpidephorus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Elpidius, Marcellus, and Eustochius
people.summary: Three believers slain together under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

people.name: Emilia, mother of Basil the Great
people.summary: Mother of Basil the Great, Gregory of Nyssa, Macrina the Younger, Peter of Sebaste, and Naucratius — the mother of saints.

people.name: Emilian, Bishop of Trebia, with one thousand companions
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Umbria slain together with a great company of his flock under Diocletian.

people.name: Emilian the Confessor, Bishop of Cyzicus
people.summary: A ninth-century hierarch who at the Constantinopolitan synod of 815 refused to subscribe to the iconoclast decree and was driven from his see into a long exile.

people.name: Emilian of Silistria
people.summary: A young Christian slave in Thrace who under Julian the Apostate publicly broke the idols of his master's town and, refusing to deny it, was burned at the stake on the banks of the Danube.

people.name: Emilian the Confessor, Bishop of Cyzicus
people.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Cyzicus exiled by the iconoclast Leo V the Armenian.

people.name: Emmelia of Caesarea
people.summary: Mother of Basil the Great, Gregory of Nyssa, Macrina the Younger, Peter of Sebaste, and Naucratius — by all reckoning the mother of saints.

people.name: Empress Pulcheria of New Rome
people.summary: Fifth-century empress and sister of Theodosius II, defender of orthodoxy against Nestorius and convener of the Fourth Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Epaphras of the Seventy
people.summary: The Colossian disciple of Paul whom the apostle calls his fellow prisoner.

people.name: Ephraim, Bishop of Mylasa in Caria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ephraim, Founder of Novotorzhok Monastery
people.summary: An eleventh-century Hungarian-born monk of Russia who in mourning his brother George founded the Boris and Gleb monastery at Novotorzhok.

people.name: Ephraim of Antioch, Patriarch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ephraim, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Antioch who as governor of the east before his elevation had defended his city through earthquake and Persian assault.

people.name: Ephraim the Great, Bishop of Atsquri
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ephraim the Great of Atsquri
people.summary: Ninth-century Georgian monk and bishop of Atsquri.

people.name: Ephraim the Syrian
people.summary: The fourth-century deacon of Edessa whose hymns gave the Syriac-speaking world its theology in verse, and whose Lenten prayer — 'O Lord and Master of my life…' — still bows the Orthodox to the ground each weekday of Great Lent.

people.name: Epicharis and Gaiana of Rome
people.summary: A senator's wife and her companion at Rome beheaded under Diocletian after long torture for their confession of Christ.

people.name: Epictetus the Priest and Astion the Monk of Halmyris
people.summary: A priest and a monk of third-century Scythia Minor crowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Epictetus the Priest and Astion the Monk of Scythia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Epimachus of Pelusium
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Pelusium in Egypt who, learning that the persecutors were taking believers at Alexandria, went out to meet them and freely confessed Christ.

people.name: Epiphanius of Cyprus
people.summary: The fourth-century archbishop of Salamis and a tireless adversary of heresy. Also commemorated: St. Germanus, Patriarch of Constantinople, who defended the holy icons against the first iconoclasm.

people.name: Epiphanius, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Erasmus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eubula, mother of Panteleimon
people.summary: A Christian noblewoman of Nicomedia who raised her son Panteleimon in the faith.

people.name: Eudocia the Empress
people.summary: Wife of Theodosius the Younger who in her last years lived in penance and pilgrimage at the holy places of Jerusalem.

people.name: Eudokia (Ia) of Anatolia and her companions
people.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Persia martyred under Sapor II with a great company of believers.

people.name: Virgin-martyr Eudokia of Heliopolis
people.summary: A Samaritan woman of Heliopolis in Phoenicia, immensely wealthy and famously beautiful, who at the witness of a Christian monk gave away all her possessions and entered a convent — beheaded for the faith under Trajan.

people.name: Righteous Eudokimos of Cappadocia
people.summary: A Cappadocian officer of the ninth century whose holiness was concealed beneath the duties of his rank; his sanctity was revealed only after his repose, when miracles began at his tomb.

people.name: Eudoxius, Zeno, Macarius, and the one thousand one hundred and four soldiers with them
people.summary: A company of Christian soldiers at Melitene of Armenia who in the fourth century forfeited honor and life rather than offer sacrifice.

people.name: Eugene
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eugene and Macarius, Confessors of Antioch
people.summary: Two priests of Antioch exiled by Julian the Apostate to the wilderness of Mauretania, where they ended their days in fast and prayer.

people.name: Eugene of Trebizond and his companions Candidus, Valerian, and Aquila
people.summary: Four Christians of fourth-century Trebizond on the Black Sea coast who endured the fire and the rack for the name of Christ.

people.name: Eve of the Nativity of Christ
people.summary: A day of strict fast and the Royal Hours of the Nativity. Also commemorated: Eugenia, the daughter of the prefect of Alexandria, who disguised as a man entered a monastery and afterwards converted her family before her martyrdom in Rome.

people.name: Eugraphus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eulalius, Bishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eulampius and Eulampia
people.summary: A brother and sister of Nicomedia under Maximian who, denounced for refusing to share in the imperial sacrifices, walked together to the lions and to the sword.

people.name: Eulogia, mother of Theodosius the Cenobiarch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eulogius and Eulampius of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eulogius I, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Sixth- and seventh-century patriarch of Alexandria, friend and correspondent of Pope Gregory the Great.

people.name: Eulogius, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Late-sixth- and early-seventh-century patriarch of Alexandria, friend and correspondent of Gregory the Great of Rome.

people.name: Eulogius the Confessor, Bishop of Edessa
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Edessa exiled by the Arian Valens and restored at the emperor's death.

people.name: Eulogius the Hospitable of Constantinople
people.summary: A sixth-century Christian of Constantinople so given to hospitality that the angels in vision were said to seek out his lodging.

people.name: Eumenius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Gortyna
people.summary: A bishop of Crete in the seventh century, a gentle ascetic and worker of miracles, who reposed at Thebes after a journey to support persecuted brethren.

people.name: Euphemia the All-praised
people.summary: The fourth-century maiden of Chalcedon, tortured with peculiar cruelty under Diocletian, whose tomb in 451 would later receive and confirm the Tome of the Fathers at the Fourth Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Euphemia of Chalcedon (commemoration of her confirmation of the Tome at the Fourth Council)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euphrasia of Nicaea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euphrasia of the Thebaid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euphrasius, Bishop of Ionopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euphrosynus the Cook
people.summary: A humble ninth-century cook in a monastery of Palestine whose hidden holiness was revealed in a vision granted to one of the elders.

people.name: Euplius and Natalios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euplus the Deacon of Catania
people.summary: A deacon of Catania in Sicily under Diocletian who carried the book of the Gospels openly through the streets and, when arrested, was beheaded with that very book pressed to his breast.

people.name: Eupraxia of Tabenna
people.summary: Fourth-century maiden of Constantinople who entered a women's community at Tabenna in upper Egypt at the age of seven.

people.name: Eupsychios, Nearchos, and Karterios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eupsychios of Caesarea
people.summary: A young man of second-century Caesarea in Cappadocia tortured and beheaded under Hadrian for confessing Christ.

people.name: Eupsychius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: A newly-married noble of Caesarea under Julian the Apostate who together with his bridal companions broke down the temple of Fortune; for this he was beheaded, and a famine which followed the deed lifted only at the emperor's death.

people.name: Euschemon the Confessor, Bishop of Lampsacus
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Lampsacus exiled by the iconoclast Theophilus for his confession of the holy icons.

people.name: Eusebios and Charalampos of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eusebiotus, Alypius, and Juliana
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eusebius
people.summary: A guard at the Roman court of Diocletian converted by the witness of the bishop Theopemptus and crowned with him.

people.name: Eusebius, Neon, Leontius, Longinus, and four companions of Nicomedia
people.summary: Eight Christians of fourth-century Nicomedia slain together under Diocletian.

people.name: Eusebius, Nestablus, Zeno, and Nestor
people.summary: Four believers of Gaza torn to pieces by the pagan mob in 362 under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Eusebius, Nestabus, Zeno, Nestor, and Busiris of Gaza
people.summary: Three brothers and two companions of Gaza torn to pieces by the pagan mob under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Eusebius of Phoenicia
people.summary: A second-century believer of Phoenicia who of his own will came forward and confessed Christ before the judge.

people.name: Eusebius, Bishop of Samosata
people.summary: A fourth-century defender of Nicene Orthodoxy against Arianism, exiled under Valens and slain by a tile thrown from a rooftop by a heretical woman as he returned to his see.

people.name: Eusebius, successor of Anatolius at Laodicea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eusebius the Hermit of Asikha in Syria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eusebius the Priest and Martyr Felix of Terracina
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eusebius the Recluse of Mount Coryphe
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Forefeast of the Holy Transfiguration
people.summary: The eve of the Transfiguration, when the Church prepares to ascend with the Lord to the mountain. Also commemorated: the soldier Eusignius of Antioch, beheaded under Julian the Apostate at one hundred and ten.

people.name: Eustathios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eustathios, Thespesios, and Anatolios of Nicaea
people.summary: Three sons of a wealthy merchant of Nicaea baptized by Bishop Anthimus, who under Maximian Galerius died together under torture.

people.name: Eustathius and Callinicus of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eustathius, Archbishop of Antioch
people.summary: Fourth-century archbishop of Antioch and an early adversary of the Arians at the First Ecumenical Council, deposed afterwards and exiled to Trajanopolis.

people.name: Eustathius of Carrhae and his companions
people.summary: An eighth-century Christian of Carrhae in Mesopotamia and his companions slain together by the Saracens.

people.name: Eustathius, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Eleventh-century patriarch of Constantinople in the reign of Basil II.

people.name: Eustathius Placidas, his wife Theopiste, and his children
people.summary: A Roman general under Trajan converted by the vision of a Cross between the antlers of a stag on the hunt; reduced to poverty, separated from his family, then restored to command, he and his household were finally roasted alive in a brazen bull.

people.name: Eustathius the Cobbler of Mtskheta
people.summary: Sixth-century Persian convert at Mtskheta in Georgia slain by his own people for embracing Christ.

people.name: Eustathius the Confessor, Bishop of Kios in Bithynia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eustathius the Soldier of Ancyra
people.summary: A fourth-century Roman soldier of Galatia thrown into the river and delivered alive by an angel before his final crowning.

people.name: Eustochius, Gaius, Proba, Lollia, and Urban of Ancyra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eustolia and Sosipatra of Constantinople
people.summary: Two consecrated virgins of seventh-century Constantinople, the latter the daughter of the emperor Maurice, who together founded a convent in the imperial city.

people.name: Eustratius the Wonderworker of Tarsus
people.summary: A ninth-century abbot of Tarsus renowned for his ascetic discipline and his many miracles.

people.name: Eutexios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euthymius and Neophytus, founders of Docheiariou Monastery on Mount Athos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euthymius I, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euthymius the Confessor, Bishop of Sardis
people.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Sardis exiled three times and at last beaten to death for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Euthymius the Great
people.summary: The fifth-century Armenian-born monk of Palestine whose lavra outside Jerusalem trained Sabbas, Theoctistus, and a generation of saints, and whose stand at the Council of Chalcedon held the Holy Land in Orthodoxy.

people.name: Euthymius the New of Thessalonica
people.summary: A ninth-century monk of Athos, founder of a monastery on the holy mountain, who in his last years went up to Constantinople to confess the icons under the resurgent iconoclasts.

people.name: Euthymius the New, John of Iveron, George of Iveron, and Gabriel of Iveron
people.summary: Founders and companions of the Georgian monastery of Iveron on Mount Athos.

people.name: Euthymius the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euthymius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Madytos
people.summary: A tenth-century bishop of Madytos on the Hellespont known for the gift of miracles.

people.name: Euthymius the Younger of Thessalonica
people.summary: Ninth-century monk of Mount Athos and founder of a monastic community at Peristera near Thessaloniki.

people.name: Eutropia of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutropius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutropius, Cleonicus, and Basiliscus
people.summary: Three companions in arms of the great-martyr Theodore the Tyro who, after his crowning, were arrested at Amasea and crucified together for the same confession.

people.name: Eutyches, disciple of John the Theologian
people.summary: A hearer of the apostle John and afterwards of Paul, who confessed the faith with such constancy that even hunger, lions, and the rack would not silence him; he reposed at last in peace.

people.name: Eutychianus, Strategius, and Myron
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutychios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutychius and Florentius of Nursia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutychius, Bishop of Melitene
people.summary: A first-century disciple of the apostles who labored as bishop of Melitene in Armenia and received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Eutychius, Disciple of John the Theologian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eutychius, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: A sixth-century patriarch who presided over the Fifth Ecumenical Council at Constantinople, was deposed and exiled by Justinian for theological reasons, and was triumphantly restored under Justin II.

people.name: Eutychius the Subdeacon of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Evagrius, Benignus, Chrestus, Arestus, Kinnudius, Rufus, Patricius, and Zosima of Tomis
people.summary: Eight believers of Tomis on the Black Sea coast slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Evagrius of Georgia
people.summary: Sixth-century disciple of Shio of Mgvime in the Iberian wilderness.

people.name: Evagrius of Nitria
people.summary: A fourth-century monk of the Egyptian desert and disciple of Macarius the Great whose treatises shaped the inner life of Eastern monasticism.

people.name: Evagrius of Shio-Mgvime
people.summary: Sixth-century Georgian disciple of the Thirteen Syrian Fathers in the cave-monastery of Shio in Iberia.

people.name: Evangelus, Bishop of Tomi
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Evanthia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Evarestus of the Stoudios Monastery
people.summary: A ninth-century monk of the great Stoudios monastery of Constantinople in the days of Theodore the Studite.

people.name: Evellius of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Eventius and Theodoulus, Presbyters of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Evodius and Onesiphorus of the Seventy
people.summary: Two of the Seventy: Evodius the second bishop of Antioch after Peter, and Onesiphorus whom Paul names in the second letter to Timothy.

people.name: Fabian, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Rome whom Cyprian called incomparable, martyred under Decius.

people.name: Faith
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Fantinus the Younger of Calabria
people.summary: A tenth-century wonderworker of southern Italy, abbot of a Greek monastery in Calabria.

people.name: Fathers John, Sergius, Patrick, and others of the Monastery of St. Sabbas
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Faustus, Basil, and Lucianus of Darion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Faustus, crucified for five days
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Feast to the All-Merciful Saviour and Most Holy Mother of God
people.summary: The 1164 commemoration of the simultaneous victories of Prince Andrei Bogoliubsky of Russia and the emperor Manuel Comnenus, both attributed to the Cross of the Lord.

people.name: Virgin-martyr Febronia of Nisibis
people.summary: A young nun of Nisibis whose teacher had raised her for Christ from childhood, and who in 304 endured the most brutal torments under Diocletian without yielding her vow of virginity.

people.name: Felicissimus and Agapitus, Deacons of Rome
people.summary: Two deacons of Rome martyred with Pope Sixtus II in the persecution of Valerian.

people.name: Felix, Bishop of Spoleto; the Presbyter Januarius; and Martyrs Septeminus and Fortunatus of Lycaonia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Felix the Presbyter and Eusebius the Deacon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Fifteen Martyrs cast into the Sea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Firmilian, Bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia, disciple of Origen and convener of the synod of Antioch that excommunicated Paul of Samosata.

people.name: Firmus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Five Martyrs of Ashkelon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Five Martyrs of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Five Young Virgin-Martyrs of Lesbos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Flavian I, Archbishop of Antioch
people.summary: Late-fourth-century archbishop of Antioch and friend of John Chrysostom, defender of the Antiochene see against Arian intrusion.

people.name: Flavian the Confessor, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople deposed and beaten at the Robber Synod of Ephesus, vindicated posthumously by the Council of Chalcedon.

people.name: Flavian the Confessor of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople deposed by the Robber Synod of Ephesus and beaten so severely that he died of his wounds; vindicated at Chalcedon.

people.name: Flavian the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Flavianus II of Antioch, Patriarch and Confessor
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Antioch deposed and exiled by Anastasius the emperor for his defense of Chalcedon.

people.name: Flavius with Augustus and Augustinus of Asia Minor
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Florentius of Thessalonica
people.summary: An early Christian of Thessalonica burned alive for his confession.

people.name: Florus and Laurus of Illyria
people.summary: Twin stonemasons of the second century, commissioned to build a pagan temple in Illyria; they made of it a church instead, and were cast with their fellow workers into a deep well for confession of the Cross.

people.name: Florus, Bishop of Amisus
people.summary: A seventh-century bishop of Amisus on the Black Sea coast.

people.name: Forefathers Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
people.summary: The three patriarchs whom the Lord names His own — the fathers of Israel and the icon of the faithful.

people.name: Forefeast of the Procession of the Precious Wood of the Life-giving Cross
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Fortunatus, Achaicus, and Stephanas of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul in First Corinthians as the household of Stephanas, the first-fruits of Achaia.

people.name: Fortunatus, Donatus, twelve virgins, and six laymen of Sirmium
people.summary: A great company of believers of fourth-century Sirmium in Pannonia slain together under Diocletian.

people.name: The Forty-Five Martyrs of Nicopolis in Armenia
people.summary: A company of Christians from Nicopolis who in 319 were burned together at the stake by Licinius after enduring the breaking of their legs and many days of starvation.

people.name: Forty Martyrs of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: The Forty Holy Martyrs of Sebaste
people.summary: Forty Roman soldiers of the legion at Sebaste in Armenia under Licinius who, refusing the imperial sacrifice, were left naked on a frozen lake. One forsook them for the warm bath at the shore; one of the guards leapt in to take his place, and the forty crowns remained intact.

people.name: The Forty-two Martyrs of Amorion
people.summary: Forty-two officers of Byzantine Amorion taken to Samarra in 845 after the city's fall to the Caliph Mu'tasim; after seven years of imprisonment they were beheaded together for refusing Islam.

people.name: Four Martyrs of Tyre
people.summary: Four believers of Tyre in Lebanon slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Friardus of Vindumitta in Gaul
people.summary: A sixth-century Gaulish ascetic of Anjou who lived in a cell beside the chapel he had built with his own hands.

people.name: Frumentius, Archbishop of Abyssinia, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: Fourth-century Tyrian merchant boy who, taken into the Ethiopian court, was sent back by Athanasius as the first bishop of Axum and the enlightener of Ethiopia.

people.name: Fulvianus, the Prince of Ethiopia
people.summary: The Ethiopian ruler who in repentance for the martyrdom of Matthew received baptism, took the name Matthew, and ended his days in episcopal labors.

people.name: Gabriel, Abbot of St. Stephanos in Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gaius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gaius, Faustus, Eusebius, and Chaeremon, Deacons of Alexandria
people.summary: Four deacons of third-century Alexandria slain together for the name of Christ under Valerian.

people.name: Gaius, Pope of Rome, with Gabinus the Priest
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Galacteon and Pompeian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Galaction and Episteme
people.summary: A young husband and wife of third-century Emesa who shortly after their marriage parted by mutual agreement for the monastic life, were arrested for the faith, and were beheaded under Decius.

people.name: Gallicanus the Former Consul
people.summary: A fourth-century Roman general and consul who at the witness of John and Paul of Rome embraced the faith and at length received the crown under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Gelasius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gelasius of Palestine
people.summary: A fifth-century monk of Palestine whose obedience and wisdom are remembered in the sayings of the desert fathers.

people.name: Gelasius the Actor of Heliopolis
people.summary: Third-century actor of Heliopolis in Lebanon who while mimicking Christian baptism on the stage was suddenly converted in earnest and confessed Christ; the audience tore him to pieces on the spot.

people.name: Gemellus of Paphlagonia
people.summary: A Christian of fourth-century Ancyra crucified under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

people.name: Gennadius I, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Constantinople under whom the canons of Chalcedon were applied to discipline in the great city.

people.name: George, Abbot of Mount Sinai
people.summary: A sixth-century abbot of the Sinai monastery, brother of John Climacus.

people.name: George Arselaites
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: George, Bishop of Mitylene
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: George the Confessor, Metropolitan of Mytilene
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Lesbos who under the iconoclast Leo the Armenian endured beatings and exile rather than surrender the icons of the Church.

people.name: George Limniotes the Confessor
people.summary: Eighth-century anchorite of Mount Olympus in Bithynia whose hands were cut off and head burned by the iconoclast Leo III.

people.name: George, Metropolitan of Nicomedia
people.summary: Ninth-century metropolitan of Nicomedia, friend of Photius the Great and composer of many canons and troparia in the office books of the Church.

people.name: George of Atsquri
people.summary: Ninth- and tenth-century bishop of Atsquri in Georgia.

people.name: George of Mount Maleon
people.summary: A fifth- or sixth-century anchorite of the Peloponnese remembered for his austerity.

people.name: George, Parodus, and Peter of Bulgaria, with Prince Enravota-Boyan
people.summary: A bishop, two priests, and a Bulgarian prince all crowned together in the ninth century at the hands of the Bulgar khan Malamir.

people.name: George, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: George the Builder of Iveron Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: George the Canon-writer, Bishop of Amastris
people.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Amastris on the Black Sea, hymnographer and defender of his people from a great Russian raid.

people.name: George the Chozebite
people.summary: George the seventh-century abbot of the Chozeba monastery in the Wadi Kelt above the Jordan, and Domnica the Constantinopolitan ascetic of the fourth century who passed her last sixty years in silence at a small chapel by the harbor.

people.name: George the Confessor, Bishop of Amastris
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Amastris on the Black Sea, hymnographer and defender of his people from a great Russian raid.

people.name: George the Confessor, Bishop of Antioch in Pisidia
people.summary: Eighth-century bishop who under Constantine V the dung-named was driven from his see for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: George the Confessor, Bishop of Deifeltos
people.summary: Seventh-century bishop and confessor in the days of the monothelite controversy.

people.name: George the New, Wonderworker of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: George the Scribe and Sabbas of Khakhuli
people.summary: Two eleventh-century monk-calligraphers of the Khakhuli monastery in Tao-Klarjeti in Georgia.

people.name: George the Standard-Bearer of Mytilene and Kherson
people.summary: A ninth-century bishop exiled for his confession of the holy icons.

people.name: George the Trophy-bearer
people.summary: The young Cappadocian officer of the Roman army under Diocletian whose endurance through every torture and final beheading made him the icon of the soldier-saint of all the East; his cult spread from Lydda throughout the world.

people.name: George Ugryn of Russia
people.summary: An eleventh-century page of Prince Boris of Russia slain together with his master, brother of Ephraim of Novotorzhok.

people.name: Gerasimus of the Jordan
people.summary: A fifth-century desert father of the Jordan valley near Jericho who is the saint of the lion — he drew the thorn from a wild lion's paw and the beast became his servant until his death.

people.name: Germanus of Dacia Pontica
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of the lower Danube in modern Romania.

people.name: Germanus, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Eighth-century defender of the holy icons against the first iconoclasm.

people.name: Gerontius and Basilides
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Glorification of St. Herman of Alaska, Wonderworker of America
people.summary: Monk of Valaam who came with the first Orthodox mission to Alaska in 1794.

people.name: Glyceria
people.summary: A virgin of Trajanopolis in second-century Thrace who confessed Christ before the prefect, smashed the idol of Zeus, and was given to the lions, who would not touch her.

people.name: Glycerius and Athanasius the Magician of Nicomedia
people.summary: A Christian of Nicomedia and a converted magician slain together with George.

people.name: Gobron (Michael) of Georgia and the one hundred and thirty-three soldiers with him
people.summary: Tenth-century Georgian commander and his company slain together under Sajid sultan Abu'l-Qasim for refusing Islam.

people.name: Golinduc (Mary) of Persia
people.summary: A noblewoman of sixth-century Persia who at her conversion took the name Mary in baptism and was crowned for the name of Christ.

people.name: Prophet Malachi
people.summary: Malachi the last of the prophets of the old covenant, who foretold the messenger to come before the great and terrible day. Also commemorated: Gordius the centurion of Caesarea who under Licinius descended from his desert cave into the city's arena to confess Christ.

people.name: Gormizdas of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gratus and Nonnus, monks
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Great-martyr George
people.summary: Commemoration of the dedication of the great basilica at Lydda built over the relics of the soldier-martyr.

people.name: Gregentios, Archbishop of Zafar
people.summary: Sixth-century missionary bishop sent to the kingdom of the Himyarites in southern Arabia to consolidate the church after the persecution of Dhu Nuwas.

people.name: Gregory, Archbishop of Novgorod and Pskov
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory, brother of Gregory the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory of Acragas, Bishop of Sicily
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory of Constantius, Bishop in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory of Crete
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory of Khandzta
people.summary: Ninth-century father of Georgian monasticism in the Klarjeti wilderness whose Vita was written by Giorgi Merchule.

people.name: Gregory of Khandzta, Wonderworker of Georgia
people.summary: A ninth-century father of Georgian monasticism in the Klarjeti wilderness who shaped the renewed cenobitic life of Iberia.

people.name: Gregory of Nyssa
people.summary: The fourth-century Cappadocian father, younger brother of Basil the Great, whose meditations on the soul's ascent to God remain among the deepest in the Christian tradition — 'every end is a new beginning.'

people.name: Gregory of Panedus, Confessor of the holy icons
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apostle Philip
people.summary: One of the Twelve, who brought Nathanael to Christ and at the feeding of the five thousand counted the cost; he preached through Phrygia and was crucified head-downward at Hierapolis.

people.name: Gregory Palamas, Archbishop of Thessalonica
people.summary: Fourteenth-century defender of the divine energies (his Sunday is the Second of Great Lent).

people.name: Gregory the Confessor, Archbishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Ninth-century patriarch of Alexandria who suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Forefeast of the Entrance of the Theotokos
people.summary: The eve of the Entrance into the Temple. Also commemorated: the ninth-century monk of Greek Decapolis whose ascetic struggle and journeys through Asia Minor and Italy left a luminous example to his disciples.

people.name: Gregory the Dialogist, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Sixth- and seventh-century bishop of Rome whose Dialogues taught the East of the Western desert and whose Liturgy of the Presanctified is appointed in Lent.

people.name: Gregory the Elder, Bishop of Nazianzus
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nazianzus and father of Gregory the Theologian, brought into orthodoxy by his wife Nonna and his son.

people.name: Gregory the Illuminator of Armenia
people.summary: The fourth-century apostle of Armenia, who having endured fourteen years in a pit at Ardashes was raised to baptize King Tiridates and bring the whole nation to the faith of Christ.

people.name: Gregory the Theologian, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: The fourth-century friend of Basil the Great whose Five Theological Orations at Constantinople, given in the small church of the Anastasia, decided the dogma of the Trinity for the Second Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Gregory the Theologian, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory the Wise of Ohrid
people.summary: Bishop of Ohrid in Moesia in the early eleventh century.

people.name: Gregory the Wonderworker, Bishop of Neo-Caesarea
people.summary: A third-century disciple of Origen who found his see at Neo-Caesarea with seventeen Christians and left it with seventeen pagans, having converted the city by miracles, by teaching, and by a creed received in vision from St. John the Theologian.

people.name: Gregory the Wonderworker, Bishop of Neo-Caesarea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Gregory, Victor, and Geminus of Heracleon in Thrace
people.summary: Three companions slain together at Heracleon in Thrace under Diocletian.

people.name: Gudelia of Persia
people.summary: A noblewoman of fourth-century Persia who under Sapor II refused the worship of the sun and brought many of her household to Christ before being flayed alive.

people.name: Gurias, Samonas, and Habib of Edessa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hebarestes of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hegesippus the Chronicler
people.summary: Second-century Christian historian of Jewish descent whose Memoirs preserve early traditions of the Church of Jerusalem.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Constantine and his mother Helena
people.summary: The emperor who saw the sign of the Cross before the Milvian Bridge in 312 and granted peace to the Church, and his mother who in old age sought out and recovered the True Cross in Jerusalem.

people.name: Helen, Equal-to-the-Apostles, mother of Constantine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Heliodorus and Dosa of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Heliodorus of Pamphylia
people.summary: A third-century believer of Pamphylia who under Aurelian was hung up and his sides torn before being given up to death.

people.name: Helladius the Eastern
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Helladius
people.summary: A third-century bishop in the East, scourged and burned for confessing Christ during Diocletian's persecution. Few details survive beyond the constancy of his witness.

people.name: Heraclides and Myron, Bishops of Tamassos in Cyprus
people.summary: Two first-century bishops of Cyprus consecrated by the apostle Paul who at last received the crown by fire.

people.name: Heraclius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Heraclius, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Third-century bishop of Alexandria, the first churchman whom historical sources called by the title Pope.

people.name: Heraclius, Paulinus, and Benedimus of Athens
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Apostle Matthias
people.summary: Chosen by lot to fill the place of Judas among the Twelve, he preached the Gospel in Judea, Cappadocia, and on the shores of the Caspian Sea, and was stoned and beheaded for the faith.

people.name: Herman of Alaska, Wonderworker of America
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hermas of the Seventy, Bishop of Philippopolis
people.summary: One of the Seventy whom Paul names in his Letter to the Romans, by tradition the author of the Shepherd.

people.name: Hermas, Patrobas, Linus, Gaius, and Philologus of the Seventy
people.summary: Five of the Seventy whom Paul names in the Letter to the Romans: Hermas bishop of Philippopolis, Patrobas of Pozzuoli, Linus second bishop of Rome after Peter, Gaius of Ephesus, and Philologus of Sinope.

people.name: Hermes and Theodora of Rome
people.summary: A brother and sister of second-century Rome martyred together under Hadrian.

people.name: Hermes, Serapion, and Polyaenus of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hermias of Comana Pontica
people.summary: A second-century soldier of Comana who endured every form of torture under Antoninus and at his beheading converted his executioner.

people.name: Hermippus and Hermocrates, companions of Hermolaus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hermogenes
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hermogenes, Caius, Expeditus, Aristonicus, Rufus, and Galatas of Melitine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hermogenes the Wonderworker, Bishop of Samos
people.summary: An early bishop and wonderworker whose relics are venerated in Cyprus.

people.name: Hermolaus and those with him
people.summary: An aged presbyter of Nicomedia who instructed the young Panteleimon in the faith and baptized him; under Maximian he was beheaded with two fellow priests.

people.name: Hermylas and Stratonicus
people.summary: A deacon and his fellow soldier and friend at Belgrade under Licinius, whose ordeals strengthened each other rather than broke them; both were sewn into a net and cast into the Danube.

people.name: Herodion of Patras of the Seventy
people.summary: One of the Seventy whom Paul greets in Romans, kinsman of the apostle, who reposed bishop of Patras in Achaea.

people.name: Heron the Philosopher
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hesperos (Exuperius) and Zoe, with their sons Cyriacos and Theodoulos
people.summary: A family of fourth-century Attalia in Pamphylia, all slain together for refusing to share in the imperial sacrifices.

people.name: Hesychios the Sinaite
people.summary: Seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose two centuries On Watchfulness and Holiness shape the hesychast tradition.

people.name: Hesychios the Sinaite, Abbot of St. Catherine's
people.summary: Seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose two centuries On Watchfulness and Holiness shape the hesychast tradition.

people.name: Hesychios the Theologian of Jerusalem
people.summary: Fifth-century priest of Jerusalem whose commentaries on Leviticus and the Twelve Prophets, and whose collected centuries on watchfulness in the Philokalia, became classics of the Eastern tradition.

people.name: Hesychius of Dorostolum
people.summary: An early-fourth-century soldier of Moesia under Diocletian who at his discharge confessed Christ and was beheaded.

people.name: Hesychius the Faster of Bithynia
people.summary: An eighth-century ascetic of Mount Olympus in Bithynia renowned for his fasting.

people.name: Hesychius the Palatine of Antioch
people.summary: A high officer of the imperial palace at Antioch under Maximian drowned in the river for his Christian confession.

people.name: Hesychius the Senator of Antioch
people.summary: An imperial official of fourth-century Antioch under Maximian, drowned in the river for the name of Christ.

people.name: Hesychius the Silent of Mount Horeb
people.summary: A sixth-century hesychast of Sinai whose unbroken silence and ceaseless prayer shaped the Sinaitic monastic tradition.

people.name: Hierax (Ierakos) of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hierax the Ascetic of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hieria the Widow of Mesopotamia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hiero-Confessor Theodotus, Bishop of Cyrenia in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hieromartyr Phocas of Sinope
people.summary: Late-fourth-century translation of the bones of Phocas of Sinope from his see to Constantinople.

people.name: The Hieromartyrs of Cherson (Basil, Ephraim, Eugenius, Capito, Agathodorus, Etherius, Elpidius)
people.summary: Seven bishops sent from Jerusalem in the time of Diocletian to evangelize the Crimea who, in the fierce paganism of Cherson, were one by one stoned, dragged behind chariots, or beheaded.

people.name: Hieron of Melitene and thirty-three companions
people.summary: Thirty-three Christians of Melitene in Armenia under Diocletian, including the brave farmer Hieron who refused conscription and led his fellow soldiers to confession and execution together.

people.name: Hierotheus, first Bishop of Athens
people.summary: A disciple of the apostle Paul, present at the dormition of the Theotokos.

people.name: Hierotheus, First Bishop of Hungary
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hierotheus of Athens
people.summary: Together with Dionysius, a disciple of Paul at Athens and one of its first bishops, present at the dormition of the Theotokos and martyred in the apostolic age.

people.name: Hilarion the New, abbot of Pelecete
people.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelecete on the Black Sea, defender of the icons under Leo the Isaurian and his successors, who repeatedly endured imprisonment and torture for the cause.

people.name: Hilarion of Tvali in Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hilarion the Great
people.summary: A native of Gaza and the disciple of Anthony, who brought the desert life from Egypt to Palestine and afterwards to Cyprus, where he reposed in the wilderness at Paphos.

people.name: Hilarion the Iberian
