people.summary: Ninth-century Georgian monk of Mount Athos and later of Olympus whose journeys brought the spiritual treasures of Georgia to the Greek-speaking world.

people.name: Hilarion the New of Dalmatia
people.summary: Abbot of the Dalmatian monastery in Constantinople and a defender of the holy icons under Leo the Armenian, who suffered scourgings and imprisonment for confessing them.

people.name: Hilary the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hippolytus of Rome and his companions
people.summary: Third-century bishop and martyr at Ostia near Rome, slain together with more than twenty fellow Christians under Claudius II.

people.name: Hippolytus of Rome, with the Martyrs Concordia, Irenaeus, Abundius, and Coronatus
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Rome dragged by wild horses for his confession, together with his nurse and the believers slain with him under Valerian.

people.name: Hippolytus, Priest of Antioch
people.summary: A third-century priest of Antioch who having left the Novatianist schism for the orthodox faith received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: The Holy Fathers slain at the monastery of St. Sabbas
people.summary: Forty-four monks of the Great Lavra of St. Sabbas martyred in 614 by a band of Saracens — burned in their cells or driven into a cave where their bones were left in heaps.

people.name: Hope
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hosius the Confessor, Bishop of Cordova
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Cordova in Spain, court bishop of Constantine the Great and a chief drafter of the Nicene Creed.

people.name: Huldah
people.summary: The prophetess whom King Josiah's emissaries consulted at the finding of the book of the law in the temple.

people.name: Hyacinth of Amastris
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hyacinth of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: A young chamberlain of the emperor Trajan, betrayed by his fellow servants as a Christian, who in prison refused to break his fast even unto death and was crowned at the age of twenty.

people.name: Hypatius, Abbot of Rufinianes near Chalcedon
people.summary: Fifth-century abbot whose monastery near Chalcedon trained a great many of the monks who would attend the Fourth Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Hypatius, Abbot of Rufinus near Chalcedon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Hypatius and Theodoulos, companions of Leontius
people.summary: The two Roman soldiers sent to seize Leontius and instead converted by him, both beheaded with him at Tripoli.

people.name: Hypatius and thirty-six companions of the Thebaid
people.summary: A great company of believers of upper Egypt slain together for the name of Christ in the persecution of Diocletian.

people.name: Hypatius, Bishop of Ephesus, and Andrew the Presbyter
people.summary: A bishop of Ephesus and his priest, confessors of the holy icons under Leo III the Isaurian in the eighth century.

people.name: Hypatius, Bishop of Gangra
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Paphlagonia who took part in the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, and on his return journey was set upon by Novatian heretics and stoned to death.

people.name: Hypatius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Gangra
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Gangra in Paphlagonia, a father of the First Ecumenical Council, slain by Novatianists on his return from Constantinople.

people.name: Hyperechius of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Iasimos the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ibestion the Confessor of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Iconoclast-era Martyrs Andrew, Stephen, Paul, and Peter
people.summary: Four confessors of the eighth century who suffered under Constantine V the dung-named for the holy icons.

people.name: Ignatius, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
people.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the Bithynian monastery near Triglia who succeeded Peter and Luke in its leadership.

people.name: Apostle James, brother of John the Theologian
people.summary: One of the sons of Zebedee, who left his boats with his brother at the Lord's call and stood with him on Tabor; the first apostle to be martyred, slain by Herod at Jerusalem in the year 44.

people.name: Forefeast of the Nativity
people.summary: The eve of the eve of the Nativity. Also commemorated: the second-century bishop of Antioch, third successor of Peter, taken to Rome under Trajan and given to the lions, who wrote on the way the seven letters that mark the earliest pattern of the Church.

people.name: Ignatius of Mount Stirion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ignatius, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: A ninth-century patriarch of Constantinople — son of the emperor Michael I — twice driven from his see in the rivalry with Photius and twice restored to it.

people.name: Ignatius the God-bearer, Bishop of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ignatius the God-bearer of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Illyrius of Mount Myrsinon
people.summary: An ascetic of the Peloponnese remembered for his quiet labors of prayer.

people.name: Indiction: Beginning of the Church Year
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Inna, Pinna, and Rimma of Scythia
people.summary: Three Gothic disciples of the apostle Andrew, frozen to death in the rivers of Scythia for the name of Christ.

people.name: Innocent and Sabbatius of Sirmium
people.summary: Two early-fourth-century clergy of Pannonia slain together under Diocletian with thirty companions.

people.name: The 14,000 Holy Innocents slain by Herod at Bethlehem
people.summary: The children of two years and under whom Herod, in his rage at the magi, slew at Bethlehem and in all its borders. The Church names them the first martyrs for Christ — slain not for confession but in His place.

people.name: Innocent of Alaska, Apostle of America
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Innocent the Priest of the Mount of Olives
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ioannis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ioannis Palaiolavritis of the Judean Desert
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Irenaeus, Bishop of Sirmium
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Sirmium in Pannonia beheaded on the bridge of the Sava under Diocletian.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Dormition
people.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast of the Dormition. Also commemorated: the second-century bishop and disciple of Polycarp who from Gaul defended the apostolic tradition against the gnostics.

people.name: Irenaeus, Or, and Oropsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Irenaeus, Pellegrinus, and Irene of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Irene and Sophia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Irene Chrysovolantou
people.summary: Tenth-century abbess of Constantinople of Cappadocian birth, foundress of the Chrysovalantou convent.

people.name: Irene the Athenian, Empress of New Rome
people.summary: Eighth-century empress regnant who at the Seventh Ecumenical Council in Nicaea restored the holy icons to the Church.

people.name: Isaac and Joseph of Karnu
people.summary: Two believers of ninth-century Georgia put to death at Tiflis by the Saracens for refusing Islam.

people.name: Isaac and Martin
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Isaac, founder of the Dalmatian Monastery, with Dalmatius and Faustus
people.summary: Three fourth- and fifth-century abbots of the Dalmatian monastery at Constantinople in unbroken succession.

people.name: Isaac of Dalmatia
people.summary: The fourth-century abbot who rebuked the emperor Valens on his march against the Goths for closing the churches; the emperor died as Isaac foretold, and the monastery of Dalmatia rose at his prayer.

people.name: Isaac the Catholicos of Armenia
people.summary: Fifth-century catholicos of the Armenian church who with St. Mesrop directed the translation of the Scriptures into the new Armenian alphabet.

people.name: Isaac the Confessor, Founder of the Dalmatian Monastery
people.summary: Fourth-century anchorite who in coming to Constantinople rebuked the Arian Valens to his face and afterwards founded the great Dalmatian monastery there.

people.name: Isaac the Syrian, Abbot of Spoleto
people.summary: A sixth-century Syrian monk who came west to Spoleto in Umbria and founded a great cenobitic community there.

people.name: Isaac the Syrian, Bishop of Nineveh
people.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Nineveh who after a few months returned to the desert, leaving the Church his Ascetical Homilies that have shaped Orthodox prayer ever since.

people.name: Isaacius and Meletius, Bishops in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Isaacius, Apollo, and Codratus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Isaias the Solitary of Scetis and Palestine
people.summary: Fifth-century anchorite of Egypt and Palestine whose discourses in the Philokalia gave the East a classic of inner stillness.

people.name: Isaurus and Aphrodisius, companions of St. Longinus
people.summary: Two of the soldiers under Longinus at Golgotha who shared in his confession and in his crown.

people.name: Isaurus the Deacon and his companions
people.summary: A third-century deacon of Athens and seven companions slain together at Apollonia in Macedonia.

people.name: Isaurus the Deacon and his six companions
people.summary: A third-century deacon of Athens and his fellows tortured and beheaded at Apollonia in Macedonia.

people.name: Ischyrion and the Five Soldiers of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ischyrion, Bishop in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Isidora the Fool-for-Christ of Tabennisi
people.summary: A fourth-century maiden of Tabennisi in upper Egypt who concealed her ascetic holiness under the disguise of madness, revealed only at the visit of Abba Pitirim.

people.name: Isidore, Bishop of Antioch
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Antioch persecuted by the Arian party.

people.name: Isidore of Chios
people.summary: A Roman officer of the third century, betrayed as a Christian by his pagan father. Beheaded on Chios after refusing to sacrifice; his well still marks the place.

people.name: Isidore of Pelusium
people.summary: The fifth-century Egyptian ascetic and disciple of Chrysostom whose two thousand surviving letters of spiritual counsel show the desert's wisdom translated into the address of the world's needs.

people.name: Isidore the Presbyter of Scete
people.summary: A fourth-century priest of Scete in Egypt whose sayings stand in the apothegms of the desert fathers.

people.name: Isidore the Simple of Alexandria
people.summary: Fourth-century ascetic of Alexandria whose perfect simplicity Athanasius the Great praised in his life of Anthony.

people.name: Jacob, Abbot of Batheos Rhyakos Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jacob, Bishop of Nisibis
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nisibis on the Persian frontier called the Moses of Mesopotamia, a hesychast of the wilderness and a father of the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Jacob of Nisibis
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nisibis on the Persian frontier, a hesychast of the wilderness, father of Ephraim the Syrian, and present at the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Jacob the Ascetic
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jacob the Confessor of Catania
people.summary: Eighth-century bishop of Catania in Sicily who under Leo the Isaurian suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Jadorus and Isidore
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: James, son of Alphaeus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: James, son of Zebedee
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: James the Ascetic of Porphyreon
people.summary: A sixth-century hermit of Palestine who in penance after his fall from grace lived in a tomb until his death.

people.name: James the Ascetic of Syria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: James the Faster of Phoenicia
people.summary: A sixth-century Syrian ascetic whose fast and discipline are remembered in the Lausiac History.

people.name: James the Presbyter, Azadanes, and Abdicius of Persia
people.summary: A priest and two deacons of fourth-century Persia slain together under Sapor II.

people.name: James the Solitary of Syria
people.summary: A fifth-century recluse of Syria who lived shut up in a cave near Cyrrhus for fifteen years.

people.name: Januarius and Pelagia of Nicopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Januarius of Beneventum and his companions
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Beneventum in Italy under Diocletian who with six others was beheaded at Pozzuoli; the cooled blood preserved in a vial at Naples has been observed to liquefy at its annual exposition.

people.name: Jason and Sosipater of the Seventy
people.summary: Two kinsmen of Paul named in Romans 16, who together evangelized the island of Corfu; their preaching converted a number of brigands at the king's prison, and they reposed in peace after long labors.

people.name: Jason and Sosipater of the Seventy
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jason, Bishop of Damascus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jeremiah of Damascus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jesse (Ise) of Tsilkani in Georgia
people.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Tsilkani, one of the Thirteen Syrian Fathers who came up from Antioch into Iberia.

people.name: Dormition of Righteous Anna, mother of the Most Holy Theotokos
people.summary: The repose of the grandmother of the Lord, who with Joachim received the promise of the Virgin in their old age and so became the root from which the Mother of God grew.

people.name: Joanna the Myrrhbearer
people.summary: Wife of Chuza, Herod's steward, named in Luke's gospel and one of the myrrhbearers at the empty tomb.

people.name: Joannicius the Great of Bithynia
people.summary: A ninth-century soldier turned monk on Mount Olympus in Bithynia, defender of the holy icons against the second iconoclasm and prophet to many who came up the mountain seeking counsel.

people.name: Joasaph the Monk-Prince of India
people.summary: An Indian prince whose conversion through the elder Barlaam is recounted in John of Damascus's spiritual romance.

people.name: Righteous Job the Long-suffering
people.summary: The patriarch of Uz, who lost his children, his health, and all his goods in a single day and yet would not curse God. The Church reads from his book on Holy Friday, the day of patient suffering.

people.name: John and Gabriel of Iveron on Mount Athos
people.summary: Tenth-century Georgian co-founders of the Iveron monastery on Mount Athos with John Tornicius.

people.name: John and George, Patriarchs of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John and Tarasios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John, Andrew, Heraclemon, and Theophilus, Hermits of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John, Antioch, Antoninus, Moses, Zebinas, Polychronius, Moses, and Damian, Ascetics of the Syrian Desert
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John Barsanuphius of Nitria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John, Bishop of Gothia
people.summary: Eighth-century bishop of the Crimean Goths who from his exile suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: John, Bishop of Irenopolis
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Irenopolis in Cilicia, one of the three hundred and eighteen fathers at the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: John, Bishop of Karpathos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John, Bishop of Polybotum in Phrygia
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop and wonderworker of Phrygia who at the second iconoclast council under Constantine V refused to deny the icons.

people.name: John Cassian, Abbot of St. Victor in Marseille
people.summary: The fifth-century Latin abbot whose Institutes and Conferences brought the wisdom of the Egyptian desert into the western monastic tradition. The Church commemorates him only every fourth year, as the Lenten quiet of his life merits at long intervals a quiet feast.

people.name: John Cassian, Abbot of St. Victor in Marseille
people.summary: Fifth-century Latin abbot whose Institutes and Conferences brought the wisdom of the Egyptian desert into the western monastic tradition.

people.name: John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: The fifth-century preacher whose golden mouth shaped the Eastern liturgy that bears his name, twice exiled by an Arian empress for the faithfulness of his words; he reposed at Comana saying 'Glory to God for all things.'

people.name: John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John Climacus, author of The Ladder of Divine Ascent
people.summary: The seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose Ladder maps the soul's ascent to God in thirty steps — the work commemorated on the fourth Sunday of Great Lent and read in monasteries throughout the season.

people.name: John, disciple of St. Gregory the Decapolite
people.summary: A ninth-century disciple of Gregory of Decapolis at Constantinople, an unyielding confessor of the icons under the second iconoclasm, who reposed in peace at the monastery his teacher had founded.

people.name: John, Disciple of Gregory of Decapolis
people.summary: A ninth-century monastic of Constantinople, unyielding confessor of the holy icons.

people.name: John, Heraclemon, Andrew, and Theophilus of Oxyrhynchus
people.summary: Four desert hermits of Oxyrhynchus in Egypt whose long life of stillness was discovered to the world only at their repose.

people.name: John II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John IV and John V Chrysostom, Catholicoi of Georgia
people.summary: Two eleventh-century catholicoi of the Georgian church who guided their nation through troubled times.

people.name: John IV the Faster, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: The strict ascetic-patriarch of Constantinople from 582 to 595, the first to bear the title Ecumenical, who reposed leaving only a wooden spoon.

people.name: John Lampadistes of Cyprus
people.summary: A tenth-century Cypriot ascetic blinded by his unfaithful betrothed's family who reposed in peace at the age of twenty-two.

people.name: John Mavropous, Metropolitan of Euchaita
people.summary: Eleventh-century hymnographer and metropolitan of Euchaita whose vision joined the Three Hierarchs in their common feast.

people.name: John (Maximovitch) of Tobolsk
people.summary: Eighteenth-century metropolitan of Tobolsk and Siberia, missionary among the Siberian peoples, kinsman of John Maximovitch of Shanghai and San Francisco.

people.name: John Moschos
people.summary: Seventh-century monk of Palestine whose Spiritual Meadow gathers the stories of the Eastern desert fathers.

people.name: John of the Ancient Caves
people.summary: A monk of the lavra of St. Chariton in Palestine in the eighth century, called of the Ancient Caves because the place he settled had been used by hermits before him going back to the third century.

people.name: John of Cathares
people.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the Cathares monastery at Constantinople, confessor of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: Barbara
people.summary: A noble maiden of third-century Heliopolis whose pagan father, finding she had received baptism while he was away, denounced her himself and beheaded her with his own hand — and was struck dead by lightning as he came down the mountain.

people.name: John of Egypt, the Anchorite
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt renowned for his gift of clairvoyance.

people.name: John of Gothia
people.summary: Eighth-century bishop of the Crimean Goths who from his exile suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: John of Kronstadt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John of Lycopolis
people.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Thebaid renowned for his gift of prophecy.

people.name: John of Merv
people.summary: An early-eleventh-century Christian of Turkmenistan slain by Muslims for refusing to deny Christ.

people.name: John of Patalaria, Confessor
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John of Rila, Founder of Rila Monastery
people.summary: Tenth-century hermit of Bulgaria and founder of the great monastery of Rila that became the spiritual heart of the Bulgarian nation.

people.name: John of Zedazeni and the Thirteen Holy Syrian Fathers
people.summary: Sixth-century leader of the Syrian fathers who came up from Antioch into Iberia to plant and nurture Georgian monasticism.

people.name: John, Presbyter in Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John Scholasticus, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Constantinople and canonist whose Synagoge of canons shaped the church's law.

people.name: John Talaia, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Bishop and James the Presbyter of Persia
people.summary: A bishop and his priest in fourth-century Persia put to death together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

people.name: John the Cappadocian, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Chozebite, Bishop of Caesarea
people.summary: A sixth-century desert father of the wadi Chozeba in Palestine, made bishop of Caesarea against his will.

people.name: John the Clairvoyant of Lycopolis
people.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of upper Egypt to whom Theodosius the Great and many other rulers sent for prophetic counsel.

people.name: John the Confessor and the forty companions with him
people.summary: A confessor of Egypt or Palestine in the late third century beheaded together with forty of his fellows.

people.name: John the Confessor, Metropolitan of Chalcedon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Confessor of Psichaita
people.summary: Ninth-century monk of Constantinople who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: John the Confessor of Psychaita
people.summary: Ninth-century monk of Constantinople, confessor of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: John the Exarch of Bulgaria
people.summary: Tenth-century Slavic theologian at the court of King Symeon of Bulgaria, translator of John of Damascus into Slavonic.

people.name: Third Finding of the Honorable Head of John the Baptist
people.summary: The relic of the Forerunner's head, hidden again during the iconoclast turmoil of the ninth century, was rediscovered in Comana around 850 and translated to Constantinople.
people.feastDayLabel: May 25; June 24; August 29; September 23; January 7; February 24

people.name: John the God-bearer of Crete
people.summary: An eleventh-century desert monk of Crete who reposed in a cave at Cape Akrotiri.

people.name: John the Harvester (Theristus) of Calabria
people.summary: A ninth-century Sicilian who escaped Saracen captivity to live as a monk in Calabria, where the wheat-fields are said to have been gathered for his community by his prayer.

people.name: John the Hermit of Cilicia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Hut-dweller
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Merciful, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: John, the seventh-century patriarch whose alms-giving emptied the treasuries of his see and filled the homes of the poor. Also commemorated: Nilus, the fifth-century desert father of Sinai whose letters are read among the gathered fathers.

people.name: John the Russian, Confessor of Euboea
people.summary: An eighteenth-century Russian soldier taken captive by the Turks and made a slave at Procopion, who endured years of mockery and witness rather than embrace Islam.

people.name: John the Short (Kolobos) of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Scetis whose master's barren stick blossomed at his obedient watering — the apothegm of monastic patience.

people.name: John the Silent, Bishop of Colonia
people.summary: A sixth-century bishop of Colonia in Armenia who at twenty-eight forsook his see for the silence of Mar Saba and lived seventy-six years there.

people.name: John the Soldier
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Soldier of Tralles
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Solitary of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John the Theologian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John (Tornicus) of Iveron
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: John Vladimir, King of Serbia, Martyr and Wonderworker
people.summary: Eleventh-century prince of the Serbs slain by his cousin and afterwards honored at Elbasan as a wonderworker of the Balkan church.

people.name: Jonah
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jonah and Barachisius of Persia, with eight companions
people.summary: Two brothers of fourth-century Persia and the companions who confessed Christ with them under Sapor II.

people.name: Jonah the Sabbaite
people.summary: Ninth-century presbyter of Mar Saba in Palestine, father of the hymnographers Theodore and Theophanes the Branded.

people.name: Jonah the Wonderworker, Archbishop of Novgorod
people.summary: Fifteenth-century archbishop of Novgorod whose prayers were credited with delivering the city from famine and his see from interference.

people.name: Jonas and Barachisius of Persia, with nine companions
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Jonilla and her infant son Turbo
people.summary: A young mother and her child of second-century Cappadocia, crowned together in the persecution under Marcus Aurelius.

people.name: Jordan the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Joseph, Abbot of Alaverdi
people.summary: A sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen Holy Fathers who came from Antioch into Iberia.

people.name: Joseph Analytinus of Raithu
people.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of the Sinai wilderness whose nights of prayer were attended by a heavenly light.

people.name: Joseph of Scetis
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Joseph the Confessor, Archbishop of Thessalonica
people.summary: Ninth-century brother of Theodore the Studite, exiled for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Joseph the Hymnographer
people.summary: The ninth-century monk of Sicily, exiled to Constantinople by the Arab conquest, whose hymns are appointed in every canon of the Octoechos and whose canons fill the Menaion through every saint's day.

people.name: Joseph the Hymnographer of Sicily
people.summary: Ninth-century Sicilian monk in Constantinople whose canons fill the Menaion and Octoechos.

people.name: Jude, brother of the Lord
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian and Basilissa of Antinoe, with their household
people.summary: A young couple of fourth-century Egypt who by mutual vow lived as brother and sister and turned their house into a refuge before all were crowned together.

people.name: Julian and his disciple Eunos of Alexandria
people.summary: An aged Christian of third-century Alexandria, so weakened with gout that he had to be carried into court, and his disciple Eunos who shared in his crown.

people.name: Julian, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Alexandria, eleventh in succession from the apostle Mark.

people.name: Julian, dragged through thorns
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian of Anazarbus
people.summary: A young Christian of fourth-century Cilicia sewn into a sack with serpents and cast into the sea, whose tomb at Antioch Chrysostom honored in homily.

people.name: Julian of Antinoe with his wife Basilissa and companions
people.summary: A young couple of Egypt who at their wedding vowed mutual chastity, opened their house as a refuge for the persecuted, and were crowned together under Diocletian.

people.name: Julian of Dagaz of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian of Dalmatia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian of Galatia and forty companions
people.summary: A Galatian Christian beheaded with forty of his fellows in the persecution under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Julian of Heliopolis in Syria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian of Syria
people.summary: Fourth-century ascetic of the Syrian wilderness near Cyrrhus whose discipline Theodoret of Cyrus admired.

people.name: Julian of Tarsus
people.summary: A young noble of Cilicia under Diocletian who, after long imprisonment and many torments, was sewn into a sack with vipers and cast into the sea.

people.name: Julian the Deacon of Aegina
people.summary: Fourth-century deacon ordained at Athens whose preaching baptized many before he withdrew to a hermitage near Lake Maggiore in Italy.

people.name: Julian the Hermit
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Mesopotamia who lived in the wilderness near the Euphrates.

people.name: Julian the Physician of Emesa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julian the Presbyter and Caesarius the Deacon of Terracina
people.summary: A priest and a deacon of first-century Italy slain at Terracina on the Tyrrhenian coast.

people.name: Juliana and her son Saturninus of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Juliana of Nicomedia
people.summary: A noble maiden of fourth-century Nicomedia who refused the betrothal her pagan father had arranged, endured an interview with the demon in her prison cell, and was beheaded under Maximian.

people.name: Juliana of Strobilos and Leo of Myra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julitta (Julia) of Tabennisi
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julitta of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julius of Aegina, the Missionary Priest
people.summary: An early-fifth-century priest of Aegina who with his brother Julian labored in evangelism through northern Italy.

people.name: Julius of Dorostolum
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Roman soldier of Moesia beheaded under Diocletian for his Christian confession.

people.name: Julius the Presbyter of Novara
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Julius the Veteran of Dorostolum
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Junia, fellow laborer of Andronicus
people.summary: An apostolic-age fellow-worker of the apostle Paul named together with Andronicus in the Letter to the Romans.

people.name: Justa the Nun
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Justin Popović of Ćelije
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Justin Popović of Ćelije, Archimandrite
people.summary: Twentieth-century Serbian theologian and elder of the monastery of Ćelije whose dogmatic theology gathered the witness of the Fathers for a new age.

people.name: Justin the Philosopher
people.summary: The second-century apologist who, finding Christ to be the answer his philosophy had been seeking, opened a school of Christian thought in Rome and gave his life under Marcus Aurelius for the truth he had come to love.

people.name: Justinian I, Emperor of New Rome, and Saint Theodora the Empress
people.summary: The sixth-century emperor whose codification of the law and great churches — above all the Holy Wisdom — shaped the Christian Roman commonwealth, and the empress who stood beside him.

people.name: Justus and Matthew of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Justus, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Justus the Soldier of Rome
people.summary: A first-century Roman soldier converted at the sight of a vision of the Cross and crowned for the name of Christ.

people.name: Juvenal, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Jerusalem at the Council of Chalcedon, under whom the see was raised to the dignity of a patriarchate.

people.name: Juventinus and Maximinus of Antioch
people.summary: Two soldiers in the imperial guard at Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate for refusing to eat what had been offered to idols.

people.name: Kalliniki and Vasilissa of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Karion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Karion and his son Zachariah of Egypt
people.summary: A father and son of fourth-century Egypt who together took up the desert life at Scetis after Karion's wife had refused to give up the world.

people.name: Kassiani the Hymnographer
people.summary: A ninth-century noblewoman of Constantinople who, refused the imperial throne by Theophilus, entered a convent and composed many of the Church's hymns.

people.name: King and Prophet David
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Korilla (Cyrilla)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Kosmas the Hermit
people.summary: A seventh-century Cretan ascetic confessor remembered for his solitude and life of prayer.

people.name: Kyra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Kyriake of Nicomedia
people.summary: A maiden born to pious parents on the Lord's Day (whence her name), who under Diocletian withstood fire, beasts, and the sword, and gave up her soul in peace at the moment of her execution.

people.name: Kyriakos and Apollinarios of Africa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Kyriakos (Cyriacus), Wonderworker of Evrychou in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Kyriakos II, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: A seventh-century patriarch of Constantinople under whom the Persian invasion of the empire began.

people.name: Kyriakos of Mar Saba
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Kyriakos the Executioner
people.summary: An executioner of Antioch who at the witness of the martyr Antiochus confessed Christ on the spot and was beheaded with the very sword by which he had served.

people.name: Lampadus of Irenopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Laodicius, Jailer of Glyceria
people.summary: The prison-keeper at Trajanopolis converted by Glyceria's patience under torture and beheaded with her.

people.name: Laurence the Monk of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Lawrence of the Arena
people.summary: A Christian slain by the pagans in the arena for refusing the sacrifices.

people.name: and Archdeacon Lawrence of Rome
people.summary: The archdeacon of Pope Sixtus II at Rome under Valerian; entrusted with the goods of the Church, he distributed them to the poor and presented those poor to the prefect as the true treasure of the Church, for which he was roasted alive on a gridiron.

people.name: Lazarus the Four-Days-Dead
people.summary: Bishop of Kition on Cyprus, friend of the Lord.

people.name: Lazarus the Iconographer of Constantinople
people.summary: A ninth-century monk-iconographer whose hands were burned by the iconoclast emperor and who at the triumph painted again the icons of the Lord and the Theotokos.

people.name: Lazarus the Wonderworker of Mount Galesion
people.summary: An eleventh-century stylite who built three monasteries on Mount Galesion near Ephesus and lived sixty years on a pillar.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Annunciation
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Nativity of Christ
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leavetaking of the Nativity of the Theotokos
people.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast that began on the eighth of September.

people.name: Leo and Parigorius of Patara
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leo I, Emperor of New Rome
people.summary: Fifth-century Christian Roman emperor in whose reign the great churches of the Theotokos were built at Blachernae and the Source.

people.name: Leo of Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leo, Bishop of Catania in Sicily
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Catania remembered for his miracle of binding the sorcerer Heliodorus, whom he led to his own execution in the flames at the bath-house and was preserved untouched.

people.name: Leo of Mandra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leo of Samos, the Wonderworker
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Samos renowned for the gift of healing.

people.name: Leo the Archimandrite
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leo the Great, Pope of Rome
people.summary: The fifth-century bishop of Rome whose Tome to Flavian became the dogmatic foundation of the Fourth Ecumenical Council at Chalcedon: two natures, perfect God and perfect man, in one Christ.

people.name: Leo the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leonidas, Bishop of Athens
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Athens slain together with eleven companions for the name of Christ.

people.name: Leonidas of Nea Epidavros and his companions
people.summary: A third-century Christian of the Peloponnese and his fellows slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Leonides, Bishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leonides of Egypt
people.summary: Father of the theologian Origen, beheaded at Alexandria under Septimius Severus.

people.name: Leontius, Dometius, Terence, and Domninus
people.summary: Four early martyrs given up together to the fire for the name of Christ.

people.name: Leontius
people.summary: A Greek soldier of Tripoli in Phoenicia under Vespasian who, with two others he had brought to Christ, was tortured and beheaded for refusing the imperial sacrifice.

people.name: Leontius the Governor of Terracina
people.summary: The Roman governor of Terracina converted by the witness of Julian and Caesarius, who shared in their martyrdom.

people.name: Leontius the Shepherd
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Leucius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Liberius the Confessor, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Living-martyr Eubotius of Cyzicus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Lolianus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Longinus of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Cilicia and Egypt, abbot of Enaton near Alexandria, whose sayings stand in the apothegms.

people.name: Longinus the Centurion
people.summary: The Roman officer who stood at the foot of the Cross and confessed 'truly this was the Son of God.' He was beheaded under Tiberius after preaching in his native Cappadocia.

people.name: Lot and Rufus of Egypt
people.summary: Two desert fathers of fifth-century Egypt remembered together in the sayings of the elders.

people.name: Love
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Lucian, Bishop of Beauvais, with Maxianus the Presbyter and Julian the Deacon
people.summary: First-century missionary bishop of Gaul and his companions, slain together near the village that later took his name.

people.name: Lucian, Presbyter of Antioch
people.summary: Early-fourth-century priest of Antioch, founder of the catechetical school there, who confessed Christ before Maximinus Daia and died in prison after long fasting.

people.name: Lucillian and those with him
people.summary: A converted pagan priest of Byzantium who, with the four young men he had baptized, was crucified for refusing to deny Christ under Aurelian.

people.name: Lucius of Adrianople
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Adrianople who suffered long imprisonment and at length reposed in chains under the Arian Constantius.

people.name: Lucius the Senator of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Lucy and her son Geminian of Rome
people.summary: A widow of Rome and her son beheaded together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Ludmilla of Bohemia
people.summary: Tenth-century grandmother of Wenceslaus of Bohemia, strangled at her prayers by her pagan daughter-in-law's assassins.

people.name: Luke, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
people.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the Bithynian monastery near Triglia.

people.name: Luke of Demena
people.summary: Tenth-century Sicilian abbot who passed his last years founding monasteries in southern Italy.

people.name: Luke of Lycaonia, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
people.summary: Third abbot of the same Bithynian monastery, who reposed after the year 975.

people.name: Luke of Tauromenium in Sicily
people.summary: A ninth-century monk and bishop of Sicily, founder of a monastery on the slopes of Etna.

people.name: Luke the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Luke the New Stylite of Chalcedon
people.summary: A tenth-century soldier turned stylite at Chalcedon, who in his lifetime came down only once from his pillar.

people.name: Luke the Soldier
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Luke the Younger of Mount Steirion
people.summary: Tenth-century founder of the great monastery of Hosios Loukas in central Greece, renowned for his miracles and the gift of clairvoyance.

people.name: Lupus of Novae
people.summary: Third-century slave of St. Demetrius of Thessaloniki whose master gave him his tunic dipped in the saint's blood; he himself was at length beheaded for the same confession.

people.name: Lupus, slave of St. Demetrius
people.summary: The faithful servant of Demetrius at Thessalonica who, having dipped his master's tunic in the saint's blood and worked many wonders with it, was himself beheaded for the same confession.

people.name: Lycarion of Hermopolis in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Lydia of Thyatira, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: The dealer in purple at Philippi who as the first European convert was baptized at the apostle Paul's preaching.

people.name: Macarius, Abbot of Pelekete in Bithynia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Macarius, Bishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Jerusalem in whose time and at whose direction Helena uncovered the True Cross and Constantine built the basilica of the Resurrection.

people.name: Macarius of Petra
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Macarius, Rufinus, Justus, and Theophilus of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Macarius the Confessor of Pelekete
people.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelekete near Prusa, exiled for the holy icons under Leo V the Armenian.

people.name: Macarius the Faster of Khakhuli
people.summary: Eleventh-century abbot of the Khakhuli monastery in Georgia, renowned for the severity of his fasting.

people.name: Macarius the Great of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Scetis, disciple of Anthony, whose Fifty Spiritual Homilies opened to the East the inner life of unceasing prayer — the lamp of the hesychast tradition.

people.name: Macarius the Great of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Macarius the Roman of Mesopotamia
people.summary: An ascetic of the eastern wilderness commemorated for his life of hesychast prayer beside the river of Mesopotamia.

people.name: Macarius the Younger of Alexandria
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of the Cells of Nitria, disciple of Anthony and Macarius the Great.

people.name: Macedonius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Macedonius II, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Constantinople, defender of the Council of Chalcedon, exiled by Anastasius the emperor.

people.name: Macedonius of Antioch
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Mount Silpius near Antioch whose long fast sustained him on barley meal mixed with water.

people.name: Macedonius, Tatian, and Theodulus of Phrygia
people.summary: Three Phrygian Christians who under Julian the Apostate were laid on burning gridirons for their refusal of the sacrifices.

people.name: Macrina the Elder
people.summary: Grandmother of Basil the Great, who from her youth had been a disciple of Gregory the Wonderworker and raised her grandchildren in the faith.

people.name: Macrina, sister of St. Basil the Great
people.summary: The eldest sister and spiritual teacher of the great Cappadocian fathers, whose life under her mother Emmelia at Annisi shaped the household from which Basil, Gregory of Nyssa, and Peter of Sebaste came forth.

people.name: Magnus and Moses the Anchorite
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Magus of Comana Pontica
people.summary: The executioner converted at the spectacle of Hermias's endurance and crowned with him.

people.name: Maharsapor of Persia
people.summary: A fifth-century Persian Christian imprisoned three years for his confession and at length starved to death in his cell.

people.name: Mairus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Major of Gaza
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Malchion the Presbyter of Antioch
people.summary: A learned priest of Antioch in the third century whose disputation refuted the heresies of Paul of Samosata at the great synod.

people.name: Malchus of Chalcis in Syria
people.summary: A fifth-century monk of Syria captured by the Saracens, freed by miracle, and at length restored to his solitude.

people.name: Malus the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mamai, Catholicos of Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mamas (commemoration near Sigma)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mamas, drowned
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mamas of Caesarea
people.summary: An orphaned shepherd-boy of Cappadocia in the third century whose lambs and wild animals lived together at his feet; under Aurelian he was given to the beasts and finally pierced through with a trident.

people.name: Mamas, Pachomius, and Hilarion the Monks
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mamelta of Persia
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian woman converted by a vision while she still served the fire-temple, stoned by the priests who had been her companions.

people.name: Manahen the Prophet and Teacher of Antioch
people.summary: First-century prophet of the church at Antioch named in Acts 13, foster-brother of Herod the tetrarch.

people.name: Manuel and Theodosius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Manuel, Sabel, and Ishmael
people.summary: Three brothers of Persian noble birth, ambassadors to Constantinople, who under Julian the Apostate refused to share in his pagan sacrifices and were beheaded.

people.name: Marcellinus, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Early-fourth-century bishop of Rome martyred under Diocletian together with three of his companions.

people.name: Marcellus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marcellus, Abbot of the Monastery of the Unsleeping Ones
people.summary: A fifth-century abbot of the great akoimetai monastery near Constantinople, whose monks kept the round of psalmody unbroken day and night.

people.name: Marcellus, Bishop of Aipeia in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marcellus, Bishop of Apamea in Syria
people.summary: A late-fourth-century bishop of Syria slain by pagans after he had destroyed the great temple of Jupiter at Aulon.

people.name: Marcellus, Philagrius, and Pancratius
people.summary: Three first-century disciples of the apostle Peter who labored as bishops in Sicily, Cyprus, and Taormina.

people.name: Marcian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marcian and Martyrius the Notaries
people.summary: Two notaries of the Patriarch Paul of Constantinople under the Arians who, refusing to surrender the records of the Nicene Council, were beheaded for the truth.

people.name: Marcian, Bishop of Syracuse
people.summary: A first-century bishop of Syracuse said to have been consecrated by the apostle Peter himself, slain in the very early centuries of the Sicilian church.

people.name: Marcian, Emperor of New Rome, and Pulcheria the Empress
people.summary: The fifth-century Christian Roman emperor and his consort, under whose patronage the Fourth Ecumenical Council was gathered at Chalcedon.

people.name: Marcian of Cyrrhus
people.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Syrian wilderness near Cyrrhus whose silent austerity drew many to seek him out.

people.name: Marcian of Iconium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marcian the Presbyter and Economos of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century priest and economos of the Great Church whose almsgiving and care for the orphans made him a friend of the empress Pulcheria.

people.name: Marciana (Euphemia), Empress of New Rome
people.summary: Wife of Justin I in the sixth century, who lies buried with her husband in the Church of the Holy Apostles at Constantinople.

people.name: Mardarius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mares the Singer of Cyrrhus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maria of Perge
people.summary: An early Christian of Pamphylia honored by the Church for her witness.

people.name: Maria of Tarsus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mariamne, sister of the apostle Philip
people.summary: Sister of the apostle Philip who labored with him and Bartholomew in the evangelization of Hierapolis and afterwards of Lycaonia, where she reposed in peace.

people.name: Marina the Virgin of Bithynia
people.summary: An eighth-century maiden who in the disguise of her dying father took the monastic habit as a male monk and lived hidden the rest of her days.

people.name: Marinus, Martha, Audifax, and Abbacum of Rome
people.summary: A Persian noble family of third-century Rome with their two sons, all beheaded together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Marinus of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: A third-century Roman soldier of Caesarea Maritima at the rank of centurion, beheaded for his confession when his pagan rival exposed him.

people.name: Marinus of Rome
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marinus, son of St. Mary the New of Byzia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Marinus the Soldier and Asterius the Senator of Caesarea
people.summary: A Roman centurion of Caesarea in Palestine and the senator who buried him after his beheading; both crowned together.

people.name: Marios (Marius), Bishop of Sebasteia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mark
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mark and Mocius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mark, Aristarchus, and Zenas of the Seventy
people.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Mark (also called John) of Bibloupolis in Phoenicia, Aristarchus of Thessalonica, and Zenas the lawyer of Diospolis.

people.name: Mark of Apollonias
people.summary: First-century nephew of the apostle Barnabas, who labored as bishop of Apollonias in Lycia.

people.name: Mark of Thasos and his companions
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of the island of Thasos crowned with a small band of fellow believers.

people.name: Mark of Triglia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mark the Anchorite of Athens
people.summary: A fifth-century recluse of the Libyan mountain whose long solitude was found out only at his death by Abba Serapion.

people.name: Conon the Gardener of Isauria
people.summary: A simple gardener of Isauria under Decius, baptized by Michael the Archangel and known for his quiet boldness; he was burned alive in a fire he calmly walked into himself.

people.name: Mark the Hermit
people.summary: Fifth-century ascetic of the Egyptian desert whose hundred and twenty chapters On the Spiritual Law are in the Philokalia.

people.name: Markellos, Dasius, and Maron
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maron of Cyrrhus
people.summary: Fifth-century hermit of Syria whose disciples gave rise to the Maronite tradition.

people.name: Martha of Cappadocia
people.summary: Mother of Symeon the Stylite, who reposed in peace at Antioch in 428.

people.name: Martha of the Wonderful Mountain
people.summary: Sixth-century mother of Symeon Stylites the Younger, whose holy life and prayers are inseparable from his.

people.name: Martin and Heraclius of Illyria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Martin of the Thebaid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Martin the Confessor, Pope of Rome
people.summary: The seventh-century bishop of Rome who at the Lateran council condemned the heresy of monothelitism and was for this seized by imperial troops, brought to Constantinople, tried, and exiled to Cherson where he died of starvation.

people.name: Martin the Confessor, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Rome who at the Lateran council condemned monothelitism, was abducted by imperial troops, and reposed in exile at Cherson.

people.name: Martin the Merciful, Bishop of Tours
people.summary: Fourth-century soldier-monk of Gaul who divided his cloak with a beggar at Amiens and went on to found Western monasticism at Marmoutier.

people.name: Martinianus the Hermit
people.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Caesarea in Palestine who burned himself slightly to drive out the temptation a wandering woman had set in him, and fled to a deserted island where he reposed in peace.

people.name: Martyr-abbot John of Zedazeni
people.summary: A ninth-century abbot of the Zedazeni monastery in Georgia put to death together with his community by Saracen invaders.

people.name: Irene of Macedonia
people.summary: Born to a pagan king and called from idols to Christ in girlhood; her courage under torture brought thousands to faith. Her feast is kept with particular joy in the Greek tradition.

people.name: Marina of Antioch in Pisidia
people.summary: A fifteen-year-old who under Diocletian endured monstrous torments and visions of the demon in serpent form, defeating him by the sign of the Cross before being beheaded — the icon of victory over the powers.

people.name: Martyr-queen Shushanik of Georgia
people.summary: Fifth-century princess of Armenia and queen of the prince Varsken of Georgia, beaten and imprisoned by him for refusing to renounce Christ when he embraced Zoroastrianism.

people.name: Martyr-reader Danax of Avlona
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sebastian and his companions at Rome
people.summary: A captain of the Praetorian Guard under Diocletian who secretly strengthened the imprisoned Christians, was discovered, shot through with arrows, and finally clubbed to death — the soldier-saint of the Western tradition.

people.name: Martyrios the Bishop
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Martyrius, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Martyrocles
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Terence, Pompeius, Africanus, Maximus, Zenon, Alexander, and Theodore of Africa
people.summary: Seven Christians of Carthage in the third century under Decius who, refusing in turn each form of induced sacrifice, were beheaded together in the city's amphitheater.

people.name: Maruthas of Martyropolis
people.summary: Late-fourth- and early-fifth-century bishop of Sophene whose city of Martyropolis was named for the bones of the Persian martyrs he gathered for honorable burial.

people.name: Mary and Martha, sisters of Lazarus
people.summary: The sisters of Lazarus of Bethany who served the Lord in their home and received him at the raising of their brother.

people.name: Myrrh-bearer and Equal-to-the-Apostles Mary Magdalene
people.summary: She from whom the Lord cast out seven demons and who stood at the Cross, came first to the empty tomb, and brought the Resurrection news to the apostles. She is the first to whom the risen Christ appeared.

people.name: Mary (Marinus) and her father Eugene of Alexandria
people.summary: A sixth-century maiden of Egypt who at the death of her mother took her father's name and lived as a monk Marinus in his monastery beside him.

people.name: Mary of Egypt
people.summary: The fifth-century desert ascetic whose long penitence in the wilderness beyond the Jordan is the icon of Lenten transformation; she lived forty-seven years unseen until the priest Zosimas found her, gave her communion, and buried her.

people.name: Mary of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mary of Jerusalem, mother of Mark the Evangelist
people.summary: The first-century Christian of Jerusalem whose house was the gathering place of the disciples after Pentecost, where Peter came after his deliverance from prison.

people.name: Mary the New of Byzia in Thrace
people.summary: A ninth-century laywoman of Thrace whose patient suffering of her husband's cruelty is honored by the Church.

people.name: Mastridia of the Jerusalem Desert
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Matrona of Chios
people.summary: Fifteenth-century maiden of the island of Chios who lived hidden in a hollow olive tree and at length founded a community where her tomb is honored to this day.

people.name: Matrona of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century ascetic who lived hidden in a male monastery to escape her pagan husband.

people.name: Matrona of Thessalonica
people.summary: A Christian slave girl of Thessalonica under Maximian whose Jewish mistress, finding her at prayer in secret, beat her to death over the course of three days.

people.name: Matronian and Anthony of Melitene
people.summary: Brothers of the martyr Hieron who shared in his confession at Melitene under Diocletian.

people.name: Matthew
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Matthias
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maura of Constantinople
people.summary: A fifth-century holy woman of Constantinople who reposed in peace under Theodosius the Younger.

people.name: Maurice, Emperor of New Rome, and his six sons
people.summary: The Roman emperor (582–602) and his six sons, slain together by the usurper Phocas, whose acceptance of death rather than betray his people is honored by the Church.

people.name: Maurice, Photinus, and the seventy soldiers of Apamea
people.summary: A Christian officer of fourth-century Apamea in Bithynia and seventy of his men, given over together to wild beasts under Maximian.

people.name: Maurice, Photinus, Theodore, and Philip of Apamea, with seventy soldiers
people.summary: A father and son and a company of soldiers of Apamea in Syria, all crowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Maurice the Soldier
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mausimas the Syrian Presbyter of Cyrrhus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximian, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, raised to the see in place of Nestorius after his deposition.

people.name: Maximianus, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, successor of Nestorius after the Third Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Maximianus, Bishop of Ravenna
people.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Ravenna whose imperial patronage built the great basilica of San Vitale.

people.name: Maximos and Dometios, monks of Nitria
people.summary: Two fourth-century princes — sons of Valentinian — who slipped from the court to take up the desert life in the cells of Macarius.

people.name: Maximus and Olympiada of Persia
people.summary: A father and son of Persia slain together under Decius for the name of Christ.

people.name: Maximus, Bassus, and Fabius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus III, Archbishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus III, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus of Ephesus
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Ephesus crowned under Decius for his confession.

people.name: Maximus of Rome
people.summary: A third-century Christian of Rome slain under Decius for the name of Christ.

people.name: Maximus, Terentius, and Pompeius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus the Confessor
people.summary: The seventh-century monk and theologian whose long defense of the two wills of Christ against the monothelites cost him his tongue, his right hand, and finally his life in exile in Lazica.

people.name: Maximus the Confessor, Pope of Alexandria
people.summary: Third-century patriarch of Alexandria who took part in the synod that deposed Paul of Samosata for the heresy of dynamic monarchianism.

people.name: Maximus the Greek
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus, Theodotus, and Asclepiodota of Adrianopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Maximus, Theodotus, Hesychius, and Asclepiodota of Adrianopolis
people.summary: Four believers of Adrianople in Thrace slain together under Diocletian.

people.name: Medula and her companions
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Melania the Elder
people.summary: Fourth- and early-fifth-century Roman noblewoman who at her widowhood went to the East, joined Rufinus at the Mount of Olives, and ended her days in the desert.

people.name: Melania the Younger of Rome
people.summary: A fifth-century noble of Rome who with her husband Pinianus sold their immense estates to ransom captives and endow monasteries, ending her days in a cell on the Mount of Olives.

people.name: Melasippus, Cassina, and their son Antoninus of Ancyra
people.summary: A father, mother, and twelve-year-old son slain together at Ancyra under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Meletius, Bishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Meletius, Archbishop of Antioch
people.summary: Meletius of Antioch (4th c.), who at his very enthronement preached against the Arians and was sent into three exiles. Alexis of Moscow (14th c.), bishop and statesman, healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian church together in dark days.

people.name: Meletius Stratelates and the 1,218 soldiers, women, and children with him
people.summary: A second-century Roman officer of Galatia and a great company of his men, with their wives and children, all crowned together under Antoninus.

people.name: Melitene of Marcianopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Melito, Bishop of Sardis
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Sardis whose apology to Marcus Aurelius and homily On Pascha shaped early Christian thought.

people.name: Meliton
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Meliton of Beirut
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Memnon, Archbishop of Ephesus
people.summary: A fifth-century archbishop of Ephesus who presided over the Third Ecumenical Council in his city in 431.

people.name: Memnon the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Menander, Sabinus, and the forty companions of Hermopolis
people.summary: Forty Christians of fourth-century Egypt slain together under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Menas, Faustus, Andrew, and Heraclius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Menas, Hermogenes, and Eugraphus
people.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Alexandria: Menas sent by Maximian to silence the Christians, who instead joined them; Hermogenes, the governor who came to judge him, likewise converted; Eugraphus, Menas's secretary. All three were beheaded together.

people.name: Menas of Egypt
people.summary: An officer of the Roman army under Diocletian who left his command for the wilderness, returned openly to the city to confess Christ, and was beheaded at Cotyaeum in Phrygia — his shrine in Egypt became famed for healings.

people.name: Menas of Mount Sinai
people.summary: A sixth-century monk of Sinai whose body was found incorrupt years after his repose.

people.name: Menas, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Constantinople who under Justinian I confirmed the Fifth Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Menignos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Menignus the Tanner of Parium
people.summary: A tanner of third-century Parium beheaded under Decius for refusing to surrender his Christian books.

people.name: Menodora, Metrodora, and Nymphodora
people.summary: Three virgin sisters of Bithynia of the early fourth century, ascetics in the wilderness who were brought before Frontonus and one by one tortured to death for their virginity and confession.

people.name: Mercurius of Caesarea
people.summary: A third-century Christian officer of Caesarea in Cappadocia beheaded under Decius and afterward sent in a vision by Basil the Great to slay Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Mertius of Mauretania
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mesrop Mashtots, Enlightener of Armenia
people.summary: Fifth-century Armenian doctor who with Sahak the Catholicos devised the Armenian alphabet and translated the Scriptures into the language of his nation.

people.name: Mesrop Mashtots the Translator of Armenia
people.summary: Fifth-century Armenian doctor who devised the Armenian alphabet and translated the Scriptures into the language of his nation.

people.name: Methodius, Archbishop of Moravia
people.summary: Ninth-century brother of Cyril, archbishop of Moravia, who together with him gave the Slavs their alphabet and the liturgy in their tongue.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Cyril and Methodius
people.summary: Brothers from Thessalonica who in the ninth century brought the Gospel and the liturgy to the Slavic peoples, devising the alphabet that bears their name and translating the Scriptures into Slavonic.

people.name: Methodius the Confessor, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Ninth-century patriarch of Constantinople who at the Triumph of Orthodoxy in 843 restored the holy icons to the Church.

people.name: Metrius the Farmer of Myra in Lycia
people.summary: An early-tenth-century farmer of Lycia whose hidden almsgiving was revealed to his neighbors only when an angel announced the birth of his son in old age.

people.name: Metrophanes, first Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: The fourth-century hierarch who from his deathbed received word of the great victory of the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, and reposed soon after in peace.

people.name: Meuris and Thea of Palestine
people.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Palestine slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Michael and Arsenius of Ulompo in Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Michael I, first Metropolitan of Kiev
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Michael Maleinus
people.summary: Tenth-century elder of Mount Kyminas in Bithynia, spiritual father of Athanasius of Mount Athos.

people.name: Michael of Parekhi in Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Michael of the Lavra of St. Sabbas, nephew of Theodore the Sabbaite
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Michael Synkellos the Confessor
people.summary: Ninth-century synkellos of the patriarchate of Jerusalem who at Constantinople suffered with the brothers Theodore and Theophanes for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Michael the Ascetic of Chalcedon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Michael the Black-Robed of Mar Saba
people.summary: Ninth-century monk of the Great Lavra slain by Saracens for his confession of Christ.

people.name: Michael the Confessor, Bishop of Synnada
people.summary: A ninth-century Phrygian hierarch who suffered exile and harsh confinement for defending the veneration of icons under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: Milles the Melodist
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Milos the Wonderworker, Bishop in Persia, with the disciples Euores and Seboes
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop and two of his disciples crowned together under Sapor II for the cause of Christ.

people.name: Miltiades, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Early-fourth-century bishop of Rome under whose pontificate Constantine issued the Edict of Milan.

people.name: Mirax of Egypt
people.summary: A seventh-century Christian of Egypt who in a moment of weakness had embraced Islam, then was reconciled by repentance and crowned for his returning confession.

people.name: Mirdat, King of Kartli
people.summary: Fifth-century king of eastern Georgia martyred for Christ.

people.name: Mirian, King of Iberia, and Queen Nana, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: The fourth-century Georgian king and his queen, brought to the faith by the labors of St. Nina, who together led their nation to baptism.

people.name: Mnason of Cyprus
people.summary: An early disciple from Cyprus who in the Acts hosted Paul on his last journey to Jerusalem and at length received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Mnemonios, Bishop of Amathus in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Mocius of Amphipolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Modestus I, Archbishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Late-fourth-century patriarch of Jerusalem, traditionally honored as a protector of domestic animals.

people.name: Modestus II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: Seventh-century patriarch of Jerusalem who rebuilt the holy sites destroyed in the Persian sack of 614.

people.name: Monica, mother of Augustine
people.summary: Fourth-century Christian mother of Augustine whose tears and prayers won the conversion of her son.

people.name: Monk-martyr Bacchus the New
people.summary: An eighth-century Christian of Palestine who having denied the faith in his youth returned in penance at Mar Saba and was crowned at Jerusalem for his second confession.

people.name: Monk-martyr Barsabas, Abbot of Ishtar in Persia, with ten companions
people.summary: A fourth-century abbot of a Persian monastery and ten of his community beheaded together under Sapor II.

people.name: Monk-martyr Isaac of Córdoba
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Monk-martyr John, Abbot of Monagria in Cyprus
people.summary: An eighth-century abbot of a Cypriot monastery drowned for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Monk-martyr Nicholas of Vouneni
people.summary: An early-tenth-century monk of Thessaly slain by Bulgarian raiders on his way to martyrdom.

people.name: Monk-martyr Paphnutius of Egypt and the five hundred and forty-six with him
people.summary: An Egyptian father of the early fourth century who under Diocletian led many hundreds of his fellow Christians together to martyrdom.

people.name: Monk-martyr Paul of Crete
people.summary: An eighth-century monk of Crete slain under Constantine V the dung-named for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Monk-martyr Paul of Cyprus
people.summary: An eighth-century monk of Cyprus burned alive by iconoclasts for refusing to trample on a holy icon.

people.name: Monk-martyrs Conon and his son Conon of Iconium
people.summary: A father and son of third-century Iconium beheaded together under Aurelian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Monk-martyrs David, John, and Menas of Palestine
people.summary: Three monks of seventh-century Palestine slain together by the Saracens in the first decades after the conquest.

people.name: Monk-martyrs Josiah and Joseph of Mount Kharasam in Persia
people.summary: Two fourth-century Persian monks slain together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

people.name: Monk-martyrs Patermuthius and Coprius of Egypt with Alexander the Soldier
people.summary: Two desert ascetics of Egypt and a soldier converted by their witness, all beheaded together under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Monk-martyrs Patermuthius, Coprius, and Alexander of Egypt
people.summary: Two desert ascetics of Egypt and a soldier converted by their witness, all slain together under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Montanus the Presbyter and his wife Maxima of Sirmium
people.summary: A priest and his wife of Sirmium drowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Moses
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Moses and Sarah of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Moses the Black of Scetis
people.summary: A formerly Ethiopian highway robber who in repentance fled to the Scetis desert, struggled long under the guidance of Macarius, and became a priest whose meekness drew his own former companions to Christ.

people.name: Moses the Black of Scetis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Moses the Hungarian of the Kiev Caves
people.summary: An eleventh-century page of Boris and Gleb, taken into Polish captivity, who endured five years of torture rather than break his monastic vows.

people.name: Myron of Cyzicus
people.summary: A presbyter of Achaia under Decius who, finding pagans seizing a Christian congregation on the Nativity day, defended his flock and was tortured to death with peculiar cruelty.

people.name: Myron the Wonderworker, Archbishop of Crete
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Myrrh-bearer Salome
people.summary: Mother of the apostles James and John and one of the women who came at dawn to the empty tomb.

people.name: Myrrhbearer Mary of Clopas
people.summary: The kinswoman of the Mother of God named among the women who stood by the Cross and who came at dawn to the tomb.

people.name: Myrrhbearer Mary, wife of Cleopas
people.summary: The kinswoman of the Mother of God named among the women who stood by the Cross and came at dawn to the tomb.

people.name: Narcissus, Bishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Late-second- and early-third-century bishop of Jerusalem who at one hundred and sixteen reposed in peace after a long life and a withdrawal into the desert.

people.name: Narses and Artemon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Narses and Isaac
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nathaniel of Nitria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Naucratius, Abbot of the Stoudios Monastery
people.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the great Stoudios monastery of Constantinople, successor of Theodore the Studite.

people.name: Naucratius, brother of Basil the Great
people.summary: Fourth-century brother of Basil who from boyhood took to the wilderness near the Iris, where his sister Macrina and his mother Emmelia visited him.

people.name: Naum (Nahum) of Ohrid, Equal-to-the-Apostles
people.summary: Disciple of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century enlightener of the Bulgarians, founder of a great monastery on Lake Ohrid.

people.name: Naum of Ohrid
people.summary: Disciple of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century enlightener of the Bulgarians, founder of a great monastery on Lake Ohrid.

people.name: Nazarius, Gervasius, Protasius, and Celsus
people.summary: Four Italian martyrs of the apostolic age remembered at Milan, whose tomb was revealed to Ambrose in a vision and whose translation drew the city out in procession.

people.name: Neadius the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Neaniscus the Wise of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nearchus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nectarius, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: A senator of Tarsus made archbishop of Constantinople in 381 and confirmed in office by the Second Ecumenical Council over which he then presided.

people.name: Onesiphorus and Porphyrius
people.summary: Two friends of fourth-century Ephesus, tortured and dragged behind horses to their deaths under Diocletian. Also commemorated: Matrona, who disguised as a man entered the monastery of St. Bassian and lived hidden for many years.

people.name: Neon
