people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Neophytus, Bishop of Urbnisi
people.summary: A seventh-century bishop of Urbnisi in Georgia slain for the name of Christ.

people.name: Neophytus, Dometius, Priscus, Martin, and Nicholas of Blachernae
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Neophytus, Gaius, and Gaianus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Neophytus of Nicaea
people.summary: A young Christian of Nicaea slain under Diocletian at the age of sixteen.

people.name: Neophytus, Zeno, Gaius, Mark, Macarius, and Gaianus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nereus and Achilleus of Rome
people.summary: Two soldiers of the imperial guard at Rome under Trajan, baptized and at length beheaded for refusing the imperial sacrifice.

people.name: Nerses and Joseph of Persia
people.summary: A bishop and his disciple beheaded together in fourth-century Persia under Sapor II for the cause of Christ.

people.name: Nesius
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nestor
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nestor and Arcadius, Bishops of Tremithous in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nestor and Tribiminus of Perge
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nestor of Magydos, Bishop of Perge in Pamphylia
people.summary: A third-century bishop of Magydos in Pamphylia who at Perge confessed Christ before the governor Pollio and was crucified.

people.name: Nestor of Thessalonica
people.summary: A young Christian of Thessalonica blessed by Demetrius from prison who went out and slew the imperial gladiator Lyaeus in single combat; for this confession of Christ he was beheaded.

people.name: Nestorianus, Tribimius, Marcellus, and Anthony of Perge
people.summary: Four Christians of Pamphylia slain together for the name of Christ under Decius.

people.name: New Martyr Grand Duchess Elizabeth
people.summary: Founder of the Convent of Martha and Mary in Moscow; cast alive into a mine-shaft at Alapayevsk.

people.name: Nicander and Marcian of Dorostolum
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicander, Bishop of Myra
people.summary: A first-century bishop of Myra in Lycia and disciple of the apostle Titus, who together with the presbyter Hermas was slain for Christ.

people.name: Nicander of Egypt
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Egypt slain under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Nicanor the Deacon, of the Seventy
people.summary: One of the seven deacons appointed by the apostles, slain at Jerusalem with two thousand others on the day Stephen was stoned.

people.name: Nicephoros, Presbyter of the Ephapsios Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicephorus, Abbot of Katabad
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicephorus, Abbot of Medikion
people.summary: An eighth- and ninth-century abbot of the monastery of Medikion in Bithynia, confessor of the holy icons.

people.name: Nicephorus and Stephen
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicephorus II Phocas, Emperor of New Rome
people.summary: Tenth-century Byzantine emperor whose victories pushed back the Arabs in the east and whose monastic friend Athanasius founded the Great Lavra of Athos under his patronage.

people.name: Nicephorus of Antioch
people.summary: A layman of Antioch who, having quarreled grievously with the presbyter Sapricius and afterwards humbled himself even to lying at his feet, took up the martyr's crown that Sapricius had spurned.

people.name: Nicephorus the Confessor, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: A ninth-century hierarch who fought the iconoclasts both with the pen and by personal suffering, deposed by Emperor Leo the Armenian and dying in exile after fourteen years of confession.

people.name: Nicephorus of Sebaze
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicetas, Abbot of Synnada in Phrygia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicetas, Bishop of Remesiana
people.summary: Fifth-century missionary bishop of the lower Danube whose hymns and catechetical works survive; tradition makes him the author of the Te Deum.

people.name: Nicetas, John, and Joseph of Nea Moni on Chios
people.summary: Eleventh-century founders of the New Monastery of Chios, gathered by a vision of the icon of the Theotokos found in the brush.

people.name: Nicetas the Confessor, abbot of Medikion
people.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Medikion in Bithynia, scourged and exiled to a fortified rock under Leo the Armenian for confessing the holy icons.

people.name: Nicetas of Pythiae
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicetas the Confessor, Bishop of Apollonias in Bithynia
people.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Bithynia exiled and persecuted for his confession of the holy icons.

people.name: Nicetas the Confessor, Bishop of Chalcedon
people.summary: An eighth-century hierarch who endured beating, imprisonment, and exile under the iconoclast emperors rather than yield the icons to fire.

people.name: Nicetas the Confessor of Paphlagonia
people.summary: A ninth-century patrician of Paphlagonia who set aside his honors for the cause of the holy icons and reposed in exile.

people.name: Nicetas the Goth
people.summary: A fourth-century convert of the Gothic nation, baptized by Theophilus of the Goths, who was burned alive by his pagan kinsman Athanaric for refusing to abandon Christ.

people.name: Nicholas, Archbishop of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicholas, Bishop of Patara
people.summary: Late-third-century bishop of Patara in Lycia and uncle of Nicholas of Myra.

people.name: Nicholas II Chrysoberges, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Late-tenth-century patriarch of Constantinople, friend of the emperor Nicephorus II Phocas.

people.name: Nicholas Mystikos, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Tenth-century patriarch of Constantinople who in disputes with Leo VI defended ecclesiastical canons against imperial pressure.

people.name: Nicholas of Katopinos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: St. Nicholas the Wonderworker to Bari
people.summary: In 1087, sailors translated the relics of St. Nicholas, archbishop of Myra, from his violated tomb to the city of Bari in Italy, where they remain and continue to issue myrrh.

people.name: Nicholas the Confessor, Abbot of the Stoudios Monastery
people.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the great Stoudios monastery at Constantinople who twice fled his see in the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Nicholas the Monk of Bulgaria
people.summary: An early-ninth-century soldier of Bulgaria who having endured the Bulgar persecution withdrew into the monastic life.

people.name: Nicholas the Sicilian of Mount Neotaka in Euboea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nicodemus, Gamaliel, and Abibus
people.summary: The fifth-century translation from Jerusalem of the relics of Nicodemus, Gamaliel, and his son Abibus, found together with those of the Protomartyr Stephen.

people.name: Nicon, Abbot of the Monastery of St. Gerasimus
people.summary: A sixth-century abbot of the monastery of Gerasimus on the Jordan.

people.name: Nicon of Sicily and his hundred and ninety-nine disciples
people.summary: A second-century convert from a centurion's household in Naples who fled to Sicily, gathered 199 fellow soldiers as monks, and was beheaded with them all under the persecution of Decius.

people.name: Nike
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nikita the Stylite of Pereyaslavl-Zalesski
people.summary: Twelfth-century repentant tax-collector of Russia who lived bound in iron chains on a pillar for the rest of his days.

people.name: Nikolai of Žiča and Ohrid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nikon, Abbot of Radonezh
people.summary: Fifteenth-century disciple and successor of Sergius of Radonezh as abbot of the Holy Trinity Lavra.

people.name: Nikon the Metanoeite
people.summary: Tenth-century preacher of repentance who, going from town to town in the Peloponnese and Crete, called the recently reconquered Greeks back to the faith.

people.name: Nilus the Faster of Sinai
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Nina of Georgia
people.summary: A young woman of Cappadocia who in the early fourth century brought the Gospel to Georgia by way of Armenia, healing the Iberian queen Nana and converting King Mirian. With her cross made of vine wood bound by her own hair, she is the apostle of the Georgians.

people.name: The Nine Martyrs of Cyzicus
people.summary: Nine Christians from various cities of Asia Minor who, gathered at Cyzicus on the Sea of Marmara, were beheaded together for refusing the imperial sacrifice in the fourth century.

people.name: Niphon, Bishop of Constantia in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Niphon, Bishop of Novgorod
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Niser
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Noble Lady and her Servant Martyrs
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nonnus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nonnus, Bishop of Heliopolis
people.summary: A fifth-century bishop whose preaching outside a church in Antioch converted the celebrated dancer Pelagia.

people.name: Nun-martyr Martha, Abbess of Monemvasia, with her three companions
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nun-martyr Martha of Monemvasia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Nun-martyr Paraskevi of Rome
people.summary: A second-century Roman virgin of Greek parents who under Antoninus broke the city's idols and was beheaded for the name of Christ.

people.name: Nun-martyrs Thecla, Mariamne, Martha, Mary, and Enmatha of Persia
people.summary: Five Persian nuns beheaded together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of fire.

people.name: Nuns Marina, Kyra, and Domnica of Beroea
people.summary: Three Syrian women of fifth-century Beroea (Aleppo) who together founded a women's community and lived shut up in cells the rest of their days.

people.name: Nymphas and Eubulus of the Seventy
people.summary: Two of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

people.name: Oecumenius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Trikala
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Olbian (Albianos) the Monk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Olbian, Bishop of Anaea in Asia Minor
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Great Princess Olga of Kiev
people.summary: Princess of Kiev who in her old age traveled to Constantinople and was baptized by the patriarch, taking the name Helen. The first Christian ruler of the Rus', whose grandson Vladimir would complete the conversion of the land she had begun.

people.name: Olympiodora
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Olympios the Prefect
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Olympius the Confessor, Bishop in Thrace
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Onesimus, Archbishop of Ephesus
people.summary: An early-second-century successor of John the Theologian and his companions on the Ephesian throne.

people.name: Onesimus of the Seventy
people.summary: The runaway slave of Philemon at Colossae whom Paul met in his Roman imprisonment and sent back to his master as a brother; later bishop of Ephesus after Timothy, and crowned with martyrdom at Rome.

people.name: Onesimus, the runaway slave of Philemon
people.summary: The slave for whom Paul wrote to Philemon, who became the apostle's son in the faith and at length bishop of Ephesus.

people.name: Onesimus the Wonderworker of Caesarea
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Onisius (Onesimus) of Isauria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Onuphrius the Great
people.summary: The fourth-century desert father of the Egyptian Thebaid who lived sixty years in the wilderness clothed only in his own hair, and whose life was preserved for the Church by Paphnutius. Also commemorated: St. Peter of Athos.

people.name: Or (Horus) of the Thebaid
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Oraiozela of Byzantium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Orentius and his brothers, of Lazica
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Orentius, Pharnacius, Eros, Firmus, Firminus, Cyriacus, and Longinus near Lazica
people.summary: Seven brother-soldiers of Cappadocia exiled to the Caucasus by Maximian, where each in turn met his end.

people.name: Orestes
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Orestes the Physician of Cappadocia
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian physician of Cappadocia bound to a wild horse and dragged to his death.

people.name: Ortisios of Tabennisi
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pachomius and Papyrinus of Greece
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pachomius the Great
people.summary: The fourth-century desert father of Egypt who received from an angel the rule of cenobitic life, gathering the monks of Tabennisi into the first true monastery.

people.name: Paisios the Athonite
people.summary: Twentieth-century elder of Mount Athos.

people.name: Paisios the Great, God-bearer
people.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt whose ascetic gifts were rumored to have made the Lord himself appear in his cell.

people.name: Beginning of the Nativity Fast
people.summary: The forty-day fast in preparation for the Nativity of the Lord begins this day. Also commemorated: three confessors of Edessa, the first two who reposed in chains under Diocletian, and the deacon Habib burned under Licinius.

people.name: Palladius of Galatia, Bishop of Helenopolis
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Helenopolis in Bithynia whose Lausiac History preserves for the Church the lives of the Egyptian and Palestinian fathers.

people.name: Palladius of Thessalonica
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Palladius the Hermit of Antioch
people.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Antiochene wilderness renowned for his miracles.

people.name: Pallamon of Egypt
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt and instructor of Pachomius the Great in the cenobitic life.

people.name: Pambo of Egypt
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Nitria whose sayings stand among the great apothegms of the elders.

people.name: Pamphilus and Capito
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pamphilus of Caesarea and his twelve companions
people.summary: A presbyter of Caesarea in Palestine and pupil of Origen who established the great library there, and was beheaded with eleven others under Maximinus Daia after long imprisonment.

people.name: Pancharius of Nicomedia
people.summary: A noble courtier of fourth-century Nicomedia under Diocletian who at his Christian mother's letter returned to the faith he had renounced and was crowned for it.

people.name: Pancratius, Bishop of Tauromenium in Sicily
people.summary: A disciple of the apostle Peter, sent as the first bishop of Tauromenium in Sicily, who after years of evangelization was stoned by pagans for casting down their idols.

people.name: Pantaenus the Confessor of Alexandria
people.summary: Late-second-century master of the catechetical school of Alexandria and teacher of Clement, who labored as missionary in India.

people.name: and Healer Panteleimon
people.summary: A young physician of Nicomedia, converted by his teacher Hermolaus, who healed all who came to him without payment and was beheaded under Maximian — when his blood touched the ground, the olive tree to which he was bound burst into fruit.

people.name: Papas of Lycaonia
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Lycaonia tortured and nailed to a tree for his confession.

people.name: Papas the New
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paphnutius and his daughter Euphrosyne of Alexandria
people.summary: Fourth-century Alexandrian father and daughter whose paths of monastic asceticism, hidden from each other, were one in heart.

people.name: Paphnutius of Alexandria
people.summary: Father of Euphrosyne the Younger of Alexandria, who at her revelation passed his last years a monk in the cell that had been hers.

people.name: Paphnutius of Jerusalem and the five hundred and forty-six with him
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paphnutius of Kephala
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt, contemporary and acquaintance of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Paphnutius of Thebes
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Thebaid and disciple of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Paphnutius the Confessor of Egypt
people.summary: A confessor of the Egyptian Thebaid imprisoned in the mines under Diocletian; the eyeless witness who at Nicaea stood for the marriage of the clergy.

people.name: Papias, Bishop of Hierapolis
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Hierapolis in Phrygia, a disciple of John the Theologian whose Exposition of the Sayings of the Lord is partially preserved.

people.name: Papias, Diodorus, and Claudianus of Perge
people.summary: Three Christians of Pamphylia slain together for the name of Christ under Decius.

people.name: Pappias, Diodorus, and Claudianus of Attaleia
people.summary: Three believers of third-century Pamphylia beheaded together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Pappos, Bishop of Chytri in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Papylinus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paramonus and three hundred and seventy with him
people.summary: A Christian of Bithynia under Decius who openly rebuked the governor and his companions for forcing 370 Christians to sacrifice; all three hundred and seventy-one were slain together.

people.name: Parasceva of Epibatae
people.summary: Eleventh-century maiden of Thrace who labored long in the desert beyond the Jordan and whose relics now rest at Iași in Romania.

people.name: Paraskeva
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Terence and Neonilla, with their seven children
people.summary: A Christian family of Syria under Decius who refused the sacrifice together; the parents and all seven children were tortured and finally beheaded as one.

people.name: Paraskeva of Iconium
people.summary: A third-century maiden of Iconium named for the day of the Lord's Passion who endured great torture for the name of Christ.

people.name: Pardus the Hermit of Palestine
people.summary: A sixth-century muleteer of Palestine who, holding himself guilty of a child's accidental death, lived out his days in extreme penance in the desert.

people.name: Parthenius and Calogerius of Rome
people.summary: Two brothers of third-century Rome, eunuchs of the imperial household, slain together under Decius.

people.name: Parthenius, Bishop of Lampsacus
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of the Hellespont known for his power over demons, whom he cast out from a young madman at the bidding of Constantine the Great.

people.name: Pasicrates, Valentine, and Julius of Dorostolum
people.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Moesia slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Pasicrates, Valentinian, and Julius of Dorostolum
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Passarion the Presbyter of Palestine
people.summary: A fifth-century priest and abbot in Palestine whose lavra near Jerusalem trained the first generation of Judean monastics.

people.name: Patapius of Thebes
people.summary: A seventh-century Egyptian ascetic who fled the praise of men by going up to Constantinople, where he lived hidden as a healer of the sick at a small cell near the Blachernae church.

people.name: Patriarch Cedronus (Kedron) of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Patriarchs Arsacius and Sisinius of Constantinople
people.summary: Two patriarchs of fifth-century Constantinople who succeeded in turn after the deposition of John Chrysostom.

people.name: Patrick, Bishop of Prusa
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Bithynia, martyred with three of his presbyters after a sermon at the hot springs of Prusa in which he turned the praise of pagan miracles back to the God who made the waters.

people.name: Patrobas, Hermas, Linus, Gaius, and Philologus of the Seventy
people.summary: Five of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

people.name: Paul and his sister Juliana of Ptolemais
people.summary: A brother and sister of third-century Syria who at his witness she converted, and both were martyred together with their three executioners under Aurelian.

people.name: Paul and his sister Juliana of Ptolemais, with Quadratus, Acacius, and Stratonicus
people.summary: A brother and sister of third-century Egypt and three companions converted by their witness, all crowned together under Aurelian.

people.name: Paul and John, Ascetics of Edessa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul and Simon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul and Tatta of Damascus and their five children
people.summary: A household of fourth-century Damascus — parents and five children — all crowned together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Paul, Apostle to the Nations
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul, Bishop of Neocaesarea
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Neocaesarea on the Euphrates whose hands had been disabled by the persecutors and who came so to the First Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Paul, Chionia, and Alevtina of Caesarea
people.summary: A brother and his two sisters of fourth-century Palestine martyred together under Maximinus Daia.

people.name: Paul of Latros
people.summary: Tenth-century anchorite of Mount Latros near Miletus whose pillar drew many disciples to settle around him.

people.name: Paul of Xeropotamou on Mount Athos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul, Pausirius, and Theodotian, brothers of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul the Confessor, Bishop of Plousias
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul the Confessor, Bishop of Plousias in Bithynia
people.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Bithynia exiled for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Paul the Confessor of the Kaiuma Monastery
people.summary: An eighth-century monastic confessor of Constantinople who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under Constantine V.

people.name: Paul the New Ascetic of Mount Latros
people.summary: A tenth-century anchorite of the holy mountain near Miletus whose discipline drew many to him.

people.name: Paul the Obedient of Cyprus
people.summary: A Cypriot monk whose perfect obedience became proverbial in the monastic tradition.

people.name: Paul the Physician of Corinth
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul the Simple
people.summary: A simple Egyptian farmer of the fourth century who at sixty came to Anthony the Great for the monastic life, whose obedience surpassed his master's instruction.

people.name: Paul the Standard-bearer of Corinth
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul the Syrian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Paul, Valentina, and Theia of Diocaesarea
people.summary: Three companions of fourth-century Palestine slain together under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Paula of Rome
people.summary: Late-fourth-century Roman noblewoman, friend and patroness of Jerome, who at her widowhood took her daughters into the East and founded a community at Bethlehem.

people.name: Pausicacus, Bishop of Synnada
people.summary: A late-sixth-century bishop of Phrygia known for his miracles of healing and the wisdom of his pastoral counsel.

people.name: Pausilipus of Heraclea in Thrace
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pelagia of Tarsus
people.summary: A young woman of fourth-century Tarsus who refused the emperor's son in marriage to keep her vow to Christ, and was placed in a heated bronze bull where she gave up her soul in song.

people.name: Pelagia the Penitent
people.summary: A celebrated dancer of fifth-century Antioch, converted by the sermon of Bishop Nonnus at the doors of a church; she put on man's clothing and lived hidden on the Mount of Olives until her sex was discovered at her repose.

people.name: Pelagia, Theodosia, and Dula of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: Three Christian maidens beheaded together under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

people.name: Peregrinus, Lucian, Pompeius, Hesychius, Pappias, Saturninus, and Germanus of Dyrrachium
people.summary: Seven Christians of second-century Macedonia who at the witness of Astius openly confessed Christ together.

people.name: Pergetos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Persa
people.summary: A holy woman commemorated together with Thecla in the Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter and Paul, Bishops of Nicaea
people.summary: Two ninth-century bishops of Nicaea who together steered the church of that city in the years following the Seventh Council.

people.name: Peter, Andrew, Paul, Dionysius, and Christina of Euridinos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter Apselamus of Eleutheropolis
people.summary: A young Christian of Palestine under Maximinus Daia, of a noble family of Eleutheropolis, who at twenty was burned for his confession.

people.name: Peter, Bishop of Sebaste in Armenia
people.summary: The youngest brother of Basil the Great, raised by his sister Macrina, who served as bishop of Sebaste at the end of the fourth century.

people.name: Peter I and Samuel I, Catholicoi of Georgia
people.summary: Two sixth-century catholicoi of the Georgian church who consolidated its independence from the see of Antioch.

people.name: Peter I, Archbishop of Alexandria
people.summary: Early-fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, last martyr of the great persecution under Maximinus Daia, beheaded outside his city in 311.

people.name: Peter I of Bulgaria
people.summary: Tenth-century king of Bulgaria, son of Symeon the Great, who at the end of his reign embraced the monastic life.

people.name: Peter II, Patriarch of Alexandria
people.summary: Fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, successor of Athanasius the Great, who in his short tenure suffered exile under the Arian Valens.

people.name: Peter of Anium
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian martyred at Hieropolis under Maximinus Daia.

people.name: Peter of Atroa
people.summary: A monastic father of ninth-century Bithynia who suffered under the second iconoclasm.

people.name: Peter of Avlona in Palestine
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Palestine slain at Avlona near Samaria under Maximinus Daia.

people.name: Peter of Blachernae
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter of Capitolia
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Bostra in Arabia, slain by the Saracens for his witness.

people.name: Peter of Constantinople, the former Tax-Collector
people.summary: A hard-hearted publican of sixth-century Africa who, struck by a vision of his deeds weighed against a single loaf, gave away all his goods and lived hidden as a slave.

people.name: Peter of Damascus
people.summary: Eighth-century bishop and writer of Damascus whose tongue was cut out by the Saracens for the cause of orthodoxy.

people.name: Peter of Egypt
people.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Abba Lot in the Egyptian desert.

people.name: Peter of Galatia
people.summary: A fifth-century hermit of Antioch born in Galatia, whose ninety-nine years of asceticism Theodoret of Cyrus remembered.

people.name: Peter of Monovatia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter of Mount Athos
people.summary: Ninth-century desert ascetic of Athos, the holy mountain's first known hermit.

people.name: Peter of Sinope
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter, Patriarch of Jerusalem
people.summary: A sixth-century patriarch of Jerusalem who took part in the deliberations against the Origenists.

people.name: Peter, Severius, and Leucius of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter the Ascetic and Acepsimas of Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter the Cappadocian, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
people.summary: A tenth-century abbot of the monastery near Triglia in Bithynia who succeeded its founder.

people.name: Peter the New, Bishop of Crete
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Peter the Patrician of Constantinople
people.summary: A ninth-century imperial official who left his honors to become a monk at Constantinople.

people.name: Peter the Publican of Constantinople
people.summary: A sixth-century tax-collector at Constantinople struck by a vision of his deeds weighed against one loaf, who gave all to the poor and lived hidden as a slave.

people.name: Peter the Silent of Galatia and Antioch
people.summary: A fifth-century hesychast born in Galatia who at twenty took to a tomb-cell at Antioch, living ninety-eight years on bread and water.

people.name: Peter the Wonderworker, Bishop of Argos
people.summary: Tenth-century bishop of Argos in the Peloponnese famed for the gift of healing.

people.name: Petronia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Petronius of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century disciple of Pachomius the Great in the cenobia of upper Egypt.

people.name: Petronius of Ephesus
people.summary: A second-century disciple of John the Theologian who reposed at Ephesus where his teacher had labored.

people.name: Phanourios the Newly-Revealed of Rhodes
people.summary: A late-medieval saint of Rhodes whose icon was discovered after centuries; the body of the martyr is depicted at the moment of his confession.

people.name: Pharmuthius the Recluse of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philadelphus and Polycarp the Monastics
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philaret the Merciful of Amnia
people.summary: Eighth-century landowner of Paphlagonia whose continual almsgiving impoverished his house, but whose granddaughter Maria was chosen bride of the emperor Constantine VI.

people.name: Phileas, Bishop of Thmuis
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Thmuis in Egypt slain together with the magistrate Philoromus under Diocletian.

people.name: Philemon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philemon and Archippus, and Apphia, sister of Philemon
people.summary: The Christians of Colossae to whom Paul addressed his shortest letter, asking that the runaway slave Onesimus be received as a brother. All three were stoned to death together at Colossae.

people.name: Philemon and Domninus of Thessalonica
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philemon and Martyr Apphia of Colossae
people.summary: The recipient of Paul's shortest epistle and his wife, both stoned to death at Colossae together with Archippus.

people.name: Philemon and Martyr Fortunatus
people.summary: A bishop and his companion crowned together for the faith.

people.name: Philemon, Bishop of Gaza
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Phileortus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philetas the Senator with his wife Lydia and their sons
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philetas the Senator, with his wife Lydia, their sons, and companions
people.summary: A senator of second-century Illyria and his household, all crowned together under Hadrian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Philip
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philip, Bishop of Gortyna in Crete
people.summary: A second-century bishop of Gortyna whose treatise against the Marcionites is mentioned by Eusebius.

people.name: Philip, Bishop of Heraclea, with Severus, Memnon, and the thirty-seven soldiers
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Thrace and his companions slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

people.name: Philip I, Metropolitan of Moscow
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philip, Protus, Hyacinth, Basilla, and Claudia, with St. Eugenia
people.summary: Companions of Eugenia of Rome — her father the prefect, two slaves who served her in the monastery, and others of her household — slain together for the faith.

people.name: Philip the Confessor, Metropolitan of Moscow
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philippikos the Presbyter and his brother Martyr Barsimos
people.summary: A priest of Syria and his brother slain together in Persia for the name of Christ.

people.name: Philogonius the Confessor, Bishop of Antioch
people.summary: Early-fourth-century lawyer of Antioch who as bishop in the years before Nicaea defended the Church against the rising Arian party.

people.name: Philon, Bishop of Karpasia in Cyprus
people.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Karpasia renowned as a wonderworker.

people.name: Philoneides, Bishop of Kourion in Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philonidis of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philoromus of Galatia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philoromus the Magistrate of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philosophos of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philosophus of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philoterus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philoterus of Nicomedia and the six soldiers with him
people.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Nicomedia slain together with the soldiers who guarded him.

people.name: Philotheos the Presbyter, Wonderworker of Asia
people.summary: A tenth-century priest of Asia Minor known for his many miracles and his peace under unjust suffering.

people.name: Philotheus of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Philoumenos, Valerian, and Phaedrus of Ancyra
people.summary: Three Christians of third-century Ancyra each given his own torment — fire and nails for one, the sword for another, hot resin for the third.

people.name: Phocas
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Phocas and Hermylus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Phocas of Sinope
people.summary: A bishop of Sinope in the second century who, hearing that soldiers had come to seize him, dug his own grave the night before, set them a meal in his house, and at dawn revealed himself to be the man they sought.

people.name: Phocas the Gardener of Sinope
people.summary: A pious gardener of fourth-century Sinope who fed his executioners at his table the night before going freely to his beheading.

people.name: Phosterius the Hermit
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Phota, Photis, Parasceva, and Cyriaca, sisters of Photini
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Photina the Samaritan Woman
people.summary: The Samaritan woman at the well of Sychar to whom the Lord revealed Himself, who afterwards under Nero shared in the crown of her sons and sisters.

people.name: Photine of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Photini the Samaritan Woman
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Photius the Great, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Photius, the great ninth-century patriarch of vast learning who defended Orthodoxy against the Filioque and confirmed the mission to the Slavs. Also commemorated: Bucolus, the disciple of John the Theologian and second bishop of Smyrna.

people.name: Photios the Great, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Photius and Anicetus of Nicomedia
people.summary: An uncle and his nephew at Nicomedia under Diocletian who, declaring Christ before the emperor's tribunal, were cast into a furnace and crowned together.

people.name: Photius, Founder of Akapniou Monastery in Thessaloniki
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Photius the Monk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Phygellus, Bishop of Ephesus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Piama the Virgin
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pierius of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pinuphrius of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt who twice fled the abbacy laid upon him for the sake of obscurity.

people.name: Pinytus, Bishop of Knossos
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Knossos in Crete, an early church writer commended by Eusebius for his sound counsel.

people.name: Pionius, Presbyter of Smyrna
people.summary: A third-century priest of Smyrna who, before he was burned in the same place where Polycarp had been crowned, taught the gathered crowd from his pyre.

people.name: Pior of Scetis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pitirim of Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Piulius (Publius)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Placilla, Empress of New Rome
people.summary: Wife of Theodosius the Great, remembered by Gregory of Nyssa for her almsgiving and personal service to the sick.

people.name: Plato of Ancyra
people.summary: A young man of Ancyra of noble birth in the fourth century who under Galerius endured nineteen tortures and was finally beheaded for the confession of Christ.

people.name: Plato the Confessor, Abbot of the Stoudios
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Platon the Confessor, Abbot of the Sakkudion
people.summary: Eighth- and ninth-century elder of the Stoudios monastery, uncle of Theodore the Studite, exiled and beaten for the cause of the holy icons.

people.name: Platonides and her companions of Ascalon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Platonis (Platonida) of Nisibis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Plotinus the Monk
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ploutodoros
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Plutarch, Archbishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Plutarch, Serenus, Heraclides, Heron, Rhaïs, Potamiaena, and Marcella of Alexandria
people.summary: A company of catechumens and the maiden Potamiaena of Origen's school at Alexandria, slain together under Septimius Severus.

people.name: Poemen of Palestine
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Poemen the Great
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose sayings, gathered in the Apophthegmata, distill the wisdom of the cell: 'A man teaches his neighbor by example, not by speech.'

people.name: Pollion the Reader of Cibalis
people.summary: An early-fourth-century reader of the church at Cibalis in Pannonia burned alive under Diocletian.

people.name: Polybius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Rinokyr in Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Polycarp of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Polycarp, Bishop of Smyrna
people.summary: The disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Smyrna for sixty-five years, who under Marcus Aurelius answered the proconsul's demand to revile Christ: 'Eighty and six years have I served Him, and He has done me no wrong; how then can I blaspheme my King who saved me?'

people.name: Polychronia, mother of George the Trophy-bearer
people.summary: A pious Christian mother of Cappadocia who raised her son George in the faith before his martyrdom at Lydda.

people.name: Polychronius of Gamphanitus and his companions
people.summary: A fourth-century priest and the ten companions martyred with him for Christ.

people.name: Polychronius the Bishop and the priests Parmenius, Helimenas, Chrysotelus, with deacons Luke and Mocius, and laymen Maximus and Olympius
people.summary: A great company of clergy and laity of Babylon Fortress in Egypt slain together under Decius.

people.name: Polyeuctus of Caesarea and Timothy the Deacon
people.summary: Two early-fourth-century martyrs of the great persecution, Polyeuctus given over to the fire at Caesarea in Cappadocia and Timothy slain in Mauretania.

people.name: Polyeuctus of Melitene in Armenia
people.summary: A Roman officer of third-century Melitene whose conversion came suddenly through the witness of his Christian friend Nearchus; he was beheaded for tearing up the imperial edict.

people.name: Polyeuctus, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Tenth-century patriarch of Constantinople in the reigns of Constantine VII and John Tzimiskes.

people.name: Polyeuctus, Victorinus, and Donatus of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Polynikos
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Ponticus and Alcibiades of Lyon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pontius of Cimella in Gaul
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pope Marcellus of Rome with his deacons and soldier-martyrs
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pope Stephen I of Rome and companions
people.summary: The third-century bishop of Rome martyred in the persecution of Valerian, slain seated in his own chair at the celebration of the Liturgy.

people.name: Porphyrios, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Late-fourth-century patriarch of Antioch and friend of John Chrysostom in the years before the latter's elevation to Constantinople.

people.name: Porphyrius and Baptus, companions of Charalampias
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Porphyrius, Bishop of Gaza
people.summary: A fifth-century bishop who through the long-prayed labor of years uprooted the entrenched paganism of Gaza, bringing down the great temple of Marnas by imperial decree after a famine breaks at his intercession.

people.name: Porphyrius the Mime
people.summary: An actor at Caesarea who in mocking Christian baptism on stage was himself touched by grace, confessed Christ, and was beheaded under Julian the Apostate.

people.name: Potamon, Bishop of Heraclea in Egypt
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Egypt beaten and exiled by the Arians who at length received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Pothinus, Bishop of Lyon
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Lyon, an Anatolian by birth, beaten to death at ninety years old in the persecution of 177.

people.name: Potitus of Gargara
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Praulius, Archbishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Prima, Ampelius, Dativus, Plotinus, Saturninus, Fabius, Felix, and companions of Carthage
people.summary: A company of believers of fourth-century Carthage in north Africa slain together under Diocletian.

people.name: Priscilla, wife of Aquila
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Priscus, Malchus, and Alexander of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Caesarea who came of their own will to the magistrate and were given to the beasts for the name of Christ.

people.name: Probus, Tarachus, and Andronicus
people.summary: Three Christians of Cilicia under Diocletian who endured eleven distinct torments before the prefect Numerian and at last were thrown to the beasts at Anazarbus.

people.name: Processus and Martinian of Rome
people.summary: Two prison-keepers of first-century Rome converted at the witness of Peter and Paul under Nero and crowned with them.

people.name: Apostles and Deacons Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, and Parmenas
people.summary: Four of the seven first deacons named in Acts, ordained by the apostles to serve the daily ministration. Tradition names Prochorus the companion of John the Theologian in his exile at Patmos.

people.name: Prochorus of the Pchinja
people.summary: Tenth-century desert father of the Bulgarian-Macedonian wilderness on the river Pchinja.

people.name: Proclus and Hilary
people.summary: Uncle and nephew of Callipolis in Asia Minor who under Trajan refused the imperial sacrifices and were beheaded together for the confession of Christ.

people.name: Proclus, Archbishop of Constantinople
people.summary: A fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, disciple of John Chrysostom, who in 438 brought home his master's relics in solemn procession.

people.name: Procopius of Caesarea in Palestine
people.summary: An officer of the Roman army who, while marching against the Christians, was met with the vision of a cross of light and converted; under Diocletian he endured prolonged torture and was beheaded.

people.name: Procopius the Confessor of Decapolis
people.summary: An eighth-century monk of Asia Minor and defender of the holy icons under Leo the Isaurian, beaten in prison and exiled with his disciple Basil until the death of the emperor restored him.

people.name: Procopius the Reader of Caesarea
people.summary: A reader of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine beheaded under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

people.name: Promus (Probus) and Hilarion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Prophet Ahijah the Shilonite
people.summary: The prophet of Shiloh of the days of Solomon who tore his garment in twelve pieces before Jeroboam, prefiguring the division of the kingdom.

people.name: Amos
people.summary: The eighth-century shepherd of Tekoa whom God called from his flocks to prophesy against the prosperous indifference of Israel and to announce the day when justice would roll down like waters.

people.name: Prophet Azariah
people.summary: A tenth-century BC prophet whose name signifies 'whom the Lord has helped.'

people.name: Prophet Baruch
people.summary: Disciple and scribe of the prophet Jeremiah, who recorded the prophet's words and was led with him into exile in Egypt.

people.name: Prophet Daniel and the Three Holy Youths Ananias, Azarias, and Misael
people.summary: The four young captives of Babylon: Daniel who in the lions' den was kept by God, and the three who in the fiery furnace sang the hymn that the Church still chants in the eighth ode of every canon.

people.name: The Synaxis of the Most Holy Theotokos
people.summary: The day after the Nativity is dedicated to the Mother of the One who was born. The Church gathers in her honor, acknowledging the woman through whom the great mystery of godliness was made visible.

people.name: and Glorious Prophet Elijah the Tishbite
people.summary: The fiery prophet of the ninth century BC who at Carmel called down fire from heaven against the priests of Baal and was taken up alive in a chariot of fire — the herald who is to return before the great Day of the Lord.

people.name: Elisha
people.summary: The disciple of Elijah who received a double portion of his spirit; he raised the Shunammite's son, cleansed Naaman the Syrian, and from his very grave brought a dead man back to life.

people.name: Symeon the Fool-for-Christ and John
people.summary: Two sixth-century friends who began as pilgrims at the Jordan and separated for a life of solitude; Symeon returned to Emesa playing the holy fool to hide his virtues, John remained an unknown ascetic.

people.name: Prophet Habakkuk
people.summary: The prophet who watched on his tower for the answer of God to his question 'How long?' and received the word that became the keynote of Pauline theology: 'The righteous shall live by his faith.'

people.name: Prophet Haggai
people.summary: The post-exilic prophet who in 520 BC stirred up Zerubbabel and Joshua the high priest to rebuild the temple, promising that the latter house would surpass the former in glory through the coming of the Messiah.

people.name: Prophet Hanani (Ananias)
people.summary: A ninth-century BC prophet who rebuked King Asa of Judah and was imprisoned for his words.

people.name: Prophet Hosea
people.summary: The eighth-century BC prophet of the northern kingdom whose own marriage to Gomer became the parable of God's love for unfaithful Israel — a love that does not let go.

people.name: Prophet Isaiah
people.summary: The eighth-century BC prophet of Judah whose vision of the Lord seated on the throne and whose songs of the Suffering Servant the Church reads in every Holy Week.

people.name: Jeremiah
people.summary: The weeping prophet of Jerusalem, who foretold the captivity of his people and the new covenant written on the heart. Stoned by the exiles in Egypt for his witness.

people.name: Prophet Joad of Bethel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Prophet Joel
people.summary: The ninth-century BC prophet of Judah who foretold the locust as a sign of divine visitation and the pouring out of the Spirit upon all flesh in the last days, fulfilled at Pentecost.

people.name: Prophet Jonah
people.summary: The eighth-century prophet sent to Nineveh who in three days in the belly of the great fish prefigured the Lord's burial and rising.

people.name: Prophet Malachi
people.summary: Malachi the last of the prophets of the old covenant, who foretold the messenger to come before the great and terrible day. Also commemorated: Gordius the centurion of Caesarea who under Licinius descended from his desert cave into the city's arena to confess Christ.

people.name: Forefeast of the Dormition
people.summary: The eve of the Dormition. Also commemorated: Micah of Morasthi in the eighth century BC, who foretold the birthplace of the Messiah at Bethlehem and said what the Lord requires of man.

people.name: Babylas of Antioch
people.summary: The third-century bishop of Antioch who barred Emperor Philip from entering the church until he repented. Also commemorated: Moses, who led Israel from Egypt and received the Law on Sinai face to face with God.

people.name: Prophet Moses the God-Seer
people.summary: Leader of Israel out of Egypt, who received the Law on Sinai face to face with God.

people.name: Prophet Nahum
people.summary: The seventh-century BC prophet of Elkosh whose three short chapters announce the fall of Nineveh — Assyria's pride brought down before the slow patience of God.

people.name: Prophet Obadiah
people.summary: The ninth-century BC prophet whose short book pronounces the judgment of God upon Edom and the day when the kingdom shall be the Lord's.

people.name: Prophet Samuel
people.summary: The last of the judges and the first of the prophets in the regular line, dedicated by his mother Hannah from the womb, who anointed both Saul and David and judged Israel through long faithful years.

people.name: Prophet Shemaiah
people.summary: A tenth-century BC prophet of Judah who warned Rehoboam against war with the northern tribes after the kingdom's division.

people.name: Prophet Zechariah
people.summary: Post-exilic prophet of Judah whose visions of the priest crowned with the diadem of kingship pointed forward to the Messiah.

people.name: Prophet Zephaniah
people.summary: A prophet of Judah under King Josiah who proclaimed the Day of the Lord and called the daughter of Zion to rejoice, for the Lord her God was in her midst.

people.name: Prophetess Anna (Hannah), mother of the Prophet Samuel
people.summary: The barren wife of Elkanah who at Shiloh promised her unborn son to the Lord and at his weaning brought him up to the tabernacle — sung over each year on this commemoration of barrenness undone.

people.name: Proterius, Patriarch of Alexandria, with six companions
people.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Alexandria after the deposition of Dioscorus, slain by the monophysite mob in the baptistery of his cathedral.

people.name: Protomartyr Rajden (Razhden) of Georgia
people.summary: Fifth-century Persian noble baptized in Georgia and slain at Tsromi for refusing to return to the worship of fire — the protomartyr of the Georgian church.

people.name: the Protomartyr and Archdeacon Stephen
people.summary: The body of the first martyr, hidden after his stoning in Jerusalem, was recovered in the fifth century through the vision of the priest Lucian and translated to a church on Mount Zion.

people.name: Psoes of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Publia the Deaconess of Antioch
people.summary: A fourth-century deaconess of Antioch who under Julian the Apostate sang the psalms aloud against the emperor as he passed and was struck for it without flinching.

people.name: Publius and Lollion the Younger
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Publius, Bishop of Malta and Athens
people.summary: Second-century bishop of Athens, the chief man of Malta in the Acts who received the shipwrecked Paul.

people.name: Publius of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Publius the Ascetic and his disciple Theodotos of the Euphrates
people.summary: A fourth-century Syrian noble who at his father's death gave away all his goods and founded a monastery, where Theodotos succeeded him.

people.name: Publius the Egyptian
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Pyrrhus of Breti
people.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia, who labored as an ascetic at Breti in Georgia.

people.name: Pyrrus the Bishop
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Quadratus (Codratus) of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Quadratus and his companions at Corinth
people.summary: Five Christians of Corinth under Decius — Quadratus chief among them — born in a forest where their mothers had hidden during the persecution, and beheaded together when they came of age to confess.

people.name: Queen-martyr Shushanik of Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Quinctian, Bishop of Seleucia
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Seleucia who took part in the First Ecumenical Council and afterwards withdrew to live as a hermit.

people.name: Quinctilian and Capatolinus of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Quintus of Phrygia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Rabulas of Samosata
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Reginus and Orestes of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Reginus, Bishop of Skopelos
people.summary: Fourth-century bishop of the Aegean island of Skopelos, slain by Arian heretics under Constantius for his Nicene faith.

people.name: Repose of St. John Chrysostom
people.summary: His memorial is transferred from the day of his death (407) to November 13, because today belongs entirely to the Cross.

people.name: Repose of St. Sergius of Radonezh, Abbot of Holy Trinity Lavra
people.summary: Fourteenth-century father of Russian monasticism and builder of the Holy Trinity Lavra north of Moscow.

people.name: Repose of St. Silouan the Athonite
people.summary: Russian monk of the Athonite Russikon (1866–1938) whose hidden grief for the world and prayer for all sons of Adam gave the modern Church a fresh witness to ceaseless prayer.

people.name: Righteous Aaron the High Priest, brother of Moses
people.summary: The first high priest of Israel, brother of Moses, who interceded for the people in the wilderness.

people.name: Righteous Abel, the first martyr
people.summary: The second son of Adam and Eve, slain by his brother Cain — by the Church reckoned the first witness of the blood of Christ.

people.name: Righteous Abimelech of Gerar
people.summary: The Philistine king of Genesis who at God's word restored Sarah to Abraham and called the patriarch a prince of God.

people.name: Righteous Ahaz
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Anna the Prophetess
people.summary: The daughter of Phanuel of the tribe of Asher who at the Meeting of the Lord recognized the Christ-child in the Temple.

people.name: Righteous Child-martyr Artemius of Verkola
people.summary: Twelfth-year-old farmer's son of northern Russia struck dead by lightning in the fields in 1545, whose body remained incorrupt and worked many wonders.

people.name: Righteous Eleazar and Phineas
people.summary: Aaron's son and grandson, of the priestly line that succeeded him at the altar of the Lord.

people.name: Righteous Elizabeth, mother of John the Forerunner
people.summary: The kinswoman of the Theotokos whose long barrenness God remembered, who took her child into the wilderness to save him from Herod's sword.

people.name: Righteous Forefather Abraham and his nephew Lot
people.summary: The father of all believers who left his homeland at God's word, and his nephew whose righteous soul was vexed in the city of Sodom.

people.name: Righteous Gideon, Judge of Israel
people.summary: The judge who delivered Israel from Midian with three hundred men and whose fleece bedewed with the morning dew is read in the Church as a figure of the Virgin.

people.name: Righteous Gorgonia, sister of Gregory the Theologian
people.summary: A fourth-century laywoman of Cappadocia whose holy household and patient witness Gregory the Theologian sang in his great funeral oration over her.

people.name: Righteous Hannah (Anna), mother of the Prophet Samuel
people.summary: The barren wife of Elkanah whose vow at the tabernacle of Shiloh gave Israel the prophet Samuel.

people.name: Righteous Joseph of Arimathea
people.summary: The secret disciple of the Lord and member of the council who at the crucifixion asked Pilate for the body of Jesus and laid it in his own new tomb.

people.name: Righteous Joseph the Betrothed
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Joseph the Fair, son of Jacob
people.summary: The patriarch son of Jacob whose chastity in Egypt and providence over Pharaoh's grain saved his people — the Old Testament icon of patience under wrong.

people.name: Righteous Joshua the son of Nun
people.summary: Successor of Moses who led Israel across the Jordan into the promised land.

people.name: Righteous King Hezekiah of Judah
people.summary: The Old Testament king of Judah whose prayer at the threat of Sennacherib turned aside the Assyrian host.

people.name: Righteous Lazarus, the Friend of Christ
people.summary: The friend of the Lord whom Jesus raised from the dead at Bethany, and who afterwards reposed bishop of Kition in Cyprus.

people.name: Righteous Martin the Merciful
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Melchizedek, King of Salem
people.summary: The priest-king of Salem to whom Abraham brought tithes after his victory over the four kings — the type of the eternal priesthood of Christ.

people.name: Righteous Monk Manuel
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Nonna of Nazianzus
people.summary: Fourth-century wife of Gregory the Elder and mother of Gregory the Theologian, whose prayers won her husband to Orthodoxy.

people.name: Righteous Olympias the Deaconess of Constantinople
people.summary: Late-fourth- and early-fifth-century deaconess and noblewoman, friend and supporter of John Chrysostom in his exile.

people.name: Righteous Phineas, grandson of Aaron
people.summary: The zealous priest of Israel whose covenant of peace is set forth in Numbers.

people.name: Righteous Sarah, wife of Abraham
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Tabitha of Joppa
people.summary: The widow of Joppa whom the apostle Peter raised from the dead, as the book of Acts recounts.

people.name: Righteous Tarasius the Wonderworker of Lycaonia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Righteous Virgin-martyr Glaphyra of Nicomedia
people.summary: Fourth-century handmaid of the empress Constantia who, fleeing Licinius's pursuit, escaped to St. Basil at Amasea and reposed in his protection.

people.name: Romanos and Teletios of Nicomedia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Romanos, Bishop in Macedonia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Romanos the Deacon of Parium
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: The Protection of Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
people.summary: St. Andrew the Fool-for-Christ and his disciple Epiphanius beheld the Mother of God at Blachernae spreading her veil over the people in prayer — the Pokrov, the maternal sheltering remembered by all Slavic Orthodoxy.

people.name: Romanus of Raqqa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Romanus the Deacon of Caesarea
people.summary: A fourth-century deacon of Caesarea in Palestine whose tongue was cut out and who continued to confess Christ until they strangled him in prison.

people.name: Romanus the Soldier of Rome
people.summary: A Roman soldier converted by the witness of Lawrence in his prison and beheaded with him.

people.name: Romanus the Wonderworker of Cilicia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Romulus and the eleven thousand soldiers with him
people.summary: A Roman officer in early-second-century Armenia who confessed Christ with eleven thousand of his men under Trajan.

people.name: Romulus, Donatus, Silvanus, and Venustus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Rostislav, Prince of Great Moravia
people.summary: Ninth-century Slavic prince who invited the Byzantine mission of Cyril and Methodius into his realm.

people.name: Royal Martyrs of Russia
people.summary: Tsar Nicholas II, Tsarina Alexandra, and their five children, slain at Yekaterinburg in 1918.

people.name: Rufinus and Saturninus, with Maximus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Rufinus the Deacon with Aquilina and the two hundred soldiers of Sinope
people.summary: A deacon, a noblewoman, and two hundred soldiers of third-century Sinope crowned together under Decius.

people.name: Rufus and Rufianus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Rufus of Thessaloniki
people.summary: Bishop of Thessaloniki in the early fifth century, a defender of orthodoxy in the wake of the Third Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Rufus the Recluse of the Kiev Caves
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas, Bishop of Dafnousia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas Stratelates
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas the Goth
people.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Wallachia drowned in the river Buzău for refusing to share in the pagan rites of his Gothic kinsmen.

people.name: Sabbas the Goth of Wallachia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas the Monk of Benephali
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabbas the New of Sicily
people.summary: Tenth-century abbot of a monastery in Sicily and southern Italy who at last reposed at Rome after long years of monastic labor.

people.name: Sabbas the Sanctified
people.summary: The sixth-century father of Palestinian monasticism, founder of the Great Lavra in the Judean wilderness — the cradle of the Typikon that still shapes the daily services of the Orthodox Church.

people.name: Sabbas the Stratelates and seventy soldiers with him
people.summary: A Gothic officer in the Roman army at Rome under Aurelian who together with seventy of his fellow soldiers was beheaded in 272 for confessing Christ.

people.name: Sabiana, Abbess of Samtskhe
people.summary: An eleventh-century abbess in Georgia who led a great community of women in the Samtskhe region.

people.name: Sabinus, Archbishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sabinus of Egypt
people.summary: A nobleman of fourth-century Hermopolis under Diocletian who from a hidden cell in the marshes was betrayed by a beggar to whom he had given alms, and was drowned in the Nile for confessing Christ.

people.name: Sabinus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Catania
people.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Catania in Sicily who, longing for solitude, withdrew to a hermitage where he received many gifts of healing.

people.name: Sadoc, Bishop in Persia, and the one hundred and twenty-eight with him
people.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop and a great company of his flock slain together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

people.name: Sadoc of Persia and the one hundred and twenty with him
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop in the Persian church and his clergy and people, all beheaded together under Sapor II.

people.name: saints Zenon and Zoilus, ascetics
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Salamanes the Silent of the Euphrates
people.summary: Fifth-century hesychast of the Euphrates region whose perfect silence even with his own family is remembered.

people.name: Salome of Ujarma
people.summary: An Iberian princess and helper of Nina of Georgia, whose home was a refuge for the apostle of the Georgians.

people.name: Salonas the Roman
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Salumptinus, Bishop of Jerusalem
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sampson the Hospitable
people.summary: A wealthy physician of Rome who in the sixth century gave away all his goods to open a hospital at Constantinople, healing the bodies of the poor and gently leading their souls to Christ.

people.name: Samuel the Presbyter of Edessa
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sarah of Scetis, Abbess
people.summary: A fourth-century desert mother of Egypt whose sayings stand among the apothegms.

people.name: Sarapion of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sarbel and Barbe of Edessa
people.summary: A second-century brother and sister of Edessa who shared one confession and one martyrdom.

people.name: Sarbellus
people.summary: An early witness of Edessa stoned for confessing Christ.

people.name: Sarbelus and Bebaia of Edessa
people.summary: A brother and sister of second-century Edessa who shared one confession and one martyrdom.

people.name: Sarmata of Egypt
people.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt, disciple of Anthony the Great.

people.name: Sarmean, Catholicos of Kartli in Georgia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Saturninus, Jakischolus, Faustianus, Januarius, Marsalius, Euphrasius, Mammius, the Seven Former Thieves
people.summary: Seven robbers of first-century Corfu converted in prison by Jason and Sosipater and beheaded for the name of Christ.

people.name: Saturninus, Thyrsus, and Victor of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sava, First Archbishop of Serbia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Schinon (Skinus)
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sebastian the Duke and Anatolius the Officer
people.summary: A duke and an officer of the Roman court who joined Photini in her confession and her crown.

people.name: Sebastiana of Heraclea
people.summary: A first-century disciple of the apostle Paul beheaded at Heraclea in Thrace under Domitian.

people.name: Sebastiani
people.summary: A first-century disciple of the apostle Paul from Marcianopolis who at length received the crown of martyrdom.

people.name: Secundus, Enlightener of Spain
people.summary: An apostolic-age missionary to Spain whose evangelism by tradition began the Iberian church.

people.name: Secundus the Deacon of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Seleucius, sawn in two
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Seleucus of Galatia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sennouphios of the Latomos Monastery in Thessalonica
people.summary: A ninth-century standard-bearer of the imperial guard guided in a vision to take the monastic habit at the Latomos monastery.

people.name: Sennuphius the Standard-bearer of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Seraphim of Sarov
people.summary: Uncovering of his relics in 1903.

people.name: Serapion
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Serapion, Bishop of Thmuis
people.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Thmuis in Egypt, companion of Anthony the Great and friend of Athanasius the Great, whose Sacramentary preserves the earliest extant Egyptian eucharistic prayers.

people.name: Serapion of Zarzma in Georgia
people.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the monastery of Zarzma in Georgia and a builder of churches in his land.

people.name: Serapion the New of Alexandria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Serapion the Sindonite
people.summary: Fifth-century Egyptian father remembered for traveling clothed in nothing but a linen sheet, giving even his cloak to the poor.

people.name: Serapion the Sindonite of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sergius and Bacchus
people.summary: Two officers of high rank in the army of Maximian, exposed as Christians and stripped of their honors; Bacchus was beaten to death, Sergius beheaded after being made to run in shoes lined with nails.

people.name: Sergius (George) of Constantinople
people.summary: Ninth-century imperial confessor of the icons under Theophilus the Iconoclast.

people.name: Sergius II, Patriarch of Constantinople
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sergius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Euphrosyne of Alexandria
people.summary: Euphrosyne of fifth-century Alexandria, who disguised as a young monk lived thirty-eight years in a male monastery beside the father who searched for her. Also commemorated: the great fourteenth-century abbot of Russia, builder of the Holy Trinity Lavra.

people.name: Sergius, Stephen, and Castor
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sergius the Magistrate, Founder of Nikitiatus Monastery
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Seridus, Abbot of Gaza
people.summary: Sixth-century abbot of the monastery near Gaza where Barsanuphius and John the Prophet lived enclosed.

people.name: The Seven Holy Youths of Ephesus
people.summary: Seven Christian soldiers of Ephesus under Decius who, fleeing persecution, slept hidden in a cave for nearly two centuries and woke in the reign of Theodosius the Younger to confirm the Resurrection of the body to a doubting age.

people.name: Severian, Bishop of Scythopolis
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Severian of Sebaste
people.summary: A senator of fourth-century Sebaste who visited the Forty Martyrs in prison and was stretched on the rack and stoned for his confession.

people.name: Severus and Artemidorus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Severus, Bishop of Catania
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Severus the Presbyter of Interocrea in Italy
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Shenoute the Archimandrite
people.summary: Fifth-century Coptic abbot of the White Monastery, called the father of the Third Ecumenical Council.

people.name: Shio of Mgvime
people.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia, who founded the cave-monastery of Shio.

people.name: Sibelius (Sobel) of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Silas, Silvanus, Crescens, Epenetus, and Andronicus
people.summary: Five of the Seventy named in Paul's letters: Silas the prophet, Silvanus the bishop of Thessalonica, Crescens of Galatia, Epenetus 'the firstfruits of Achaia,' and Andronicus 'of note among the apostles.'

people.name: Silas, Bishop of Corinth in Persia
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Protomartyr and Equal-to-the-Apostles Thecla
people.summary: A noblewoman of Iconium converted by the apostle Paul whose witness she followed through fire, beasts, and exile; she labored as an evangelist and reposed in old age in a cave at Seleucia.

people.name: Silvanus, Bishop of Gaza, with forty companions
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Gaza beheaded under Maximinus Daia together with a great company of his clergy and people.

people.name: Silvanus, Disciple of Pachomius the Great
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Silvanus, Luke, and Mocius of Emesa
people.summary: A bishop, a deacon, and a reader of fourth-century Emesa slain together for the name of Christ.

people.name: Silvanus of Gaza and the forty companions with him
people.summary: A priest of fourth-century Gaza beheaded with forty of his fellows after he had been worked in the porphyry mines until he could see no longer.

people.name: Silvanus the Ascetic of Gaza
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Simeon I, Patriarch of Antioch
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Simeon Stylites the Younger of Cilicia
people.summary: A sixth-century stylite of Mount Admirable near Antioch who from boyhood lived on a pillar and reposed there at the age of seventy-five.

people.name: Simeon the God-Receiver
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Simon the Wonderworker of Calabria
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Simon the Zealot
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sisinius, Bishop of Cyzicus
people.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Cyzicus who endured many torments under Diocletian and afterwards reposed in peace.

people.name: Sisinnios, Bishop of Ephesus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sisoes the Great
people.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose dying brethren saw him conversing in radiance with the saints and the Lord, asking only that he be given a little more time for repentance.

people.name: Six Holy Martyrs
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Six Holy Martyrs at Melitene
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sixtus II, Pope of Rome
people.summary: Third-century Athenian bishop of Rome beheaded by Valerian in the catacomb of Praetextatus while celebrating the Liturgy.

people.name: slain at the monasteries of Sinai and Raithou
people.summary: Fourth- and fifth-century monks killed by Bedouins while at prayer in their cells.

people.name: Snandulia of Persia
people.summary: A noblewoman of fourth-century Persia who gathered the bodies of the holy martyrs by night for honorable burial.

people.name: Socrates the Presbyter and Theodote the Deaconess of Ancyra
people.summary: A priest and a deaconess of third-century Ancyra in Galatia who suffered persecution together.

people.name: Soldier-martyrs Basilides, Cyrinus, Nabor, and Nazarius of Rome
people.summary: Four Christian soldiers of Rome under Diocletian, slain together at the Aurelian Way.

people.name: Solochon, Pamphamer, and Pamphalon of Chalcedon
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Solomon, Archbishop of Ephesus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophia and her Daughters Faith, Hope, and Love
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophia and Irene of Egypt
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophia and her three daughters Faith, Hope, and Love
people.summary: A widow of Rome under Hadrian who, having raised her three young daughters in Christ, watched them tortured one by one; the children went bravely to their crowns, and three days later their mother gave up her soul at their grave.

people.name: Sophia, Mother of Orphans, of Ainos
people.summary: A holy widow of Thrace who in the tenth and eleventh centuries gathered orphaned children into her household for the love of Christ.

people.name: Sophia the Healer
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophia the Wonderworker
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophronios, Bishop
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.

people.name: Sophronius, Abbot of the Monastery of St. Theodosius
people.summary: Sixth-century abbot of the great monastery of Theodosius the Cenobiarch in the Judean desert.

people.name: Sophronius, Archbishop of Cyprus
people.summary: Commemorated in the Orthodox Synaxarion.
